This bill deliberately slows how representation keeps up with population. Right now, the Maori population and enrollment growth justifies an additional Māori seat. An eighth seat is due. That's based on the current data, not projections. But instead of allowing that adjustment, the Electoral District Boundaries Amendment Bill changes the timing rules delaying

[00:05:22] that process further. This is not neutral, this creates a representation lag by design. Boundary reviews are pushed out to every second parliamentary term. New counting rules exclude recent data and the system locks in that delay. The result is simple. Māori representation falls behind where it should be. For Pere Rākau, this has a real impact. We represent thousands of whānau across our marae. Our population is growing, our enrollment is growing. If seats do not increase, where the numbers justify, then our voting powers in effect is reduced. This is also inconsistent with the Crown's own actions. In 2025, the

[00:06:09] Crown apologised to Ngāhapū o Ngāti Rāngenui, including Pere Rākau, that acknowledged our rangatiratanga and committed to a relationship based on partnership and equity. And here we are one year later, the Electoral District Boundaries Amendment Bill proposes a system that delays Māori representation. Those two positions can't coexist. Under Ticititi, this matters. Active protection means protecting Māori participation. Equity means our representation must keep pace with our population. This bill does neither. It also raises a basic democratic issue. If the population grows but

[00:06:54] representation does not adjust in time, then some votes are worth less than others. That affects Māori most of all, but affects actually the integrity of the whole system. And this isn't something that can just be fixed later. If the next election runs without an additional Māori seat that is already justified, that loss of representation is real and permanent for that cycle. You can't rewind that. So our position is clear. Withdraw the Electoral District Boundaries Amendment Bill, 2026, recognise that an 8th Māori seat is already warranted, ensuring representation reflects the current population not delayed data. So in closing, this is not just an

Hey, thank you for that. Can I just ask, do you have a clear view why the Māori Electoral Boundaries is not going to be reviewed before the 2029 election? Whether it's in 2029 or whether it goes with the current system of every six years or every five years, the amendment and the adjustment can be made and adapted, this bill can adapt it for it to happen now. And so slowing that from every six, every five years to every

Yeah, Tangi Kauraua nursery. If you're available, that would be great. Are they on screen? All right, cool. Welcome, if you're there.

[00:21:56] How are you going? Sorry, just realised I had my camera and my mute button on. No problem at all. Hey, now, we've got 10 minutes with you, looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. My name is Jessamyn Oanoa, and thank you for inviting me to participate.

And as you were saying, I'm from Tangikaroro Native Nursery. My wanting to participate is because I am concerned that many of the whānau where I'm from in Te Araroa, which is on the east coast, most northern point of the North, sorry, eastern point of the North Island. Very isolated rural Māori community. I'm currently in Auckland right now just because I'm here for a hui. But normally you would find me in a little town called Te Araroa.

That's my concern regarding the bill as it stands. My concern is that specific sectors of the community would be left out.

I'm just concerned that there are many young rangatahi who have now graduated from local schools, secondary schools. My daughter's one of them. She's now going to AUT. Rangatahi who will be their first time voting in this upcoming elections. Whose data is significant.

[00:25:16] It's going to be important. It's going to show the huge increase in the number of young Māori that are going to be participating in the elections and as voters. What I've seen in the amendment is that our whānau who are incarcerated will not have the benefit of being able to vote if it carries the way that it's written. And I just wanted to point out that at least 70% of women in prison at the moment are young Māori female. And that is a huge concern for our people. Same with our men over 50% of our male

[00:26:06] prison population are Māori. So that also is a concern to me. I want to see a more fair way for our people forward where they are able to participate in the decisions that are going to impact hugely on their lives. I want to see resources that can help with the rehabilitation of our people. But what I also wanted to point out is that, sorry, I just referred to my notes, iwi Māori are not advisers or priority communities. We are your partners. I agree with that statement that Kere Kōwhai mentioned earlier

[00:26:53] and also when she mentioned about many Māori are not even visible in government service data and that there are far reaching consequences for iwi in the Crown. I'll give you an example of that in terms of what I do as a co-owner operator with my dad for our native plant nursery. [00:27:43] that they hope to then take their knowledge on to other erosion prone land up and down the state Highway 35. I mean, I love the idea of us repopulating our Te Tairawhiti region with native Te Punarākau. My concern is that how does that sit with mana when those seeds are actually taonga to us? They come from our Te Punarākau, our taonga trees. They represent the mana of our whenua that we are kautiaki of. If we are not in the data, then the mana of what we represent is also not in the data.

[00:28:34] So that's why I'm saying that I totally agree with our Iwi Chairs Forum asking for three solutions, a Māori descent population file and Iwi Māori survey program and Māori descent census. And I'll give you another example of why that matters. In November last year, I took my daughter because she was very ill with eczema. And while we were waiting to be helped for six hours in that time, we were seen once at the beginning when we first arrived and then much later on at 5.30 in the morning, we arrived at something like 11 at night, the night before. My daughter wasn't seen because I couldn't prove

[00:29:23] that she was a New Zealand citizen born in New Zealand of Māori descent. She was born in that hospital, Middlemore Hospital. Her father was born at Middlemore Hospital. I am mana whenua, as well as tanata whenua. And still, my daughter couldn't get the help that she needed. Sorry about this. That was the time we were meant to start. But what I'm saying to you is if data was consistent across departments that represented our ethnicity, our... Sorry, I just need to get back to what I had written.

[00:30:10] If there had already been a Māori descent population file, that wouldn't have happened to my daughter. She was bleeding and we were visible where we were to everybody. There was no space for her to go into a private room so that she could at least not be so visible with all the eczema all over her body. It was a really traumatic experience for us as a whānau. Not just the eczema, but to be asked to justify being Māori, to justify being a New Zealand citizen and to have to justify that we had the right to be treated

[00:30:56] in our own hospital where she was born. So that's why this sort of data is important. It needs to be captured. It needs to be shared across different departments in the same way so that our children aren't victimised and traumatised this way. It shouldn't be happening to any New Zealand citizen, not just Māori but any New Zealand citizen, but particularly because we have whakapapa to Ngātaki Tāmaki, we have whakapapa to Ngāti Porou, Te Whānau Apanui. So it was very damaging for us. Thanks, Jasmine.

[00:31:44] Sorry to hear you about your daughter. I hope she's better now. Yes, she is. Good. Now, I've got a question from Rima Nakle. Thank you, Mr Chair. Jasmine Rima Nakle, Member of Parliament for Takāneini. More on proposition again. I'm really sad to hear what you went through and if you'd like my office to follow up and give you, try and understand why you guys went through that, I'm happy to take that on board, whether you're in my electorate or not, because I think you deserve answers if something was untoward. But I guess one of the aims for what we're doing is to try and collect data in a more streamlined way. But yeah, please feel free to contact me, Rima Nakle,

[00:32:30] and I'm happy to try and understand more for you and your whānau how that happened. Thank you, Rima. All right. I don't think we've got any questions for you, but you're a similar theme, so it's not that we don't understand the issue. We've had a number of people raise the issue, so thank you for your contribution this afternoon. Are you back home? Back home up north? I will be going back home next weekend. All right, no doubt. Can't wait to get back to the coast. Oh, kāwhai, kāwhai, looking forward to it. Bye-bye. Right, now, are we going back to Fiona? No, she's... Right, it sounds like we've done pretty about Fiona.

[00:33:18] Now, we're going to Sam, I'm going to have to ask you, and yes, all of you, we're going into a process where we can talk about...

[00:00:00] Thank you. Now, I'll just start with giving an example of why it's important to be counted. So I'm older, my parents are dead, I'm not going to be kicked out of my home if I disclose information about my identity. That's not the situation for everybody. I've come here today wearing a T-shirt saying challenge biphobia. Even wearing that in some countries in the world would be enough to get me put in jail. But that's not Aotearoa, New Zealand. However, it is important that we recognise why people, particularly those who may come from different places in

[00:00:46] the world, may be hesitant about sharing information about themselves through administrative data, which they are not confident about being kept safe, secure. They don't know whether it'll be confidential. They may be a younger person living in a conservative family who is uncomfortable about their parents seeing them for who they are. This is so for the whole of the rainbow communities, LGBTIQ plus and all the MVP, FAF, Pasifika terms, these are issues of social stigma. And the 2023 census, as you will know, was the first time that people were able

[00:01:33] to self-identify and disclose confidentially information about themselves in a way that meant that they could be counted and that that information could then feed into planning for services for a whole range of areas where it's important to be counted, like 173,000 people on the LGBT plus communities in Aotearoa in 2023. And statistics NZ admits that's an underestimate because there are still concerns about that confidentiality and trust. But if we compare that to administrative data first and you asked agencies to give account, you would probably get maybe 10% of that. As a bisexual person, I have never been asked my sexual

[00:02:22] orientation by any government agency that has recorded my information before that 2023 census. So that is invisible information about me. The same with people from different iwi and hapu who may never have been, when they might identify as Maori, but it may not go any further. They may not identify, be asked that. So administrative data has got massive gaps and that is a risk. So as you will have seen from my, I echo a lot of previous submissions about even if we were living in a perfect society where people felt confident sharing information about themselves, we're not there yet. And yet that information is really important.

[00:03:11] And there's lots of things I could say but I'll, oh and just that when I say it's really important, I used to help run hackathons using sort of data that's been collected from sources and I know how important it is to business. But I'll stop there. Any questions? Right, thank you Kay. I think that's very good and thank you all as we're busy, you're also busy and that you're quite lengthy submissions. So thank you for that. And I'll just show this to the point of the camera that I don't know if it can be seen. But I'm wearing badges so that when I'm traveling people can know that I'm safe to engage with and I'm being counted. But as I say, lots of people don't have that luxury. Okay, thank you very much. Right. Now Elizabeth Walker, I think

[00:04:00] it's that one. Hello. Hello. Hey Elizabeth, we can hear you. Right. We've got five minutes with you. Thank you. My name is Elizabeth Walker. Good afternoon. I'm here today to ask the committee to commission an independent report of expert review of the admin data first model and consequence cessation of the periodic census of populations and dwellings before it's too late. Just put my camera on. You heard my intro? Yes. Okay. I have over 40 years experience working in the data modeling management and governance of data. I worked for New Zealand statistics department, Data Bank New Zealand, Westpac New Zealand and then had 20 years with

[00:04:50] Teradata data warehouse where for 10 years I governed all Azure Pacific client data projects across industries and government. I've encountered a lot of data designs and data problems, especially data gaps. When clients had manual or perhaps no source systems in place for crucial data items, data warehouse projects were inevitably delayed or halted and the clients ensuing business plans and projects were also put on hold. I'm humbled by the range and depth of expert testimony here today. Please consider the impact of data gaps and low quality data on New Zealand government's decision making and change management capabilities, as so many people described. I believe that data and data quality are the lifeblood of every

[00:05:42] organization and every government in our modern digital world and without the census data you've heard that we stand to lose the kete or basket or spine perhaps that connects and contains all our people information identifying individuals, dwelling location, ages, relationships to their households and in most cases that's their family. We'll lose granular data about individuals and families coming directly from people. That's you and me so it's more accurate and probably the most highly trusted data in government and beyond. It's more than a century of continuous high quality long longitudinal data and data that directly affects electoral boundaries foundation

[00:06:35] of our democracy. Apart from the census no government department council locates people people, prisons in New Zealand as you've heard and I don't think I can be represented in the same way as a taxpayer, a beneficiary, a patient, a landholder. Government departments hold only slim slices of data as we would say. New Zealand's population statistics are key pieces of our national infrastructure. I view the census data as playing a pivotal role in New Zealand's national population statistics. I was pleased to see Koi2 come up before me. I was going to talk about how much I supported their proposals and how integral I saw the

[00:07:24] census as part of the population strategy that they were talking about. I can say more but to conclude along with the repercussions experts here describe it's the lack of process and rigor that I've heard this morning and reaching this point that is perhaps most disturbing. Please reconsider our loss of population and family data and commission an independent expert review of the decisions and options regarding the census. For this abandoned, thank you very much for listening. Are there any questions? Yeah thank you Elizabeth. Now any questions from anyone? This is good. Straightforward. No thank you Elizabeth that's very good and we've got a number of submissions on the same

Hi I'm Steve Farrow and I thank the Surgeon Select Committee for Angus Administrator or providing me with the opportunity to make this oral submission. I assume you have read my written

One thing the committee needs to know is that I take this opportunity seriously that is why the clerk

My written submission canvassed the high level concerns I had. I think one document told me that entrenched clauses of the act would

Government health data does not capture and is often withheld from the government by minority but important segments of the population. You put in some information about some international examples where there have been reviews. What in your view would a New Zealand equivalent review look like? I think the key element that's got a really broad range of stakeholders. I think this process has highlighted the range of interested parties and the uses of data that even we weren't

[01:40:58] aware of. The range of submitters has definitely highlighted that. It needs technical expertise in there as well and it needs I think a cross-party approach. This is data that will update all of us and so this is something that has to sit out for 20 or 30 years. The value of data is not just now it's the trends that it creates in the long term. Thank you. Right. Thank you very much for your submission. Right. Sam, I see you're sitting there. We've got two submissions and we know who you are. And so nice to see you again. So do you. And we've often seen shortly as I said. But anyway we've got your submission for yours. Five minutes I think. Martin. Oh you're very careful because you, yes.

[01:41:44] Right. Yes. So it's in ballot productions as concerned with the census bill. Now we haven't put a position whether it's for or against. We're wanting to make sure that it's robust and have safeguards in place so that there are steps in place for data corrective measures. And our biggest concern is the Maori electoral population considering I'm in my 30s right now. My Maori friends are having bigger families than my non-Mori friends. So that's something that I've noticed that's been evident. That's also come up between the two last census that the Maori population has gone up by 3% or the population that I've identified has gone up. The other concern which was this was discussed after we submitted it but is the 2028 planned census. Our biggest concern is the fact that we are borderline on

[01:42:35] eight Maori electorates in the country. And we want to make sure with the new proposed boundaries that go on for the next election, every Maori electorate MP has 5,000 more constituents than their North Island counterpart. And that itself is already quite on a dicey line. And that could be entrenched for two elections. And we're concerned that that is going to put a lot of stress on those MPs who already themselves are covering quite some geographically large areas, including, was it Te Tai Tonga being the biggest one? We, was it now on a more personal note, I'm one who's only ever done an electronic census in my life as an adult, including the census place lost by 2013 electronic census. However, they managed to get my landline number

[01:43:26] that they only would have got from that census to ring me to say it was missing. So I think that is something that can be a bit of a laugh considering in the previous census, I was in a different part, I was in the O4 telephone corridor, not the O6. So that's something on there. You have a landline that's shocking. At that point. At that point. Just checking. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Yeah, at that point. So that that that's something that's, yeah, that was quite intriguing that happening. And also on data matching, this was something I was warned when I was on the unemployed benefit, be careful, get paid fortnightly, because working income only has 48 weeks in the year, because they can't comprehend five,

[01:44:12] was it three fortnights happening in one month. And so that's a data collection going wrong. And often anyone who's been on the benefit working incomes, right, the beneficiaries are wrong, even when the commissioner of the IRD backs the beneficiary over working income over the wrongly established debt. So that is something with administrative data mistakes can happen. And it depends who's important data, who collects the data. And so we want to make sure that we keep some record or a failsafe that goes back so we can at least get our population records accurate or adjusted, especially when administrative anomalies come up, so that we at least have some sort of way of correcting the data. So at least we still have accurate things to do planning. We, for it's in ballot uses it, not for general elections,

[01:45:01] but local body elections, we look at we have to look at data to find out where's the right place to run our shows. Fakatane District Council is quite an interesting one, because Murupara doesn't think it belongs anywhere with itself in there. And, but trying to work out for that particular council to embody a general ward, where was the biggest population centre and where was the one that would bring in the best in studio audience. So that's something we like to do. And yeah, I'm glad we're one organisation that has put that place on the map. Yeah. Right, do I take it you've finished? Oh, for this submission, yes. We're poised for your next one too. Right, members, any questions?

[01:45:46] Yes, reference to Harry Styles. Yeah, Stacey did that one, not me, from Mr Carl Vates. Thanks, Mr Chair. Just in regards to the census in 2028 and the impact on the 2029 election, my understanding is that the 2028 census will not happen in time, even under the old model, to ensure boundary changes are in place to the 2029 election. So I'm just, there's been a few submitters that have raised that today. And I just wanted to see if that's your understanding or what your understanding is of the timing from the census to then do the boundaries review in order for them to be ready for that

[01:46:32] 2029 election. My understanding is that that timeframe, it's not possible anyway. The reason why we didn't have a boundary redraw for the 2023 election was because the census happened in the same year and the legislation states that it has to wait one, if the two collide, it waits one year. But I think we may have had in the past where there have been a couple of elections very close to censuses, such as the census 2001 was very close to 2002. And 2002 also had a snap election. And we managed to cope with new boundaries at that particular point with the 2001 census. So I'm not sure whether there is the exact delay.

[01:47:17] It depends when the planning part starts happening. That's the big part in there because the planning changes that for 2023 census that elected the electoral boundary changes didn't happen until last year. Whereas I would assume they would start in 2028 if the census happened or right at the beginning of 2029 to ensure those new boundaries were in place ready for the 2029 general election. Right. Is that right, Carl? Yeah, I have a little bit there, chair. All right. All right. Thanks, Sam. Thank you. Right. Now, last one before we're going to have a little bit of a break. And that is Anna Muir who's coming in by Zoom. Anna, are you with us? Just struggling with the camera. That's right, Anna. We can hear you loud and clear.

[01:48:10] Okay. Okay. Now I've got a button to use. Okay. So... Oh, there you are. Yes. So it's good to join you again, less than a week after the English Language Bill hearing. I think there's some overlapping themes between the English Language Bill and the Census Bill today. This bill obviously relates to very complex topics with lengthy historical origins. So some of the different words that we're using make it difficult to communicate the concepts. But so I guess along that line, I thought it would

[01:48:55] be helpful for me to start by referring to... Well, by using the words about small area information. So those are words that I've heard in the hearing today. They are on page 21 of Len Cook's written submission. I think it's 2.10b. He speaks about continuing the existing rich array of small area information. There was an earlier discussion suggesting that Port Waikato is an example of small area. Yeah, very appropriate examples from both of you. Yeah. So I'd like to highlight the needs of central government and local authorities,

[01:49:44] the areas that have unit titled developments. As the recent purchaser of a unit titled Townhouse in Auckland, I have quite deep insights about the problems in the central government as well as Auckland Council data processes for those scenarios. That causes problems not only for property transactions, but for planning purposes, with Auckland being in the middle of the plan change 120. Processes. There are some very unhelpful narratives that are based on, I guess, poor data. So I have identified some problems in central government

[01:50:38] data about unit titled properties and developments. I have recommended in the second part of my written submission that the Unit Titles Act of 2010 be amended. So there is a mandatory reporting obligation. So central government data is shared more regularly, more transparently. So all relevant decision makers in public and private contexts have access to that information. Locally, there's at least one story in the spin-off where people are saying, well, all of the townhouses are being built in West Auckland since we had the 2016 Auckland Unitary

[01:51:23] Plan. Those narratives are based on building consent data. Whereas I live in a 1985 townhouse that was built in a development established under the Unit Titles Act of 1972, before digitised processes came about. And so the age of my property has resulted in some data related problems. A bit like Jonathan Godfrey's story earlier about how the records of his blindness predate digitised health records. So just coming back to the base themes of population dwellings

[01:52:09] in digital government, depending on the age of a building on a property, there are, I guess, gaps to be resolved if we can get better collaboration between Statistics New Zealand and LINZ and others as Len Cook has recommended. The first part of my submission summarises, I guess, the range of interested parties and it identifies some groups that are often overlooked. So I think it would be worthwhile to specifically refer to those groups if the bill proceeds and that particular section is amended. If we were really going to refine the statute book, we could also sharpen up the New Zealand Business Number Act of 2016. Just before I

[01:53:00] allow some time for questions, I did want to note that I will make a supplementary submission because I have an email from Auckland Council to explain why they did not make a written submission. I've just got a problem with the screen here. So I'll send this through in writing, but the message I got from Auckland Council on the 23rd of April was, it was considered that an Auckland Council submission would not affect the outcome of the proposed changes, which was primarily related to the timing of the census and the role of the government statistician to deliver an annual census. There was no perceived contrast with Auckland Council's strategic framework and it was deemed low priority. As a rate payer

[01:53:52] in Auckland, in New Zealand's largest city, I find that a pretty disappointing response from an unelected representative of Auckland Council. I recommend that your committee requests advice or information from Auckland Council, with Morris Williamson being the former Minister of Statistics, that should help to foster some sort of input. But yeah, we're dealing with really important topics. So I also support the other recommendations to involve experts with a proper review. Right, thanks Hannah. We have heard from local government New Zealand, you've probably heard that. But Auckland Council is different and they're not a member of local government New Zealand. The rate payers of Auckland need to

[01:54:39] be reviewed by Auckland Council. No, no, no, let me add, this is a procedural point actually partly I wanted to mention. So it was a relatively lengthy public consultation period from the 5th of March until the 15th of April. They had a lot of time. It is disappointing that the submissions were only published on the Parliament website on Friday. I have hand delivered a copy of Len Cook submission to my friend, the Hon Simon Watts, Minister for Auckland and Minister of Local Government. And I pointed him to recommendation three on the cover of Len Cook submission. It says, I recommend that the Justice Committee seek

[01:55:27] advice from those who are affected by the bill in local government. Hey, thank you, Anna. Thanks for that recommendation. And your specific drafting suggestions, very helpful. Got a lot going for you, you're at Tugger University, Lord agree, very good. And you're in the Epsom electorate, so gosh, Anna. Yeah, and I forgot to mention that Len Cook delivered the graduation address at the University of Otago on the 6th of May, 2000. So that set the scene. Unbelievable. Okay, thank you, nice to see you again. Okay, thank you. Bye-bye. Right, members, we're going to have a break. Near this we can come back in three for a little bit, right? Oh, the coffee.

meetings.title: 2026/05/05 [Part 1]
meetings.description: Tuesday, 5 May 2026 | Justice Committee Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Te Mana Raraunga Lara Greaves Larissa Renfrew
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Right, who's our first submission? I am. And person, let's be in person. Yes, we know the mother, the step-brother, the whānau, the Treaty of Waitangi. Not here. Okay, let's go to the Zoom. Do we know if the Zoom's ready?

[00:00:48] Are they online? So, I'll just... And in a suspicious style. They're near, they might come back. Good morning, Marina. We've got 10 minutes with you, thinking for being available a little bit early, but looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. So, we've got 10 minutes, as I say. So, please leave some time for some questions if you can. Thank you. Thank you. Sorry, we just got the thing that our internet connection is unstable. So, let us know. Classic. Oh, right, okay.

[00:01:34] We're select committee, so, yeah. Thank you. We'll take it away. Tamanararanga, the Māori data sovereignty network brings to you in the more than 800 Māori researchers, practitioners and entrepreneurs who are passionate about data and Māori rights. As a group focused predominantly on advocacy, Tamanararanga is different to Takahiraranga Charitable Trust, who are party to the Mana Orita Agreement. However, we support the submission on this bill, made by the Gata Iwi Leaders Group. Tamanararanga also engages with the Global Indigenous Data Alliance supporting the realization of global indigenous data rights through the care principles and supporting UNDRIP and other international and national trees and conventions. Today, representing Tamanararanga at three members of Te Pākapū,

[00:02:19] the Steering Group, Dr Laro Grieves, an associate professor in politics at Victoria University of Wellington, with expertise in Māori politics, survey development, quantitative methods in Māori and LGBTQ plus demography, Dr Logan Hamley, a senior researcher at the Koa Research Services, a Ngāti Haueti-led research centre, and Larissa Renfrew, a PhD student at the University of Auckland who has conducted research related to Māori data sovereignty and ethics, Māori identity, youth wellbeing, and demography. Collectively, our group has also used the RDI in research and also been conducted, consulted by Stats and Zed, about the approach to LGBTQ post-topics in the census. Tamanararanga's position on this bill is shaped by our understanding of Māori data as both a strategic asset and a living talent to Māori. This includes both data produced by

[00:03:08] the census or the new proposed approach. Our position is also formed by the principles of Māori data sovereignty as foundational to Tamanararanga's advocacy work. Tamanararanga welcomes the opportunity to submit on this bill and we want to begin by acknowledging that there are genuine positives in the proposed model. More frequent data releases will be valuable, the flexibility to tailor surveys for different sub-populations is promising, and we commend the focus on meeting the needs of communities, including Iwi Māori, but we have serious concerns and we believe this legislation is premature. The move to administrative census removes the enumerated census before we know the replacement works. Experts across the board have raised grave concerns about data quality and Statistics New Zealand's own independent review has indicated that high quality statistics from administrative data

[00:03:56] will not be achievable by 2028. For Māori, the stakes are particularly high. Administrative data is deficit framed, it is collected at the most vulnerable moments in people's lives and does not tell the full story of our communities. Critical variables like Māori descent, Iwi affiliation and Te Reo Māori fluency have no clear plan still and Māori descent is not just a data question, it underpins our electoral system and our constitutional arrangements. There is also a deeper issue of data sovereignty and consent. This bill expands and links administrative data without free, prior and informed consent and without adequate Māori governance and without upholding the Māori agreement. Aotearoa has the opportunity to build a world-leading trustworthy data system

[00:04:42] and this bill as it stands moves us in the wrong direction. As for our recommendations, we kind of considered okay so there's been this process, it's been like quite a long consultation process, a methodological process within Stats NZ and we can definitely see some flaws in that process and really want to know like you know what is the best model going forward, how can we make this work, considering costs, considering the various issues we've discussed today and that various other speakers will discuss today. We think that an independent review of the methods, of the governance, of the decisions made so far is really our recommendation and making sure that there are reviewers with expertise in Māori data sovereignty, Māori data governance. We see like a real missed opportunity here to create like our own model, our own data ecosystem,

[00:05:31] not just kind of copying a Nordic model but making something like unique to Aotearoa New Zealand. That brings in Te Kāhū Rodona's Māori data governance model which has some great features you know for everyone but would also uphold sensitivity and do great in terms of building a trustworthy data system or robust data system, one that just works better for everyone and ultimately saves a lot of money. We think it's great that the bill moves towards greater engagement than just every five years with Māori but the way that administrative data works is the decisions are made within agencies and made within Stats NZ that need greater review and greater engagement with Māori because we know like four populations, some will make a decision that just seems

[00:06:17] like a little technical tweak here and there but it can have a lot of effect on someone's day-to-day life for you know how they interact with the state and the system. So ultimately we really think that this is like a missed opportunity to create a great data system that governs you know our data as New Zealanders and as Māori as as Tāunga like upholds Māori data sovereignty governs data as Tāunga but also as a national strategic asset. So ultimately you know we side with a lot of the experts that are saying there are great methodological issues but also point to the suite of governance issues as well with this move away from the Enumerated Census. Kia ora, thank you for listening. Good thank you very much. Now I've got a couple of questions so it's free actually. Camilla Billich first. Kia ora, thank you very much for your

[00:07:07] oral submission and your written submission. I note the strong points you've made around Māori data sovereignty, TTC and also the member of you but I just wanted to ask in terms of the if this bill was to go ahead as it's currently outlined acknowledging those strong points of your submission what if any issues do you see around the actual administrative data and how that would lock in terms of its its falsiveness. Do you have any concerns around the that that type of result that might come out of the use of administrative data? Absolutely. I think it was kind of really well laid out by the independent review that was done by StatZ that indicated that

[00:07:56] we just won't have that high quality administrative data by 2028 so if this does roll out there are going to be major gaps and those gaps are going to cause a lot of harmful Māori. Māori descent you know that's something that our electoral system is based on you know is it's one of the lowest quality indicators of the lowest rate of coverage and for us as analysts like as scientists like the first thing you do with a new method is you make sure it is super robust and you know there's always going to be no sayings but you want it to be pretty good that this you know protects around the edges but people think it's robust before you bring something counts in and for Māori descent in particular that's not the case. All right I've got a question from Mr Todd Mine's kind of a supplementary I think to Camilla's because I was looking at

[00:08:42] paragraph 36 of your submission where you talk about some of you have obviously used the IDI and you were disappointed with the quality and breadth of the data you've got a couple of examples of what you'd mean by that. So basically when you go to the IDI you think okay you're going to have millions and millions of cases of potential you know normally I do surveys so you're like wow then equals four million excellent the problem you then have is that these data were collected like when people are say like getting arrested or they're collected when someone's in hospital so there's all sorts of very strange kind of patterns in the data like missing data where you wouldn't expect it places where like people's ethnicity changes like over just over data sets at any given snapshot in time so there's a lot of

[00:09:30] issues and I'd say at the moment you know my big time reading is war green and war green registered by statistics and I would recommend it to the select committee it's an excellent book that stats NZ have said they based this approach on the Nordic model they patched all of that and made sure that there weren't issues across data sets before they implemented an administrative census so that's what we could say is that you need to make sure that you don't have these gaps or this weird inconsistency in the data and that you have a very clear transparent or a bust approach to where there are inconsistencies especially for things like the Maori descent indicator. Thanks. All right hey thank you very much for your submission that's been very helpful and very comprehensive for your submission so thank you for this morning.

[00:10:17] Right now we're going into the next one community housing out here Good morning. Nice to see you. Are you on your own this morning? I believe Paul will be joining me but I will have him kick off. I'm the deputy CEO with community housing and thank you for the opportunity to address you this morning. We won't speak to the points in our submission. We share concerns about the changes that are taking place

[00:11:03] and specifically I'd like to give a couple of examples from current work that we have underway. Our work relies on accurate data to understand what's going on with households and housing in Aotearoa New Zealand and currently doing a bit of work for a community trust in the South Island and the ability to look at say rental housing stress changes in rates of home ownership of overcrowding rely on data out of the census currently and with the change in approach what we understand from engagement with the stats New Zealand is that for communities under 5,000 in population they're just going to become invisible. You really won't be able to get down into the details like we currently are

[00:11:49] in being able to separate households into income bands, into renters and homeowners and then understanding their levels of housing stress and other outcomes. And we find it somewhat interesting at the time that we're moving toward and progressing with the social investment approach that we support and relying on the IDI to be able to understand outcomes from interventions and with these changes we have serious concerns about the ability to understand especially in smaller communities and as was noted by the prior respondents say by ethnicity and with different Iwi affiliation and so we think that really putting in place

[00:12:36] the fundamentals of the administrative data approach needs to be done before we move away from the enumerated census because of that inability to really look down into communities like districts in Waimati or McKenzie that have a few thousand households there. Now going forward we've made a number of recommendations on how we think we can mitigate some of the worst impacts but we really see the opportunity to do a pause and to put in place the safeguards that are needed to ensure that we have the ability to have accurate data on which to make informed decisions which we believe will be lost. I'm currently also participating on a stats New Zealand user group around housing domains

[00:13:26] in trying to understand how we can identify different data sets to replace what's going to be lost. And some things are fairly fundamental and in in that work we know that say renters about reliance on the MB bond data is only providing about 85% coverage. So we're losing 15% of renters which we know is a growing portion of all households. We also know that there are problems in matching Lynn's title data to addresses within stats New Zealand which is important to understand coverage of say ownership of trusts and owner occupation.

[00:14:12] And so there are some some known deficiencies in the proposed approach that's going to fundamentally change the ability to have good data for housing. I think we all recognize the fundamental importance of a home and how important it is to have a stable place to raise a family to grow connections with the community. And we would urge the committee to look at the timing of these changes and what needs to be put into place to ensure that we can continue to have robust data on which to make informed decisions that are also fundamental to the resource management reforms and projections that are going to be required to plan for growth over 30 years. And if we don't have a good understanding of the demographic and and other changes within

[00:15:05] our population in areas how can we adequately plan for that future growth and ensure that we're able to deliver outcomes that are better than what we're getting today. So with that I'll hand it across to Paul who's joined us. Morning Paul. My apologies for lateness. Andrew you must be running ahead of schedule. We are. Excellent. Well thank you for the time you've given to us. I'm going to speak more to the logic of this proposed change and suggest you know whether it's in a well-being context or in a social investment context the I'm going to suggest the cost of not doing the census is is a quantum far greater than the cost

[00:15:52] of doing it. And I'm questioning the logic for this change because in a world let's call it a social investment world which is is where we are politically at this point in time. The costs of not identifying groups and cohorts as Chris has just spoken to who are our most vulnerable the risk is that we don't identify and intervene upstream in terms of the causes of those issues. So let's talk briefly about homelessness which is one example that's in the public discourse at present. It is far more expensive to treat people in an emergency department or to have them in a prison than it is to address the upstream root causes to address that and everyone we've

[00:16:42] spoken to and including one of the most eminent statisticians in the world, Liam Cook who was our chief statistician and was also the chief statistician in the UK for a number of years has told us the dangers of not doing the census and the risks of not identifying those people inside households because this is the one opportunity we have to as a nation to look inside a household in disaggregated forms into gender into ethnicity into age and we have as you know a huge wave of older adults coming through to low fixed income retirement with no assets and no provisions for their adequate housing and so I'm suggesting to you that the logic of getting rid of the census is fundamentally flawed if you're even if you're just looking at it through a

[00:17:30] financial lens and people internationally who have suggested this in other jurisdictions have lost their jobs over this so it's something we're taking very seriously and so I'd ask you to consider that in your deliberations and also you know to not to put too fine a point on it but making homelessness illegal and then not counting them as homeless people does not fix the problem so where's the logic for this change all right hey thank you for those comments can I ask you you've made some helpful recommendations towards the end of your submission and you've one of them which is increasing the sample size from 50,000 to 100,000 which Professor talked about last week

[00:18:19] it do you think because the my understanding of the approach is obviously sample size is pretty important you've said populations under 5,000 basically get missed out by increasing it to 100,000 and then your second limb was about better community work in terms of identifying those sort of very specific communities that should be subject to some form of census how far does that go towards addressing the problem it improves the data quality faster so I mentioned in my opening remarks that you know is until 2032 that we anticipated being able to get down to communities of about 5,000 in population and so by doubling it would bring that forward

[00:19:07] and give you greater insights faster right I yeah carry on so it still doesn't replace fundamental that you'll still have gaps but it does improve if this is the the approach that's chosen to go it will help to mitigate the the worst of it and bring data forward more timely but and I'm not trying to be contentious but just you talked about homelessness even in the census we all realize that the census even now the last census had a quite a significant element that wasn't counted couldn't be counted and homelessness isn't notoriously difficult so even if you even if you carry the existing census wouldn't

[00:19:57] wouldn't we still be having trouble trying to get a better understanding of you know homelessness the usual example significant improvements been made in in process there internationally so snowballing is is a is a well-known and accepted methodology for that in terms of getting out what what we know is that homeless communities all over New Zealand even in small town and and large centres have a community amongst themselves and so with with some diligent work using that technique it can be a very effective method because they know where each other how to find each other to put it bluntly and and I think even in communities below the 5,000 threshold and it's worth mentioning for example the housing crisis that we have right now on the Chatham Islands

[00:20:45] and also in the Lucky Order the Stuart Island you know sure it's a population of 450 people but we have every summer an older adult housing crisis there which would be completely uh blind to if we were using government source data because no one from Stuart Island who's a who's a lifelong islander is going to get on a boat go to Invercargill to an msd office to report to ask to go on a list for a house which does not exist in their community so we're really worried about the rural damage that could be done by removing the census okay hey thank you for your time um very good submissions actually we've got good submissions on this bill but yours is a very good one thank you very much thank you right now we're going to our next submitter um hopefully Dr Katie Bates are

[00:21:34] you but Bruce sorry Dr Katie Bruce or Patrick Guillemore are you Patrick are they there on the screen here we are um Patrick or Katie are you available good morning Patrick how are you going and Katie Dr Katie box I think um thank you we've got 10 minutes with you um just please leave a little bit of time for questions for all shores we are the national peak body connecting Tangata whenua and community in the voluntary sector

[00:22:24] who supports the modernized census if it means more equitable more frequent and more community centered data Tangata whenua and community the voluntary sector contribute immeasurably to the well-being of Altaro so the census must be designed and now I view to see us we welcome your questions and we urge the committee to strengthen the bill so census so the census becomes more equitable more community centered and not less administrative data first risks deepening inequity our data mapping existing data government data already is in our view piecemeal small scale and disjointed particularly across statistics New Zealand the ministry of social

[00:23:09] development Oranga Tamariki uh Department of Internal Affairs and the Ministry of Business Innovation and Employment data admin admin data reflects uh who government already counts in our view marginalized groups voluntary networks informal groups rural organizations are least likely to uh to appear reliably annual outputs must include community indicators annual publication from 2030 is welcome but without explicit provision will default to popular up to populations and in our view housing only uh this must embed an annual framework voluntary rates community participation in social cohesion and please do not leave this

[00:23:57] to discretion katie oh kia ora patrick uh tenakoto it's a real pleasure to to talk to you about this really important issue today we have been trying as a peak body across the sector to understand the data that's happening across 27 000 charities a hundred over 100 000 community organizations and we have real concerns that if we are not able to do this with what's currently available then what is now on offer in this bill we'll just make that uh we'll just make that harder because essentially decision makers uh inside of the sector but also decision makers in government cannot make good funding decisions cannot make good policy decisions unless you have

[00:24:45] the right evidence and of course counting just what you already know inside of government just doesn't give you that that broader picture the sector essentially is largely invisible in the way that we present budget and in the way that that current administrative data is collected and I can give you a tiny example of having done a review into policing communities and policing outside of this mahi that police don't collect data on disabled people that they interact with and so of course there's just a complete void and a complete vacuum of information there even though we know that that overlap is really significant now the not profit institution satellite account is one of those examples of something that

[02:46:24] information will lead to poor investment decisions and wasted resources given the proof in health inequalities that rural people face we can't afford to ignore these people such that their outcomes are not able to be measured so we don't believe that the rural sector or the rural health sector in particular has been adequately consulted about the proposed changes we've seen no examples of those groups being involved in any advisory capacity and that needs to be rectified going forwards just want you to make to draw attention to the fact that the current coalition government is focused on health targets and outcomes well that may be a good thing if we because we do want improved health outcomes if we haven't

[02:47:14] evidence base for investment and accountability for that investment then we'll be failing those communities the outcome target focused approach means that accurate data is even more important and the changes proposed will make rural data invisible which will be a huge disservice to those communities in terms of focusing on the targets so in conclusion if the decision is made to proceed with the bill in its current form you'll be making a decision that will effectively right off the health of New Zealanders living in rural and remote communities we believe that an independent expert review is needed before any legislative change proceeds and that an independent assessment of the impacts of the proposed changes on rural data specifically rural health data is undertaken thanks for your time right thank you doctor we've got a question
