[02:48:54] so the data you're getting at the moment is is that sufficient in terms of allocational resourcing and stuff like that do you feel like the data you're getting at the moment is up to scratch well for the first time ever the data is beginning to be up to scratch and that health New Zealand is desegregating its major data repositories by GCH category the geographical classification of health so we're actually getting desegregation of health data by its rurality or urbanity if you like if there is such a word is that occurring I there's no willingness to do it but is that occurring as a result of a question come in our senses or

[02:49:42] is that occurring because they're using the data more more appropriately to get a better view on the rural communities we need the census data to be able to provide the GCH classifications because of the population distributions that is not coming out of health New Zealand or other administer of data since it comes out of the baseline so it senses okay cool all right hey thank you very much appreciate your time this morning thank you good right um now are we into more or are you there with us this morning or this afternoon now more are you there

[02:50:36] I am sorry very good the technical side of things kilda lovely to see you now we've got um 10 minutes with you thank you we have redraw submission thank you wonderful my name is maria cloney I'm appearing here as a co-lead for Tengaka Kahukura though I was also a member of the future census um independent evaluation panel last year our focus um with Tengaka Kahukura is on rainbow communities on queer trans intersex Takatapui people people who are mahu waka salua leua pa lopa fa fa fina a kava'ini leiti paka fa fa fina people who are non-binary

[02:51:22] or have variations of sex characteristics um many other names of names and labels rainbow people exist in every place and in every whanau and um but historically haven't often been visible in official statistics here today really to talk about the importance of high quality data for our communities and I want to ask you to delay this bill and to invest in states New Zealand to allow them to continue a full enumeration census approach as they design future high quality approaches we work towards an Aotearoa rainbow people grow up feeling safe valued in that they belong in all the places where they live learn and excess healthcare and social support and we do this work because we're not there yet this year we're acknowledging the 40th year of homosexual law reform which was a mark of acceptance and inclusion you know a while ago back at the

[02:52:10] time that I was in primary school um but at the same time this year we're also challenging political attacks on transgender women and children for example in the form of a puberty blocker ban and rhetoric about who should be allowed to play sport or use a bathroom our communities face higher rates of violence family rejection and social marginalisation and these lead to higher rates of suicidality and mental distress homelessness and addiction these outcomes obviously aren't universal but as a population they're more likely and they're heightened for people who are also experiencing racism navigating disability or marginalized in other ways so we know this sort of thing from decades of research and from community experience but until recently we haven't had good population level official data that meant we could compare our

[02:52:56] population experiences with other people in New Zealand so this has meant that our health and social disparities have been systemically under recognized in government strategies and across health and social service systems and responses to address these are under resourced census 2023 was the first one to include um questions around rainbow identities which came after decades of community advocacy almost since the time of homosexual law reform um data doesn't change much in isolation but the kind of insights we've seen already published from census 2023 could allow government agencies service providers and communities to design and resource solutions in a way that hasn't really been possible before and that impact is especially um especially over

[02:53:42] time you know as we see see trends trends emerge so we've seen progress but the proposal being considered in this bill will take us backwards as others have told you as stats New Zealand has acknowledged in their community consultation documents the shift to an admin data fair census model will result in significantly reduced data quality for the rainbow population some insights just won't be available especially for smaller population groups and within the wider rainbow population part of that's because there aren't any administrative data sources um about our identities about sexuality transgender experience or innate variations of sex characteristics they don't exist and they're not likely to exist in the near future or potentially ever um it's not usually reliable relevant safe or desirable to have government agencies collecting

[02:54:32] this sort of information um you know for example I mentioned um the issue around the puberty block abandon at the moment in a time we're seeing healthcare denied to children on the basis of being transgender it's not surprising at all that a lot of people don't provide accurate information about their gender to health services so um some of the markers that we might rely on are less reliable for um transgender intersex and queer people rainbow questions could be asked in the census attributes survey that's the proposal at the moment but the size of that survey will mean data quality will be poor or unreliable for small population groups or regional analysis and we essentially we won't have the um so the denominator effect we don't we won't have those population counts of how many rainbow people there are across the country there isn't an obvious way to mitigate the impact of this proposed change on rainbow populations

[02:55:23] want to acknowledge stats new zealand has done wonderful work um talking to rainbow community experts to keep this conversation going but as far as we can see there just isn't the maths for this yet they move from complete national enumeration proposed in this bill will just mean that we lose the level of insight that was possible um for the first time with the 2023 census there isn't a different methodology we could use to replace that so what's being proposed in this bill will give new zealand a worse public stats system as we said in our submission um production of official statistics requires investment and that's a political choice that you're um able to make so i'm asking you to make a different choice this bill will disproportionately affect rainbow populations as well as Māori disabled people pacific populations rural communities as we just heard and other

[02:56:11] groups that aren't reflected reliably in admin data so i want to recommend that you delay this change and invest further in a full enumeration approach while um building in more sources of administrative data over time as those become more reliable um Kiota thanks for your time welcoming any questions hey thank you Maura um very nice i've got a quick one question from Todd Steveson thanks Maura just um and as well i suppose it's good that you were involved in the future census independent evaluation panel i think i suppose that's that's really good and that you've and we've got your submission you've caught up problems you were just mentioning though an attribute survey at the moment that suggested solution from stats new zealand is that right and do you have any details on what that might look like um my understanding of the

[02:56:59] proposed model is that stats new zealand will um conduct an attribute survey each year so they'll do the survey of five percent of the population whatever the proposed number is now um and my understanding is that they'll continue to ask the question the rainbow demographic questions in that survey so um for small amounts of the population we'll have rainbow demographic data but we won't have there's no other way of counting um the overall population or knowing how reliable that is and those um but that those numbers the size of that survey isn't big enough to make statistical um valid statistical um analysis around smaller groups within the population no good no good submission and just like the uh rural um rural one before i think yeah it's a

[02:57:47] point well made that yeah you know these are the types of groups that will potentially be missed out by moving to this different model so thank you thank you for your time maura uh very nice thank you thank you right now we're going to blind citizens um i think we've got dr godfrey again and rose welkinson oh are they should we go to the next one after the next summer right um so do should we just pause for a moment is that what you said different perfect okay they can't just come in by phone we're just going to have a hiatus of your beaming in we're just having tittle issue just trying to connect our next submitter so if you're wondering what's going on that's

[02:58:33] what we're waiting for you you

[02:59:34] you so we've got the koi two center are you with us here

[03:00:25] oh is that you oh yeah please oh we we should sit put your hand up before please sit down now we've got um two minutes with you him a shoe there hi how are you very good thank you um thank you chair and thank you committee for the opportunity to speak here today on behalf of koi two the center for informed futures i'd like to extend an apology uh from sir peter gluckman this is a topic that he is very much passionate about and would have loved to have been here in person to uh to converse with you um if i'm i'm going to take that you've read our submission and i will start with what i believe is at the heart of this bill which is really um

[03:01:15] raising a fundamental issue for what liberal democracies face which is we need the availability of reliable trustworthy data to support long-term decision making and informed long-term decision making and high quality data is an integral part of the liberal democracy infrastructure and maintaining and uh maintaining public trust in an era where we have the rise of populism and democracies around the world faltering under the pressures of misinformation and the like for new zealand population data is being quite instrumental and particularly the census in

[03:02:02] particular in terms of how it is underpinned quite significant and quite complex decisions over a long period of time that not only shape the decisions that we're making today but will have intergenerational consequences over the much longer term and these decisions require robustness they require coverage they require credibility and they can't just be viewed as short-term efficiency for um because it is going to have some long-term consequences if we don't make the right decisions now in terms of um the opportunities that are ahead of us is obviously administrative data new analytical techniques and the possibilities that AI offer and given that the

[03:02:49] public sector is already looking at decision making using some of these tools they present a wealth of opportunities however they are not a simple substitute for well-designed survey based census data that we have been using for these decision makings to date and also equally important is that trust is not going to be maintained with a really good visibility for the public and for them to maintain confidence in how we're using these emerging techniques how we're using admin data and how we're going to evolve the i di and the likes is also equally important so we need a really good oversight we need mechanisms in which our public is maintained in the journey as we transition to a new state of what we think about and what

[03:03:39] we consider in terms of census data and the like i guess the um my closing remark and i'm really looking forward to taking some questions if that's an option is that this is a moment for us to proceed with caution yes there are significant opportunities that innovation presents and there is a lot that new zealand could do in this space however is whatever decisions we make need to be able to strengthen our long-term decision making not weaken it and if we want to make good long-term choices on issues that fundamentally are going to have intergenerational outcomes is we need robust trustworthy data with good oversight and

[03:04:27] good governance otherwise we are at a point where we risk undermining the very foundations of our democratic decision making so i'll stop there and happy to take some questions right mr. Todd Stevens oh thanks thanks for your submission and your recommendations you've raised a few things that are actually new that we haven't heard so i want to just ask some questions about those yeah absolutely please do well one is this idea of a defective population register and i actually where you talk about some of the countries that are using admin data so you that well i'm not trying to be controversial but you're obviously talking about it like every citizen having some kind of unique idea that then can help match data exactly is if you look at i guess where some of the other countries have gone with that is if you've got a good um individual

[03:05:18] level um mechanism if i dang individual i dang our population is then you can use that to build that population level um information and database that we need to then be able to provide the services that we need uh but however if you look at where some of the other countries are they're much more advanced in terms of the quality and the robustness of those admin data that they've got so they've been in a position to do that whereas we may not be there yet and we may need to go through a period of transition in order to ensure that our admin data is of the um the nature and the scale and the quality so that we are able to substitute to that if that is what the decision is um further i would also say is the way we've collected our admin data

[03:06:08] may not be the same way that others have done so there is a bit of um a journey to go through in terms of how we bring the public up to speed in terms of what that admin data is and why we needed yeah i think you can really identify too if we're going to do that it's probably a large public conversation to build more trust because i think some people would be uncomfortable shall we say about yet well they'd need to know you'd need to convince people about the that's useful the other quick two things sorry not to dominate um but you've you've mentioned AI i think that's kind of interesting and transparency around that if you just want to make some more comments about how you think yeah that should be governed and then also you seem to be saying that even the current IDI you think there should be some strengthening of um the oversight there around administrative

[03:06:55] data if you could just talk on those two okay so i'll start with the IDI is i think if you look at the evolution of the IDI the IDI is a really powerful tool that New Zealand has and if you look at where probably nearly about 10 years ago it was a world-class capability that New Zealand had and i think over time that um investment in it has deteriorated and it would be really valuable for us to um to invest in it so that if it is going to be the cornerstone on which so much of our public decision making is based on particularly in the social sector is i think there is a lot that we could do to strengthen the capabilities that are

[03:07:40] there i think that ties into the your comment on AI is there are again so many opportunities that AI proceed and at the backbone of the AI infrastructure we need good quality training data and we need good quality data that represents the population and particularly the underserved part of our population which right now we don't have confidence that the admin data we've got in place is going to be an adequate substitute for that so we do need in parallel both an AI infrastructure that supports it so that we can really leverage the data that we've already got the data we are going to collect and ensure we future prove the decisions we're making in such a way that we can truly leverage

[03:08:27] leverage the capability and the investments we're putting into these things did you just i mean my question is going to be about AI as well because i don't think that has been discussed in detail um is that is that something that you are suggesting that we would potentially include as part of the committee's recommendations on this bill that there needs to be kind of mandatory transparency around when AI is being used absolutely i i think if you look at the idea in particular there are a lot of opportunities and there's a lot of data in there that could that is and could be used further and more in a more advanced ways than we're currently using it however i think the challenge we've got which we've always had um and so do it's not

[03:09:15] just unique to New Zealand it's around social license and i think if we are going to leverage the capabilities we've already invested in and public public investments already gone into a lot of this is greater transparency means there's going to be more uptake more voluntary hopefully more voluntary uh sharing of information and better acknowledgement and awareness from the public for why this is really important at the moment um if you look at the census responses or many other things is the lack of public engagement is it's yes it means we haven't told the value proposition of why data is so important and so instrumental to how governments make good decisions

[03:10:00] but at the same time is we haven't actually clearly made it easy for public to engage and see how their data is being used and how they can actually contribute to the decisions that shape the country thank you very much no worries thank you so much for your time we short submission but very powerful so thank you very much appreciate your time this afternoon yeah they're not available okay so we're now going to call a hold we'll come back at one o'clock uh so we'll back into it over the benches thank you

meetings.title: Transport and Infrastructure Committee
meetings.description: This is "Transport and Infrastructure Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Introductory comments for a few minutes, then that's great and then we will ask lots and lots of questions. Just saying, I don't know if you follow where we've got to, but we're getting close to the end of hearing from everyone here from. Probably like, I think we're trying to get MSC in here as well. We've been to see ports in here, we've had customers in here, we've had land-based transport, we've had unions, so we've still got a few to go, but we're trying to get a coastal shipping, trying to get just a picture overall, but we can then make some judgments on, but it's very important for us to have the major shipping lines in here. So we're very grateful to be here. Thank you and thank you for the opportunity to be here.

[00:00:47] I think that this is an open statement certainly from the MSC. When we look at Zealand and the importance of trade for New Zealand and the fact that we're so far away from our markets, very much we look at a very long, skinny supply chain. Unlike other trades that we in the world where we have vessels that go round on to a number of ports and a number of countries, there is the ability to mix and match cargo so that you drive in good utilization. When we come to New Zealand, it's completely reliant on the trade into New Zealand and now the New Zealand to fill the vessel and drive the utilization both ways. So what's very important for us is how those vessels work the coast, how they make sure that they are spending as less time as possible on the coast,

[00:01:44] and are we doing the best for the New Zealand explorer and important returns of what the service that we're providing. So what you'll see from our submission, there were four pillars around that that we believe that were important to drive this. I'm happy to talk to them individually or we can do it. I think if you want to highlight some key points that would be very useful. So the key pillars that we see from in terms of if we're going to position ourselves in New Zealand for the growth that we're looking to drive and also attack cost and efficiency. When we look at New Zealand right now and you see there's roughly around about nine ports of which three are significant.

[00:02:32] And then there's a lot of other ports and you say when you look at driving capacity, the ocean capacity around those ports, what you do is you actually chew up important transit time. So a network can spend seven to 10 days on the New Zealand coast. So that means from the first port of loads to the last port of loads. If you're on the first port, your transit to your key market is plus seven days than the last port. So there's some inequities on the time that the ship's been on the water. The model that we have run on the part where we run now and it's very successful is the hub and spoke model. So that's really the first bit that we'll talk to. What that really means in terms of the New Zealand operation is where you focus your major network.

[00:03:19] So for us it's the Southern Star which is our network that goes up to Tangent Pelopas. So we need to look around a network. Good meaning. A ship. Voyager ships. So this network is called the Southern Star network. It currently comes down. It runs north-south through the coast. It then goes up to Tangent Pelopas which is our major hub. And from there we touch every service around the world. So from there you'll go anywhere from North East Asia, South East Asia, South Asia, Africa, Middle East, Europe. So essentially you're arriving in Auckland, going down the coast, then departing. Departing to Watch Armours today. Watch Armours. And going to Tangent Pelopas in Malaysia. So then if you look at the length of time that's been on the coast and then you sort

[00:04:08] of say okay well how could this be better? So our view on life is we would look to, the New Zealand has established two hubs. So you have one on the North Island, one on the South Island. And then you would feed of those hubs either by an improved coastal network or you would look at the infrastructure, land-based infrastructure to support that. Like on the obvious one being rail. So that would be Taungalutton? I don't like to put names on ports. But you'd have to pick, in the North Island Taungalut would be a strong contender. South Island would be a question of how you'd look at that. And to support that you need the coastal network that's going to support that. In the South Island you could argue that Eastern Seaboard, that could be rail, right?

[00:04:54] If you look at the North Island, when you've got places like Nelson and Napier, which are Nelson and South Island, but Napier and North Island which aren't as well serviced by rail, then you'd need to have a coastal network. To support that we would need some changes in the legislation just around the coastal network. Because the legislation sort of precludes us operating efficiently around the coast. And that's the same for any ocean carrier. The second leg of the discussion is the operational performance of some of the container terminals. We need to really nail down how we get some improvements in that space. If you look at the major New Zealand ports, they're all in the bottom

[00:05:43] portal, put to World Bank ratings on Efficiency and Dadaewoo. There is some work that's been done in Taungalut which will help if it comes through. But it doesn't answer, it doesn't provide the full answer. We need to look at how the cargo is flowing through those terminals and how they're operating. We believe that. Can you just tell us what your measure of efficiency is? Because we've had quite a number of different measures of efficiency, which have been given to us that I don't all marry up from some parties. So the World Bank publishes their measure of efficiency. I don't have that particular on me now, but it's a recognised measure. And that's where we're taking our information from and our comparison from.

[00:06:28] So we're not looking at Australian New Zealand ports or we're looking at comparable sized ports around the world. Oh, so you're not comparing one of our ports with Singapore or? No, no, no, it's comparable sized ports in operations. The other part of the pillar that we've looked at and I touched on it earlier is just what are we doing with the infrastructure to support the ships and the terminals when they're alongside? What's our ability to build sort of inland hubs so that we can stage cargo in that takes the pressure off the terminals when the ships are in and it allows the ship to come in and the

[00:07:18] terminal will work very, very efficiently. And the last one is really just around this developing this sustainable and safe school. We're very much focused on the development of people, development of skill and safety and safety culture around the operations. Okay, questions time. Yeah, right. Okay, I'm going to give Simon the first crack and then I'm going to go to the next three. But okay, I'm not seriously, I'll take it one then off that somewhere you go. Thank you, Mr. Chair. So a couple of things. You talked about efficiency of container handling. We saw Port of Auckland with the best of intentions attempt an automation project.

[00:08:04] It hasn't succeeded in the way they wanted, but we've seen automation be delivered in other ports around Asia Pacific to a greater or lesser extent. What do you think this committee should be looking at in terms of how to get the best out of automation? Like is it a stage thing? Is it a scale thing? Just pick a technology thing. One of the things we should be thinking about. So when we look at it, it's the best way to develop that is to use proven known technologies. And it would be fair to say that if you look at where we've been successful in the past and where we've developed terminals, it's by leveraging what we learned around the world

[00:08:52] and what works in the local environment. I don't think that there is one answer. It's a case of looking at the model that's been run. Is it the port, a transshipment port or a hub, a fee to port? How much do you need to invest in that? But really the focus needs to be on those hub ports. So there is no easy answer, but it's a case of leveraging off experience. And just in terms of the land-based infrastructure or inland port infrastructure, how close does an inland port need to be to the water for it to be operationally functionally effective? And what kind of connections are necessary? Are we talking about direct rail right under the crane or are we talking

[00:09:43] like a road freight route? How should we optimize that? So I'll be very much on that one. And this is around partly around an emissions discussion as well, is the inland terminal needs to have rail infrastructure and it needs to be serviced by rail. Part of that, one of the drivers around rail. And then when you look at the development that we have done on Rurikura and then the connection to the Rurikura rail side, I think there's the fact that you can fully utilize the capacity of a container on a rail wagon, but you can't use on a road wagon. So you need that scale. It needs, the terminal needs to have a strong rail marshalling area, so it doesn't need to go direct to crane.

[00:10:28] But the principle of an inland hub is that they, you start collecting cargo 10 days out before the ship arrives, but you don't want 10 days worth of vessel cargo on the terminal. So that allows the terminal then to core the cargo in after they've worked the previous ship. So an inland hub can be anything between four to five hours away from the terminal itself. Where you drive true benefit is when you place that inland hub close to the manufacturer or the processing of the product. So then you take off, do you take away the the road ball and you then provide the customer and the viable alternative to attack their transport cost, but then you collect a news rail.

[00:11:15] Very helpful insights. Thank you very much. So an example of that might be if you've got a railway line servicing, oh I'm going to use Fonterra as a major, major customer, you've got a railway line pretty much directly servicing a major plant. The railway then can take you up straight up to three hours, whatever it might be, can still take you straight up to your marshaling yards in your inland port and then straight on towards the ship. It's a bit more than that to be honest. Well a bit more than that to be fair from our view, because if you look at major plants like Fonterra plants that are all rail enabled, that's 100%, the container will rail and rail out. That's how it works. If you look at a lot of the meat processing plants,

[00:12:01] they are by the decommission deciding or they don't have a siding. So that will never work for them. So from a line perspective that you think about, if you put a rail siding in the middle of, again I'll use the Waikato as an example, you've got probably within three to four hours on a truck, you've got at least 10 plants, all individual, many different plants. So what you do is you then put the empty container in the middle of them, so the truck for the customers, just for that Hamilton back out to Te Kwawhiti for example, and then they bring the fallback to Hamilton and Rukura and then it goes on rail and to Toton or Auckland from there. Can you just talk us through the hub and spoke bit.

[00:12:52] So at the moment your ships just take us through because it sounds as though they're docking at a multiple different horse that you see them, you're telling us effectively that that's not ideal, you'd rather dock at less of them but do more work at fewer of them and then have the rest serviced by other, either coastal shipping or rail or whatever it might be. So there's a version of that, if you look at, for the Merce today we run essentially five different networks through New Zealand, so we have a network that goes to Tangier and Pelopas which is our main work, so that's our big network, so that's the southern start. So that's the one that goes, or currently goes Auckland, Napier,

[00:13:39] Littleton, Port Charmers, Tangier and Pelopas, so it's currently calling Napier as well for the minute for the apples. Then if you look at another service we run which is a similar vessel that's called the Nordenstern, and that calls Napier Nelson, Timaru, Taronga and that goes up to North Asia, so that goes up into China and so it's more of a direct service. The third network we run is direct service to the east coast of North America and that goes Port Charmers, Taronga, out. Then we have a shared networks, we have one up to the west coast which is only calls Taronga and then we have another shared network which goes to

[00:14:28] direct into Japan which calls five ports in New Zealand. Our view on life is if we looked at say the southern start became our main for one of the better terminology, hub and spoke service, is that you would then use, that would go down, come down to Taronga, Littleton for the sake of this conversation and go to Malaysia, so spends less time on the coast means you get faster transits for those key markets up to Pelopas and beyond, then you use the other networks to feed the cargo, so you would still definitely need an ocean feeder for somewhere like Nelson, which is land, land boats. You definitely need a feed of service, a coastal net. So we are talking about a coastal

[00:15:15] shipping, because that's one of the things that we get from the coastal shipping side, is a lot of, is you're competing on going through all those different places, you're competing with coastal shipping, your price, your economics is much stronger than theirs, but what you're saying is actually from your perspective, what you're doing at the moment is not optimal. I think it's important for us to differentiate between coastal and feeder, so what we're actually offering is a feeder network because we're only taking deep sea cargo, so we're focusing with the customer to put our container into the airport so that they can load it, and then we're feeding it into our mainline network. So for example, Nelson is a classic example. We have one of our services, North Start, it'll come down into Nelson from Asia,

[00:16:04] it's a smaller ship, so it can work Nelson easier, and we'll go in there and we'll position our empties in there for that market. Now the apples that go from Nelson go all over the world, so that will then pick up those apples and then take them to Toton or to Port Chalmers to connect to the different vessels, whether they're going to the East Coast, North America, whether they're going to Europe, where were they going. So what we're talking about here is a feeder operation. We're not out there competing in the coastal market, we're just putting our containers that we're going to load and around. Dan? Thanks Tony. Just continuing on the hop and spokes model you mentioned in your submission around section 198. I just want to get a clear understanding of,

[00:16:50] so you're at your 5th group, so I'm guessing that section 198 applies to the new 5, is that correct? Yes. In an ideal world, are you telling us that you think if we can claim 198 or get rid of it, or then what does that mean for the nurse? The 5 that you collapse at 2 or 3? So on the principle that four of the networks are offering really for us key direct markets as well. So the direct service to the East Coast, North America,

[00:17:38] the direct service to the West Coast, the direct service to North Asia, from the way our market share is working now, that will remain. The amendment to the act that we see that would drive a better cost efficiency and that is probably the one area is the ability to double call a port and a rotation. Can you explain that for us? Yes. So for example, if you had on the rotation right now, a deep sea ocean going vessel can only call a continuous voyage. So if we say, for example, from a business customer need, we say that we're going to come down, we're going to go to Christchurch, we can go to Timaru,

[00:18:27] to pick cargo up in Timaru, then we're going to go back to Christchurch to load. We're not allowed to do that because that's not continuous. Yeah, which is just, that's 198. So that that's the reference to the act there that that change wouldn't, it wouldn't see fewer services, it would just allow us to be more efficient. With the vessels, you've got the balance of flows of weight of draft and imports. So you can only go in with certain drafts into somebody's port. So you've got to be very careful about what you load. Because New Zealand, and if you look at it, we're an export dominant country and a very large part of our businesses and the business of all ocean carriers is your ability to put empty containers

[00:19:14] around the coast. Yeah. So sometimes it works better if you just get a double one. And is it your view that section 198 is kind of out of step with other markets? Like, how does it, if we moved away from that, would it be bringing us more into line with other markets? I think that different countries have very much different rules around what you can do on the coast. I mean, the US have got the Jones Act. Australia have got their own, their legislation as well. I think that the challenge that that we have in New Zealand is just the distance and the

[00:20:01] ability and the range of options and choice. Really, if you look at it from each network, you get one call a week. And if you can't get that right, then you're in trouble. Versus you go to some of these wider international markets, there's a vessel every second day. Okay. Thank you. Go on. What is the impetus behind that legislation? No, double. They couldn't continue us. What problem was that trying to solve? And how can we make those changes whilst still not opening up the original? I don't know. It's a legacy. It's been there for as long as I've sort of been kicking around.

[00:20:50] So it's very much a legacy legislation. I think that, yeah, there is, there will be other parties that have very, very strong views. So going back to your, you haven't spoken at the moment. So you've got the five different networks, but what I'm hearing is that they're not the optimal arrangement. You clearly want the one nine eight, so that you can actually do something where you go to the same port twice, at the very least. But if you had your optimal arrangement, what would it look like? So it wouldn't be a lot different than what you see today. In terms of, I don't see fewer vessels come because we will maintain at least four of those strings because they

[00:21:37] provide us with direct products into different parts of the market, which is very important for us. Parts of the world, yeah. But the ability to use some of those smaller vessels to move cargo, or to feed a cargo around the coast, both import and export, is the efficiency that, or the gain that would return efficiencies back to the New Zealand market. And do they still need, you still need a, excuse me, a range of different sizes of vessels to service New Zealand, presumably because the destination markets are also a different scale for us in terms of how to product going to them. Yeah. So it's a combination of markets. It's also a combination of, if we're serving coastal ports, what size vessel can that port handle? What's the total length it can handle? What's the total draft it can handle? So if you

[00:22:28] look at our workhorse service, the Sun and Star, it's certainly limited to ports that can call in and to New Zealand. So if you're looking, because we've had quite a bit of discussion about how many, you know, how many TEU, any given port could take. So if you're looking a few years down the track, we understand I think the vast majority now of new vessels, and I was not looking at the vessels going to be around a while, the vast majority of new vessels are going to be 8,000 TEU plus. So you're always going to get a range of vessels. If you look at what we're building right now, we're building in the 8,000 to 16,000 TEU range. We've got a fleet of the 23,000, 24,000 TEU range, but the model that we are running globally is quite different these days

[00:23:19] and it's quite different from other lines. So when we use the big vessels, 24,000 TEU vessels, they're on a network called the Gemini. So they're only going to call about five or six ports or eight ports in the world. And if you are a New Zealand exporter today, you'd use the Southern Star, you'd go up to Malaysia to a tranship port, you then get on one of the 24,000 TEU vessels and you'll go up to Rotterdam and put it on Rotterdam. And then in Rotterdam, we're using the 8,000 to 16,000 TEU vessels to essentially run a bus service. So you'd be dropped in Rotterdam and using the technology in the track, we can guarantee connections while the two vessels are alongside working. Employee making the most out of the

[00:24:08] technology that we have. And then it goes, it'll go to Hamburg, it'll go to Copenhagen, it'll go to wherever that Indus Nation is. So when you started, you said that basically we want two core ports, one in the north, one in the south. I'm trying to reconcile that with having a range of different size vessels that are also calling it the smaller ports as well. So the two core ports would, for example, be Totoroa and Zoltan, then you would use our North American service that would pick up cargo from there and imports would drop the imports into Nelson, that needed to be in Nelson, put the empties into Nelson, load the fruit out, then take it to another port to go back to Pelopas. So it's a case of still using our existing

[00:24:54] networks, but it's using them more efficiently. So what needs to change at those ports, those big hub ports? So they need to improve their efficiency in their rating. They need to improve their crane moves, they need to improve the overall throughput, because essentially what we're going to do, we're not actually going to take volume away from the walls around, but we are going to focus volume on those two big ports. So they need to be able to be able to step up and handle it. Okay. Do you have any sort of other questions? One question, just about cabotage. And I've taken the point about the 1-9-8 in the reference to continuous journey. The other sort of aspect to that is that the cargo

[00:25:41] needs to be incidental to what is kind of on board. So we've had a few views, different views around that, but in the carer aspect. What is your sort of acknowledgement or thinking in that particular space? In terms of incidental. In terms of the inclusion of cargo, so for coastal purposes, it needs to be incidental to the loading on the vessel. There's been some suggestion, for example, that perhaps that word shouldn't exist, but it should be more qualified in terms of quantum, those sorts of things. So just interested in your thoughts. So I'm not particularly right over that one, but I think very much that for us, it's about the feeder cargo for our network. We're focused on the deep sea cargo, we're not focused on the

[00:26:31] coastal cargo. Okay. But the fact that you are sort of on a journey around the place, still domestic cargo is a key part of, or maybe not a key part, is a part of what you carry? It's certainly not a key part of what we carry. What we have to balance up with is the capacity on the vessel for our deep sea cargo. So we won't load domestic cargo at the expense of loading the deep sea box. We also need to be very aware and cognizant of the impact to our ability to place empties into markets. I think it's fair to say,

[00:27:17] it is fair to say that we place a lot of empties around from Auckland and Toulon and down south. So if there is ability to load some cargo into that, but it's not our core, it's just the ability to move a, put a box down in the south island to load for export. So if you were able to do, I mean, you can kind of see that in terms of sovereign resilience and all that sort of stuff you need, you know, every nation needs to be able to have internal transport networks. And you essentially being able to fend your own deep sea product around the country is what you're asking for the ability to do not get involved in otherwise

[00:28:03] other domestic capital. But what would prevent you from stifling other ability to be able to to grow coastal shipping in capital and not rely on capital? So our focus is full ownership to go to and from New Zealand. When we operate around the coast, we are quite often at capacities that we can't load, we can't load domestic cargo. So when we look at, yeah, certainly, and when the sea is running hot and we're like right now,

[00:28:52] when we sail somewhere like from Napier to join another vessel or when we sail south, we're full. We're hitting our dead weight, we're hitting our numbers because it's carrying cargo that's going to go up to Toa Ronga, be discharged and then go deep sea and then that's the space that we load into from Toa Ronga off to other ports. So it's fair to say that, you know, it's not, it's not, it's something that it's fair to say that it's not something that we don't look at, but we look after, we have to look after our deep sea customers first. I appreciate your answers and I appreciate that you're a business, but if you're saying that,

[00:29:37] you know, your focus is on deep sea product and not the domestic stuff. Does your pricing mechanisms for domestic cargo align with that approach? So it's not sort of an incentivisation in terms of cost to carry domestic product or cargo in terms of the operations? If we have a customer that has a need, comes to us and says, look, you're doing all my deep sea, can you move something southbound, we will look at it if it suits our needs for putting empty equipment down there.

[00:30:28] If it doesn't suit our needs for putting empty equipment, so for example, we won't look at coastal cargo south north because we're contained a deficit in the South Island, so we will only focus on the deep sea cargo. Okay, helpful. Can I just follow that up? So sending lots of empties down south, I'm assuming that is because there is more product being created down south that needs exporting than there are relative to north. Correct. So the nature of New Zealand is 75% of the import market goes to the upper north island from Taupto on north and probably 20% will go in through Littleton and then 5% through other areas. The nature of our market is heavily leveraged into dry

[00:31:19] containers, so you're 40 foot standard containers because we're buying. That's the nature of the import market. It's the nature of the market, so you've got your outdoor furniture, your barbecues, your TVs, all those wonderful things that seem to be unspecialty really. You're outbound market and when you come down the South Island, it's heavily leveraged around food, meat, dairy, seafood, so that's a reef for export market. So everything that we can bring into New Zealand is we have to, when we're doing our import market plan, we have to balance on the dry cargo coming in, but we also have to bring empty reefism. The empty reef has come from different places to feed into New Zealand so that we have the market. If you look at the South Island,

[00:32:04] South Island is very, very strong in both refrigerated cargo, but also with the dairy industry and dry containers as well. So we're forever moving containers around to where the demand is for load, so that's a very important part of the balancing plan in it we do. So 75% of the imports come from the North Island? Yep. Question for Tracey here. What's the volume, the volumes that you're seeing in terms of the export side of things while in North and South? In terms of rough percentages, it would probably be these days, it will probably be about 55% North Island and 45% South Island. Roughly. And that 75% you said the first place, that was the upper North Island, we're about a lower North

[00:32:53] Island. That's on top of that as well. To be fair now, most of the stuff that goes into the, say, potentially over two or longer, is covering North Island. It's covering the whole North Island. Pretty much covering the North Island. So it outbounds 55, 45 North Island South Island, so it's kind of a very useful number. And your pattern you think will be influenced by other shipping lines of service in New Zealand? So those numbers that you think will be broadly reflective of? Broadly reflective. Yeah. Okay. So the bit about in and out, I mean, because we export a lot of refrigerator goods, so reefers. Yeah. So we're getting a huge chunk of our container movement. Reefers going out, then coming back empty.

[00:33:38] And then dry goods that are largely reverse, coming in full, going back out empty. So that can't do a whole lot when there's not much to do about it, but they can't do a whole lot for the cost of service in New Zealand. Yeah. I think from, I can say, from a nurse position, because of our position with certainly one key customer in terms of our dry boxes, we're pretty good. Using our dry boxes out. But the empty boxes we need to bring in. So it's a lot of milk powder that goes out of New Zealand. Yeah. So we're quite well. So dry boxes aren't too bad. It's the reefers. It's the reefers that we need to bring in. Yeah. The dry boxes all come into the North Island for the consumables, and then we need to

[00:34:25] clean them up and move them down the line. So dry boxes reasonably balanced? Yes. Yes. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Somebody else got a question? Well, I'm contemplating my next area of broad. Well, okay, that goes well. On your improved performance of container terminals, you've, I've now found the efficiency measure you're using is crane moves per hour. Yeah. So the bit that we've also got is the number of cranes you actually apply to any given vessel and others. How quickly you get the vessel loaded? Yeah. Not loaded, because obviously if you apply more cranes, they're both the same level of cranes per hour or movements per hour. You get it out twice as quickly.

[00:35:11] So is that the, is that the best model for you? Because I think what we heard from Auckland, I think it was Auckland in particular, was that efficiencies not bad, but crane moves per hour, but they only apply one of them, as opposed to they should maybe apply two. Yeah, that was the kind of thing that came through, I think. Yeah, I think certainly the ports of Auckland have more than one crane on their terminal. So it's your ability to, to lean into those cranes and use them. You will have some challenges around the, the length of the vessel and how many cranes you can put to a vessel. But we, we sort of view that when we look at the terminal efficiency there is, there's gains to be made by leveraging the learnings from some of these international

[00:35:58] terminals and the way they operate. We tend to see that the, in the North Ennis that the, all the measures and numbers that they use are quite internal or local and not really benchmarking against similar port sizes around the world. So what are the key things that we do when you've got, you've, you've made a suggestion about granting concessions for operations to experienced operators, which is a different model to what we've got here. But you know, what are the, what are the key changes you would see in our ports, in our, the ports themselves to make them more efficient. And while I'm asking a question about port, the one thing that does come through time and time again is the central relative tower on it. And that they're a little bit short of wolf space.

[00:36:43] I think that the conversation going around Tauranga and, and the wolf space here is, is a very important one on one that needs to be settled. And we can see it for stellar wolf. Yeah, yeah. Yeah. That's only part of the story though. You don't solve or hit the new heights just by making things bigger because then potentially you make the problem bigger. So unless there's the investment to go along with it and the investment needs to be in hardware, cranes, straddles, the investment also needs to be around software and systems and processes that they're using. The investment also needs to be in some of these inland ports to stage cargo to come in. So investments quite as multi-load.

[00:37:28] We, we firmly believe that international operators have a role to play there and an opportunity there to come in and achieve some of those efficiencies. You mean international operators on the port itself? Yep. Yes. Okay. Just one of the things my, the other question I was going to ask was just the, the attractiveness of New Zealand as a market to Moesk and to other shipping operators. It's been a bit of noise about that from time to time. What does it look like for you? So for Moesk it's a very important market for us. We have a very important agreement with

[00:38:20] Kotahi here in New Zealand. I think that from, that agreement is super strong for, for Moesk, super strong for Kotahi, but I think it's also super strong for New Zealand because that underpins the, the commitment that we have down to this, this part of the world and what we do down here. It underpins the investment that we're making with vessels that we come down here. As I said, it's, New Zealand isn't a cheap place to come to and operate in, but we're also aware of the role that we play. So any opportunity to do things better, smarter or faster, that at the end of the day will drive benefits back to the New Zealand export of the New Zealand importer. It's what we need to do. So we see, we see us as a very

[00:39:11] important partner to New Zealand. And that's a lot, that is a long-term commitment isn't it? It's a long contract, long-term contract, but you're obviously seeing it going, keeping on going and beyond that. Yep, so we've just completed a 10-year cycle and renewed for another 10 years. Correct, yeah. Colleagues at other questions, I have one that just flooded through my head, I'm terribly wet. I'm not seeing anything, yeah. No, it's good. There will be bits. Very clear. So anything else that you'd like to say, just to finish off with Tony? Thank you for the opportunity. I think, yeah, we recently had the chairman of our board of the APMOLA Group come through New Zealand. Again, that's an indication of just how we see New Zealand.

[00:39:59] And the opportunity that we see down here, we believe that we've got a lot to offer. And we'd like to like to see and explore those opportunities to see what we can do next. I think it's very important. An important comment is we can't do it in isolation. There are a number of parties and players around. So it's very, it would be very important for us to see a clear strategy for what New Zealand looks like going forward and how, and who's going to, then we see, really, we've got to bring that strategy together. But we are very keen to be part of that discussion and part of that growth and development. And we've had to say at the moment you're not seeing, you don't see a strategy at the moment.

We're having about 20 conversations. And we need that coordinating view to bring

[00:41:33] the people together and see that view. Good. All right. I know that's another question that I was going to ask, but thanks. It'll come to me momentarily, but I'll let everybody on. Just as, and as we mentioned in our submission, if anyone, if any of the committee members are traveling and would like to go and view one of our facilities around the world, please reach out. meetings.title: Transport and Infrastructure Committee
meetings.description: This is "Transport and Infrastructure Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Good morning, everyone. It's great to be here. Thank you for inviting us to come along and talk about City Rail Link project. My name is John Bridgman. I'm the chair of City Rail Link Ltd. How are you? With me, I've got Pat, the CE. We're just going to run through quickly. I've got a few introductory marks and paddle follow-up with a little bit more detail. I've had leadership roles in the delivery of $15 billion worth of underground transport infrastructure. I'm immensely proud of the work that our team have done since we last met here to progress the project towards completion and public use.

[00:00:49] It's a very exciting time for us at City Rail Link Ltd. We are only about 60 days away from having the physical works complete and the final run-up to public use and the facility being open. This is great news for Aucklanders and New Zealand as a whole. We can look forward to the biggest transport transformation in Auckland since the Harbour Bridge. For the benefit of those members of the committee who are new to City Rail Link Ltd, we were established in 2010 and it's been our privilege to deliver the project for Auckland on behalf of sponsors, Auckland Council and the New Zealand Government.

[00:01:37] City Rail Link Ltd is a $5.5 billion, 3.45 kilometre underground railway that will transform how Auckland moves and unlock more frequent, reliable and high-capacity rail network in the city. By removing the central city termites at White and Matar and adding two new through stations in the central city, City Rail Link will make it far, far and easier for Aucklanders to access jobs, education, events and reduce traffic congestion on the road network and support a more vibrant, productive city centre and region. Once operational, trains will be running every four or five minutes at peak times through the central city and the capacity to transport 19,000

[00:02:29] 45 minutes and I'll have the capacity to transport up to 19,000 passengers per hour through the city central stations at opening. That capacity can grow to 36,000 per hour over time and when we get 36,000 and when we get nine cars implemented across the network it can grow to 54,000 passengers per hour at peak. The City Rail Link will also transform the transport landscape in Auckland, supporting the city's growing population and creating more places for people to live, work and play. Very proud of having delivered on the sponsor's vision

[00:03:18] contained within the project delivery agreement. This included an asset life of 100 years designed into the stations to provide safe, resilient, accessible and high capacity rail system for all that can be something people are proud of for years to come or decades to come. Planning and design that went into the project in the early days has enabled City Rail Link Limited to deliver a project that is world-class in design and distinctly New Zealand that generations to come can be proud of. City Rail Link will enable, sorry City Rail Link will challenge the notion that our infrastructure should only be minimalist, minimalist and functional by being an asset that reflects an Auckland

[00:04:09] that feels more like a grown-up city. Aucklanders will also benefit from scope and design refinement that have happened along the way since such as the decision in 2019 to increase the capacity of City Rail Link to allow nine car trains rather than the six that were originally in position in the business case. Extending the platforms at the three new stations and providing the additional station entrance at Veriford Square is a future focused decision that makes City Rail Link able to continue to deliver capacity as a population continues to grow on the city. So we're grateful of the support and advocacy as sponsors have provided throughout the life of the project.

[00:04:57] Regular supporting, regular reporting to sponsors through our sponsors representatives as well as monthly meetings with the mayor and minister has ensured a productive relationship with a no surprises approach. It's an exciting time for the project as I say is the majority of physical construction works are now complete. We're now in testing and commissioning phase and well advanced and all parties are working to complete the project and compete, get people on the trains in the coming months. Three new stations are finished and standing proudly in Auckland City Centre. I think some of you are even going to go and have a look at them tomorrow. And we remain on track to open the second in the second half of

[00:05:43] this year. It's possibly the Aucklanders. It's the Aucklanders I think. The rest of us. Move to Auckland. I might have to come up and say look, carry on. So we're just months away from Aucklanders being able to experience the benefits of CRL first hand. And as you would expect at this time, we face a lot of questions about exactly when will it open. While that decision, sorry, you're going to spill the beans now. While that decision sits, it's not my decision, unfortunately, while that decision sits with sponsors and operators, we're confident that the focus now is very much on getting to that finish line, having a safe, reliable railway that's ready for passengers in the near term.

[00:06:33] And we're looking forward to getting Aucklanders on board later this year. The relationship we've had with our delivery partners, Link Alliance, has been very good. It's been positive and constructive. There's been no secret that and as many projects of the scale, there's been normal commercial discussions underway with a for some time, which are now on the final stages of being completed. And we're confident to say that Link Alliance remains focused on achieving its contractual completion obligations by the end of June, or within a couple of days after, but more or less. And the project remains on track for completion within the approved funding envelope. I'd just like to take the opportunity to acknowledge the extraordinary effort

[00:07:20] that's gone into getting to the project to this point. This has been a long, complex program delivered in the heart of the city. Link Alliance and our other partners in Auckland Transport, Kiwi Rail, Auckland One Rail and the early completion works contractors, regulators and our emergency services have all done a sterling job to get here. We shouldn't underestimate how complex this project is. There are a few and I mean a handful in the region that are as large and as complicated and as difficult as this project is to do it in Auckland with a resource that's got no experience in projects of the scale. This complexity is really outstanding. And we've been studying the timelines, etc.

[00:08:08] Everyone says this project is late. Yes, we're not up to the projected timelines, but we're only six months late in a nine year project. So it's given the challenges we've had and we're inside the funding envelopes. The changes in price have only been scoped to a large extent. It's really an outstanding project. The legacy of City Rail Link is not only that we see in the stations, but also in the standard that we set and the way the infrastructure has been delivered in New Zealand. The benchmarks this project set in sustainability, social procurement, health and safety and mana when our partnership is something the team is immensely proud of and has been rewarded for with awards from various groups.

[00:08:56] These out these outcomes matter because they endure beyond the opening date of City Rail Link. They live, they leave Auckland with critical infrastructure, transport infrastructure and they've leave the sector stronger and more capable to deliver projects like this in the future. We have better skilled suppliers, more sustainable delivery practices and we can carry these into the next generation of projects. So Pat's going to touch on briefly a couple of just with respect, how much time is great, but how much more time I've finished. I was just going to say Pat, I was going to say because otherwise we won't have much time for questions. I don't know, we're happy to take questions.

[00:09:42] I've given you a high level. I think you've given us a high level. Pat, is there anything you desperately need to say? Sorry, I'm in a minute. No, I think John's covered most of it. I did want to point out that, you know, with our main contract to link lives, but lots of other contractors working with us, but it's FinC, Solntown, Fibachi, Downer, ACOM, Tonkin and Taylor, WSP, Oricon supporting us. A lot of key parties made the success that we've had. Very, very thankful for what we've achieved in the health and safety areas that that is one of our key paramount focuses. We've had a very strong health and safety record over a long period of time, and that's all credit to the contractors that we've worked

[00:10:28] and the high standards that they maintain over with the link alliance contract alone, 24 million hours worked and with a very strong health and safety performance. So perhaps I could leave it there. There'll be questions on the target hardship fund, which has been also a critical part of the project and is now now come to a close at the end of March and we're currently in our what we call our trial operations and emergency response testing phase. So we completed more recently the systems integration, all the systems working together and now focused on running the railway and the stations together, working very closely with Auckland Transport, Auckland Rail and Kiwi Rail. That started last week and we've also started our emergency

[00:11:15] response testing first test was last week. This will continue through through May and into early June. So that's the key phase that we're in right now. And if you come on site, you may come across one of these emergency scenarios being being being worked on. That's an example of fire on the train, fire on the tunnel. There are various scenarios that we run through working very closely with friends, police and St John's. And those partners are really critical for us also. So that's the stage right now. And so still a lot of key testing to go through. But we're in a very good place. Very useful addition there. I'm going to take you to Aucklanders first and then I'm going to go to further south, so to speak.

[00:12:01] Well, OK, you're tongue used to you as well. Yeah, OK, right, OK, cool, right. Hey, thanks, John and Patrick for your leadership and to your team as well. And that's been a massively complex project over many years. So on behalf of Auckland, we're really excited about finally being open. John probably knows if my question is probably diving into the when it's going to be open. So, you know, you've mentioned 60 days from now, the works completed, so it's 30 June. Does that mean that in terms of the disruption around a CBD, is that does that come to an end at 30 June? It's it's ended already.

[00:12:46] The disruption we've created has has been tidied up for some months for any road cones and nothing to do with us. OK, that's good to know. The t-shirt that says that any road cones. One of the one of the more recent ones was the Watamata Station Plaza that was opened up. And you might recall the glasswork just being cleaned and hasn't been cleaned for about 10 years. And there's still questions about some of the follow on work that's necessary in terms of level crossings and other network improvements to be able to achieve the capacity the project offers. So can you just explain the sort of timeframe,

[00:24:22] scope, and opportunities of when that is likely to kick in in terms of hitting the nine-car metric? The nine-car thing... Well, I think the forecasts sort of talk about 2040 for six-car capacity in that sort of timeframe. Now, those depend very much on what point in time you start. Some... When there was a higher passenger transport use just prior to COVID, people were saying, well, that might be a hitter bit earlier. But you don't have to go digging up Auckland to do it when you do do it because that investment has been made now. Well, on opening will be the car capacity? 19,000. And how many cars? Oh, six. Well, Auckland Network only has six car capacity at the moment. Yeah. Yeah, that was all right. That'll be platform-based, won't it? Yes. So just interested as to how the relationship and performance issues that were experienced with the operators of the system,

[00:25:55] with Q-Rail and AT, if you're on a project you've been resolved, and are you comfortable with that they're going to be in a position to take over the assets that you've built? Yes, yes. It addressed a whole series of some of those issues, the governance gaps, but also supported the project, particularly in the system space, to overcome the need for each of the entities to have their own advice in that area. The systems piece was able to be dealt with at a project level much more effectively. And that's all to do with integration and how the project at the end of the day works.

[00:27:26] Kiwi Rail are effectively running the network, the tunnels now, and the rail part of the project. So just in terms of the lessons learned, wrap up what you're planning to do. Will you be involving Infrastructure Commission in that, given that they've already done Report 1 and Report 2? We haven't chosen a party to undertake the report yet. We're in the process of trying to select someone to help us with that, but obviously having someone with rail experience,

[00:28:12] but we're just going through that process right now to select a party to work with us. And we'll obviously be very happy to coordinate with the Infrastructure Commission. We want to build on those studies that have been done before. Yeah, I think that's really important, because given all the challenges the project had, including COVID shutdowns and changes in scope, being able to bring it in within the budget on budget, relatively on time, it's a wonderful achievement if that's what's delivered. I think it'd be incredibly helpful for the rest of the public sector delivering infrastructure to get those insights and to be able to have that shared. That's definitely the plan to share those insights externally. Absolutely. Good. A couple of quick questions from me.

Or is that just something that's sort of beyond your... That's really... I think you'd need to address that more with KiwiR. Obviously, there are some very big changes that need to happen

If you want to get to that level, is there that level of demand? That's a different matter. Yeah, and that sort of... So you've got a chicken and egg. The point is that a decision was made in 2018, really, in 2018 that we don't want to go back and have to retrofit those stations. No, basically, please. Along with the provision for platforms, platform screen doors which enable driverless trains,

So there are a number of scope changes. The platform length, the bearers for entrance, and that. Platform screen doors and some other connection issues around Britomart. So because the budget for the project was increased at one point, is that a reasonable reflection of the increases in the scope? Yes. Yes. So we want it more, we paid for more, we got more. I mean, we put the structure in place for a platform, for the platform screen doors, obviously, and the structure's there to avoid disruption further down the track of whether it had been major disruption and if it closed the restations. It's not easy to do when it's closed. If you go back to the original business case,

[00:31:21] it was 2.5 billion in 2015. There's been 1.6 billion worth of scope changes, whether it be Bearers Street, platform screen doors, all those sort of things that we've talked about today. 1.6 billion. There's about 700 million in cost of a year's delay on COVID. And a couple of 100 million or 300 million on increased risk and completely against what was understood at that original business case and some property and corporate cost issues that are about 400 million. And that's due to the 5.5 that we're currently in. It's good to have that break, Daniel. Thank you. And the other question was just about the staff,

[00:32:08] the contractors have worked on the project. And one of the things we talked about a lot is pipeline. And we know that one element then there was some coverage of that about tunnelers and we said, OK, we've done our tunneling. Brilliant. And you've got the other big project in Auckland, which is the airport. And just how is the workforce? What are you seeing of the workforce flowing through into other projects and just how that's been managed? [00:32:53] from the contractor side. The Australian market's pretty active. That's still very busy from an infrastructure perspective. So opportunities will be there. But naturally, as you've got international companies, they have to go to where the workers. And if they're specialist workers, they will often have to repatriate or show or go back. But unfortunately, we will see a lot of them leave because of their specialist skills.
