meetings.title: Justice Committee
meetings.description: This is "Justice Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Right. Are we there? All right, Mr. Heslip, five minutes. Thank you. In my professional opinion, this legislation is premature. As background, I'm an Emeritus Professor of Statistics at Massey University. I've been Professor of Statistics at the Australian

[00:00:45] National University. I've been a professional, sorry, a professorial fellow in the National Institute of Balanced Research and Statistics at the University of Willamille. I've worked internationally for, principally for the United Nations over the last 25 years. If you look at my submission, I've listed two dozen countries I've worked in. I've worked in government statistics departments in these places or with them. So my experience is principally around the methodology. And this, although you have a bill in front of you, the bill actually is going to be very difficult to bring to fruition in terms of what it's going to

[00:01:32] produce because the methodology is underdeveloped. My strong recommendation is that you set up an expert committee, including international methodologists and not like the State's New Zealand Committee, which they set up, which contain no methodologist. It contains a statistician without any experience in official statistics. So what they produced is useful but inadequate for purpose. So State's New Zealand have plans. And their plans are partly outlined in the document that they've produced and called Creating Household and Family Information

[00:02:18] from Administrative Data, Progress Limitations and the Next Census, March 2026. In it, among other things, they mention that in terms of being able to match the 2023 census with the type of administrative data that is available to the present, they are only able to match households exactly for 50% of the population, half the population. In other words, half the households are not correct. And you're planning or I don't know whether it's you. The plan is to move down this path and produce data which is clearly not equivalent to a census. New Zealand, if it moves in this direction, is completely alone.

[00:03:05] There is no country internationally that's doing this. There is moves to use administrative data, but the idea of using administrative data as a primary source is not used anywhere. So, brave or foolish, you'll have to decide. But I suggest that you're moving and this bill is moving prematurely toward something that has been talked about for 20 years internationally and abandoned in a number of countries. Egg on the face is the risk. The cost of the census previously, the last two censuses, both government statisticians and bold resigned. They were not statisticians. They were a government appointees or public service

[00:03:55] appointees without skills, the necessary skills to do the job. They probably didn't listen either. And so the cost overruns in 2023 a result of an appointment. And they're the result of not listening to some of the expert advice that is in stats, Professor, can I ask you a question? So, your contention is that the methodologies flawed. Do you see when you talk about going to a reference group and doing lots of stuff and basically delaying it? But with all your experience,

[00:04:43] do you think the methodology can be made to work that there's a much higher confidence level rather than 50%, but maybe 90% or whatever? How would you go about improving the methodology? Or are you just generally opposed to the administration style new approach of using administration data? I'm not opposed to using administrative data. The current IDI, integrated data system, has as its backbone, the census, a field based census. And if you take it out, stats New Zealand will call what they, when you take it out, they'll call what's left a spine. But the spine is not what I would call a backbone. I know it's choice of words, attitudes, but it's not a backbone because it doesn't contain the rich information that a census

[00:05:33] contains. It's information from different databases at different dates. I know you've got a counting date, but it's a legal fiction in many ways because the data comes from different databases with different ages with different quality of information. Okay, so if I am fair from what you answer there, and I'm not trying to be content, I'm just asking. You're saying without a census to create the backbone, we're going to have trouble getting reliable data. My point is, could you see a way of using admin data to create a backbone through better methodology?

[00:06:19] That's my real point. I know other countries succeeded yet. I think it needs more work, and I think that's why I think you need to be aware of the steps that if you're moving in that direction, the steps you're taking. Okay, I've got Mr Stevenson. Thanks, Professor. Thanks for your submission. A quick question. Yeah, obviously, this administrative data is available today. Have you done or you're aware of any work where it's compared the administrative data available today via Stats New Zealand and say the last census before that, and what the variations, how much that aligns? So that was what I was why I mentioned this report from Stats New Zealand, this creating household and family information through the administrative data,

[00:07:08] and why I mentioned that to match the families exactly, they've succeeded only with 50% of households. Now, one of the consequences of this for the electoral thing is there's a lot of mismatch. There are households which appear in the administrative data that don't appear in the census and by some person. This means they're actually counting people is going to be extremely difficult. Counting people accurately is going to be difficult. Setting electoral boundaries is going to be very tough. And for those of you who are in Parliament, you are not going to get, although this is peripheral, I suppose, you're not going to get detailed information about your electorate anymore. You may get some general information about it, but if you're interested in subgroups within the electorate, I'm sorry, not there. Yeah, okay. I can't commit.

[00:07:59] In terms of the alternative, we've heard that the current census as it is set up at the moment, maintaining that moving forward is not sustainable. Is that a view that you share, or do you believe that there would be a way to continue the current model as opposed to using this administrative data? If you want my professional advice, you'll be ill-advised to anything other than a field-based census next time round. It would create the time to start thinking through what it is that's needed if you're going to move to a system that's a little more like this. But there is no country in the world that doesn't either do a field-based census or have a register, as they have in Scandinavian countries. We have no register.

[00:08:50] Stats New Zealand will claim they have a spine, which is made up of not a register, but a sort of collection of administrative data sources that have got different IDs and different types of information, which are mysteriously put together to produce one animal. Despite the fact, they're really the backbones of three different cats. By register, you mean some kind of list of every person who's currently in the country? Absolutely, and that's what Scandinavian countries do. They don't run a field-based census because they can use that as a backbone or a spine to put the information from the surveys on. In New Zealand, we're going to do a survey, which is actually not as big as you think it is. It's going to struggle to give reasonable information for small groups. It's not going to have that

[00:09:41] information, either from a register or a field-based census. Yeah, I take the point that you might have a lot of detail, but it's incorrect to say, isn't it, Professor, that they're not going to do field surveys. They're just going to do smaller tailored surveys. Yes, I know, but that's a survey and not a census. It's at most 5% of the population. I mean, what about the other 95% percent? It can be used to calculate estimates with a given accuracy for large subgroups, but it's not going to give you detailed information. Certainly not for individuals, certainly not for groups, for example, within electorates. It's just not going to be possible anymore. Right, yeah, and I take that point that it's going to be not as much data, but if you're doing a 60,000 person survey type stuff, the confidence level, what's your

[00:10:35] deviation on things like that? Are you saying that can that be seen as representative of the border data around how many people are in the country, for instance, or what nationality they are, what ethnicity they are? I think I'll stay away from ethnicity for the moment. That's a whole half hour. Accept to say that different databases record ethnicity differently when you're trying to amalgamate them. There's open questions about how you might do that. You say 60,000 people. In fact, Stats New Zealand will say over the course of five years, they might have 250,000. So the first question I'd have for you is what period is the supply for? What year does the supply

[00:11:20] for is putting five years data together? It's certainly not a counting day. And the other part of this is that people want information for subgroups, and I'll stay with electorates. Let's say very roughly 120 electorates. You divide 120 into the 250,000. You now got 2,000 people over the course of five years. That's 400 a year. You don't get very good accuracy from 400 a year, even at electorate level. When you look at subgroups, you'll find you're in trouble. All right. Hey, thank you for your submission. It's been an interesting start to public submissions on this. Thank you for coming. Thank you. I think I might be unusual, and most of the people apart from Stats New Zealand who turn up won't have any

[00:12:09] psychological comment. Yeah. Thank you. Right. Thank you. Now we're going to Craig. All right. Mr. Atkinson, are you with us? Craig, are you there? All right. Here we go. Good morning. Still morning. We've got five minutes with you. Yeah. Just one clarification. It's about the electorate boundaries in the cystistics ones next week. Yeah. We've got different timings. We've got stuff next week

[00:12:59] in the following week. Yeah. Oh, yeah. Yeah. You just got the first one just sounding more about the cystistics than the electorate boundaries. That's how I'm just confirming that. Yeah. So, yeah, for the electorate boundaries and the altering of what the, I think it's a play straight out of Mademois Thatcher's playbook, where basically they would remove areas that they'd no votes one way to a different area just to take the numbers and set the pack of cards before the election goes. Yeah. I had a very questionable about when it sat in front of the Justice Select Committee, when you could say you've all got a conflict of interest and that it comes across more.

[00:13:44] And I put the word in front of the Justice Select Committee of Dodgy. And so we could rename you the Dodgy Justice Select Committee because it doesn't come across when you want into all the boundaries where boundaries are fixed and they stay when you're only doing it for an election thing. And how it refers to the cystistics, the information that we collect then through algorithms then gives you about a percentage of what, where people are going to vote. So, yeah, I think you just, it's an election cycle, isn't it? So that's my submission. All right. Thank you for that. What I would say to you is that under the previous elections, and there is a standard approach where there's a electoral commission that looks at the

[00:14:35] boundaries and that's assessed every time prior to the election. There's no change to that situation. And as MPs, we are not involved in that personally. So that's an outside appointment select process. So that doesn't change under this bill. But that's a different point. Anyone else got any questions from Mr. Atkinson? All right. Hey, thank you for your submission this morning. You looked like you're in a sunny place. Sounds great. Is that right? Yeah. Looks good. Okay. Thank you. Bye-bye. Now, we have... No, Jasmine. Right. Jasmine Richardson, are you available? Oh, yeah, of course. In Met, you could come up into some preparation if you don't mind.

[00:15:27] Jasmine, are you with us? Sorry. Just wait there. Just sit there. Just sit there. Really, just in devotion. Poist. Poist, yeah. Jasmine, are you there with us? All right, cool. Good morning. How are you? Your mute is on. I'm mute. We can hear you. Thank you. We've got five minutes with you. So looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. Oh, yes. Thank you. I just wanted to, I guess, probably reiterate

[00:16:19] what other people have already said. I'm worried about people who go out and are not enrolled, but want to go and vote on the day or a few days before and won't be able to, if these changes take effect. I'm worried about that being a lot of young people, Maldives, Pacifica. I guess people who I feel that I can relate to and believe I would want to know who they would want to... Oh, I wouldn't want to know, but I would want them to have had their say and tick their box about, I guess, the people who will lead our country. And yes, that's... Hey, thank you. Thank you, Jasmine. Unfortunately, I think that what you're relating, what you're talking about there is not under the electoral boundary,

[00:17:05] but actually under the electoral amendment, but it's under the other bill that we're looking at, and that we've considered. That's for this committee. But thank you for those comments. Anyone else got any questions for Jasmine? All right. Thank you very much for a very succinct presentation, but that's good. Good to hear, and I think everyone recognises the need for democracy to work in practice and in action. So thank you for those comments. Right. Now, Mr Marshall, you're here with us. Yes. Yes. Yeah. Hi. Thank you, everyone. Thank you for giving me the opportunity to speak.

[00:17:51] I want to talk about the electoral bill. Take up my submission as read. Reminder submission. There is an underlying malaise of our political system that in the drawing up of electoral boundaries, not only accentuates, but also entrenched, the electorate boundaries demarcate, demarcation along lines of economic and political disparity between the main centres and regions concentrate more political and economic contribution or power in the cause at the core centres at the expense of the regions. There has been a gap between the urban centres and regions described by theorists

[00:18:43] as the core and periphery, but not just disparity in resources, but also political power. This becomes even more pronounced at national level and even more so in political contexts. In this disparity between urban and regional, how well we prosper or even whether our existence in the future may depend on our ability to better integrate the two parts together, so that not one party is comparatively worse off. The framework of the unitary state existent form in New Zealand and we're necessarily, we're necessary expedient, but in practical terms and under normal conditions, it does not operate towards reconciling the two halves.

[00:19:33] Underlying many issues of race, gender, class, there is one common denominator and it is this dynamic of the stratification insecurity for oneself or one's individual group in any of these categories, if analysed deeply, has this conflict as its key driver. Peters Blunley credits the loss at the last election in part as being the inability of the last government to recognise the needs of the regions, much less listen to them, take the unaffordable cost of flight travel for regions, no viable solution has been offered, at least from the state, much more though this threatens to degrade our tradition of liberal democracy. It comes as no surprise that

[00:20:30] the last decades, in the last decades, governments and the officials have been hostile to public planning in terms of some national interest. This exchange of resources becomes political, urban dwellers may require access to resources from the local region, for the low regional dweller he faces other challenges in form of increased costs associated with maintaining the system for the resources and will seek support from the government who is by proxy the urban dweller. The next point is secondary but still important, how important is it that any system does not perpetuate itself by being mapped onto existing patterns of existing stratifications of

[00:21:19] socio-economic spatial inequality. Both major parties have a political character which favours interests of different groups. This political tribalism is at the expense of any view of attempted national interest or common good and that leads us divided and fragmented at best. The drawing up of any new election must be drawn up along lines of political neutrality which divert from following the demographic outlines in which these interests could be captured within the populations inside their borders, boundaries. I fear without more flexibility to apply a more proportionate system through the design of boundaries without such measures will lead to further loss of trust between the two sides as it's upshot.

[00:22:08] Right thank you very much. I've got a question from Mr Rose Warren. Now thanks Max, you opened up your contribution to urban regional gap and I was just wondering what safeguards do you think are necessary to ensure that changes to boundary setting rules cannot be used to advantage the government of the day? It's a really difficult one and I think it's a pre-nial question. How do you manage the problem of majoritarianism where you have voting? Most of your centres, most of your population is going to be in the main centres and that's been that way since I think the 1960s there was that

[00:22:58] shift in 1940 just before the Second World War. So this is a problem that we're going to have to struggle with but it's a challenge reconciling this problem but that's part of what democracy is. It's this process. All right hey thank you for a very thoughtful presentation is a conundrum isn't it because where people seek to live and choose to live I should say you have natural concentrations which mean economic power and all those other things but

[00:23:43] trying to devise something that is fair because there's two sides to that debate you're putting forward but thank you for that thoughtful presentation. Right Kay Jones where you're there I see. Thank you. Now we've got a private view as well. Just swapping to a message here. Thank you our members of the committee. Thank you for the opportunity to appear. Oh hey Jones. I'm a fifth generation Pākehā New Zealander with Scotland, Scottish, Shetland and English heritage and with Māori whānau. I understand you've read my

[00:24:29] submission. It was very short because I focused more on the data and statistics one and since that time I've been considering other points. Usually when I present before you I talk of my own experience and I could talk about working on the local government commission and going to areas and listening to people presenting on why the boundaries of their areas should be changed or not for voting purposes but today I would prefer to quote other people friends friends of friends and from documents that you should have read. You'll remember the recent apologies that the government gave to survivors of abuse and state care. Survivors describe their feelings thus we have been disconnected from our being

[00:25:17] like a stranger in our country alienated from family and heritage born into a world innocent and free oblivious to systemic behavior which has denied and exhausted and extinguished our flame of hope. In his apology Prime Minister Luxon said many of you were denied knowledge of what happened to you because record keeping during your time in care was so poor or deliberately inaccurate your own personal information was often withheld destroyed lost or wrong or incomplete often there was no record of your ethnic origins your family and far now the harm you experienced the complaints you made your medical treatments or what social workers said about you.

[00:26:03] Labour leader Chris Hipkins said Maori survivors themselves found themselves in a system that inherently worked against them and in care settings that were blatantly racist suffering severe abuse. Many Maori survivors were made to feel for karma shame it having been stripped of their identity as Maori. Whakapapa and Nanamahotahaki were transgressed and people were severed from their far now and when we go on to look at for example the NGOs that were advising um survivors on appearing and putting their testimony they recorded okay we're talking about

[00:26:51] the electoral boundaries precisely record keeping not being consistent when we're talking about a system of recognizing for the purposes of defining electoral votes somebody been on the Maori role they first must know that they are Maori they must be able to prove that they must be able to go on to that role I have heard from Rangatahi of the Koro who was uplifted as a child and who has no factual basis for being on the Maori role they are visibly Maori in terms of how we perceive that but they do not have that accurate data now in a census they can I self-identify but when it comes to administrative data they are erased now this is part of a process

[00:27:39] of disenfranchisement if people have difficulty getting on to a role then that takes away their right to be voting that area if there is already a large population of Maori that would be enough to justify an eighth electoral seat and you are by postponing that for another five or more years you are also reducing you're saying these votes have less value than those of people on the general role so it is a push for Rangatahi registering for the first time or people considering I've never voted before and I'm in my 50s but maybe I will this time when they make that choice of whether to be on the general or the Maori role knowing that their vote is actually going to be

[00:28:28] counted more if they're on the general may make a difference or it may make them so disillusioned angered whatever the emotional import of being seen as less and being excluded so my reasons for referring back to the survivors and states care system is because it was not just abuse of people's physical and emotional well-being it was also an attack on their whakapapa their connections to their identity and their ability to prove that and this bill does the same thing through the process that has been imposed to say we will count less we will defer we will delay we will not count people I'll stop there in case there's any questions I've got a question

[00:29:15] from mr brother thank you and thank you k 0.6 of yours mission says the effects would be to restrict the representation of all mighty role voters until 2032 or later however between now and the boundaries review that just concluded last year if there was no change there wouldn't have been any change to the prospective boundaries or mighty representation or general electorates but before 2032 anyway so how does this bill go about impacting on that well this bill says it will delay the count and if we've if the if census happened quickly and there was the count happening then that would be one of the factors so I haven't got all the details in front of me but I know

[00:30:01] that when people say oh we will delay something for this period of time I'm a former public servant saying oh we'll delay it till 2028 means it won't actually happen to 2032 the the delays in implicit in the system mean that less attention will be given to that and of course we've also got those other factors that are causing disenfranchisement like the removal of voting rights for prisoners which if you are denied the right of that doesn't have anything to say if you can't just take one element question all right because it doesn't have anything to do with this bill um what is changing through this piece of legislation that if it weren't brought in would impact mighty representation for the 2032 election because the next boundary review following what would have been the next census

[00:30:51] would have also changed for the 2032 election so what is changing well for one thing what is changing is the messaging from government about who counts and who gets to vote okay all right he thank you um yet again for presenting to the committee um and taking the time to turn up today oh well I'll perhaps be able to answer more questions on this next week when I come back to the down to statistics bill right okay thank you now I'm going to I have to ask um public members professor and who others um mr. Marshall as well could you please leave the room we're going to go into a private session but thank you for all three of you for your contributions this morning

[00:31:37] thank you I hope you didn't teach me stats at Massey University all right cool you

meetings.title: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
meetings.committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
meetings.description: This is "Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] We have only 20 minutes with you and we want to really focus on intellectual property and copyright legislation. If you would like to start with some opening remarks, if you keep them brief so that members get enough time to ask questions, that would be really helpful. Thank you. So, Chair, members of the committee, thanks for the opportunity to speak today. I'll start maybe with why this conversation matters to us. For us, intellectual property is how New Zealand turns great science into real world impact. I think as every time

[00:00:48] I've been to a select committee, we've brought some of that real world impact with us to show you. So, yeah, we knew you'd expect morning tea. So, we've brought some snapples for you, some that are not commercially available. So, for us, it's not theory. It's actually what we do every day. And I love the fact that we're having select committee discussions around intellectual property. I think it's great and it's always timely. For us, that's where great ideas meet real world impact and create value for New Zealand and in a lot of cases beyond that. So, I'll give a couple of practical views of how IP works for us on the ground. And as I said, we've brought some intellectual property with us today because while IP has legal frameworks, patents, plant variety rights, great innovation and complex multi-jurisdictional alignment, it's quite often something you can hold and in this case, eat. So, I'll

[00:01:36] give you a bit of a sense of scale. So, over the last decade for the bio economy sciences to shoot our royalty revenues have grown from around 27 million to this year forecast just over $100 million. That makes us the largest royalties earner in the country. Great for us as an organisation that's all re-invested in science but actually the thing that's great for New Zealand is those royalties only come off the back of commercial success. So, that's one of the things we really like about the royalties. So, when you think of us, obviously, we've had flat to declining government investment. So, this is a way to keep investment going back into science, which is excellent. So, look, our model is quite simple but really deliberate. We invest in long-term science. We develop intellectual property. We partner with industry incubators.

[00:02:23] We create new products and in some cases companies generate royalties, equity and returns and then in our case, we invest those back into the next generation of the same thing. So, we do it across a range of areas, licensing, joint ventures, startups and we've got a few examples of research for equity models as well. So, one of the things we quite enjoy is the flexibility that we have to be able to take different approaches because there's no one size fits all for us. So, historically, in our case, we've had a lot of commercialisation that's been science sort of push, which is great. We think there's absolutely a place for that to continue but we are starting to evolve where we're increasingly balancing that towards being more market poll as well. So, what are the things people are asking for? What are the things industry investors,

[00:03:11] entrepreneurs are asking for? How does science connect in with that to make their great ideas even better and more sticky? So, the idea is to sort of move from alongside sort of saying, what can we do with our sciences? What problems does the world need solved and how can our science help? So, one of the things I guess as we talk around things like policy and I've touched on this already is just cautioning on making things too rigid. So, as I said, the flexibility that we've had has been excellent. We've had everything from spin outs research for equity. We've had spin outs that we've kept equity. So, we've been on the cap table for. We've had spin outs where we haven't. We've had spin outs where founders have gone out of the Institute into the

[00:03:56] new start-up. We've had ones where they haven't. All of those have worked well for us. And when you look at intellectual property, there's quite a spectrum. So, if you think of sort of some of the shorter cycle innovation, a new software tool, a new device, they're quite often built by quite a discrete team, a few people that rolls out. Then you look at the apples that we'll share in a second. Those are 15, 20, 25-year breeding programs with potentially 50 people involved. Then there's post-harvest support, pest and pathogen management, and sometimes support and market. So, if we apply the rules to both, we run the risk of actually creating some challenges. So, Roger, can get that apple out somehow. So, what you're seeing here is intellectual property in the flesh. These are not commercial. So,

[00:04:42] I know whenever I've come to select committee before, people have sort of said, what's the next generation? I think, Fumzie, you've asked that a few times. This is the next generation. These are red-fleshed apples. These are not commercial. We've purposefully taken the seeds out because, IP. It's not that we don't trust you. But intellectual property needs to be protected. So, these are two different types. They're in train. You might have seen we launched alongside a partner last year, FizzApple, which is also red-fleshed apple. So, for us, we've developed a DNA marker that allows sort of baby seedlings to be tested early, which means, in our case, 100% of those trees planted in our red-fleshed breeding programs are red-fleshed types because it's not always the way. So, even if you planted the seed, there's no guarantee you'd

[00:05:29] actually get that apple. So, for us, if you look at the history, you know, 1998, we did the first generation of crosses for a red-fleshed apple, second generation in 2004, third generation in 2011, and then last year, launched Fizz alongside a partner. These are the next ones that are coming through. Why red-fleshed? High anthocyanins, so antioxidants, also good novelty, and hopefully, as you're tasting as they go around, they taste good, which is quite important for food that you eat. So, last year was the first planting. So, about 10 tonnes were produced last year of the Fizz. About 54 tonnes expected this year from Hawkes Bay and Nelson. So, this is intellectual property coming to life, but with a lot of people involved. So, behind

[00:06:16] this is genetics, plant variety rights, trademarks, global licensing agreements. So, in our case, it's, you know, for a lot of things, it's not one invention, it's a layered system of IPV. It only works because we've had long-term investment, stable ownership, and flexible commercial models. So, we see this right across what we do. So, plant varieties, as we've touched on, a small number of cultivars can generate large returns for New Zealand over decades. And then we have startups like CentiumBio, that's just had a good capital raise recently. With 15 years of research turned into a venture-backed company, the founder went along with that. It's actually still housed in our Mount Albert facility. So, we're closely connected into it, and we still sit on the cap table for it. We've got other ones like Callocurb, which is using hops extracts to help with diet sort of suppression, to

[00:07:05] help with weight loss, to compete with things like OZEMPIC, but more naturally. So, in that case, we have patents, trademarks, clinical science, and research for equity that we all connect into that. So, I guess one of the things we're really proud of is that we're already driving towards that balance that's being talked about. So, the ability, in some cases, for the scientists to actually go out and commercialise their IP, and in the other cases, where there's a big group of people for that IP to be commercialised, and that royalty revenue to come back in and actually fund future science. So, that's a big part of what we do. So, for us, I guess it's really maintaining that flexibility, that ability to be part of that. So, probably three sort of key messages. We do have a system that works pretty well today.

[00:07:52] And I think with the merger of the BSI, the ability for us to take the learnings from one into the four that have come together is really quite strong. There's clear value. There's billions of dollars. You might have seen recently, NV Apple was now a billion dollar global Apple variety, developed out of a CRI. And the second one is the strength of that system is its flexibility. So, trying to not have one size fits all and constrain things. And thirdly for us, opportunity is to sort of just make sure we strengthen that balance with the right settings. So, ultimately, we create more companies, more impact and more investment over time. So, in our case, intellectual property isn't just something we manage. It's something that builds New Zealand's future. And in our case, also, pretty good apples. So, thank you and

[00:08:39] happy to take any questions. Thank you. Your intellectual property does test really well. That's always important when it's food. And first question from Dr David Wilson. You've said all the right things for me. I've got Mark and Paul, we're a science person trying to bring those two things together. That's fantastic. I just interested in a couple of the mechanisms here because in terms of product development and business development, that sort of thing. IP in one hand is actually protecting something. Correct. And on the other hand, we also talked about research in exchange for equity. And I'm interested in the mechanism for that and how that's working. And also, if we're protecting IP, how do we

[00:09:24] actually encourage tech fusion across our system in New Zealand still protect it? Yes, there's probably two parts to that. Maybe if I touch on the research for equity, but Richard, if you want to talk about how we still allow sharing of knowledge without losing the IP, if there's anything to add. So, maybe a good example of research for equity. I don't think I'll be sharing anything that's too confidential. So, CallaCurb is a great example. CallaCurb was designed out of the science system. It was taking a Marisate, which is a bit of extract from hops and finding that they did have appetites of recent properties. It goes in a capsule. It's not something you can eat. It's got a pretty, it's a bit of extract. Very. And so, we said we wouldn't be the right people to take that to market. We put that out and someone picked that up.

[00:10:12] That then became the company that is CallaCurb. We had a small royalty arrangement as part of that and they said, actually, we really value the work you can do in clinical science linked to this. We've got some funding. So, we can pay for some of the work, but actually, would you be interested in doing research for equity at the same time? So, we said, great, we'll take some of the cash back in, but then we'll also co-invest with some of our own people and do the clinical research. We've actually got someone who speaks at international conferences alongside their chief executive still today and we own, I think it's technically 4.9% or thereabouts of that company, which is the research for equity. So, it's kind of almost passive. Yeah, correct. Yeah. So, we're not, we're on the cap table because we believe in

[00:10:57] the product and we believe it's something that can come back. So, we look at all those on a case-by-case basis and we've got another one which I can't share the details of, but we've been approached from someone that we didn't start the IP. They've started something. They've gone, actually, we think you could really help us supercharge this. Would you be interested in doing more and take a research for equity position which we're exploring today? Richard, do you want to talk about how we protect IP and still connected? Sure. So, you know, obviously our research is and we want to disseminate information where that's appropriate as widely as possible. For a number of reasons. The name one is for the individual reputation of the researchers but also the organisation as a whole. So, we attract more business and collaborations in. So, we have to balance that when knowledge is briefly shared, like through publications or any other kind of

[00:11:42] mechanism. So, we have a process where both the science leadership and also commercial make those decisions about which pieces of information are best shared widely, which others are more appropriate for protection of IP. So, that's a constant process. Supplementary. You know, New Zealand has got a bit of a problem hasn't it in terms of keeping and owning our specialisations and intellectual property because we grow these firms and they leave or they get bought. And as a result, I think over the last 20, 30, even 50 years, we had a bit of a hollowing out. So, if we're looking at our whole scale of businesses, the medium-sized ones, it's like hollowed out. We got lots of smalls, we got a few bigs and not as many mediums and

[00:12:29] I'm really interested into this and the stickiness of those medium-sized enterprises for the New Zealand economy. So, we own them and we turn them into large businesses. Yeah, look, one probably good thing I'd say is there's been a group of us that have been connecting for, I don't know, five or six months now, trying to exactly discuss this challenge. How do we get not just start-up investment in, but that investment that gets you through the valley of death and get something started. Obviously, you know, New Zealand, we do exceptionally well on the global stage, but we're small. I mean, our consumer base is small, right? And so the opportunity to actually grow companies is quite often international, but obviously we'd like them to still be based in New Zealand

[00:13:14] or directed out of New Zealand. So, how we do that, how we bring investment into New Zealand and actually recognise that New Zealand is the right place to continue to grow these things is definitely a challenge. I don't know, I think we've got the answer, but it is something we're trying to work alongside the system to say how do we do this. Things like apples and kiwi fruit and some of those areas are quite different because we do have a natural advantage. We grow exceptionally good climate reproduction in New Zealand, and so the IP for those does both stay here and move around the world. So, NV, you might know the number, but I think it's 60% of NV or more planting is international, 40% locally, but IP is still held locally. It's controlled. Okay, next question. Thank you. Thanks for being with us. I have a question about our international arrangements,

[00:14:01] particularly the UK and European trade deals, which include four New Zealand, some obligations around our food system and the IP within it and regional indicators. How are we progressing those obligations domestically for our exporters? What's your role in working with exporters to understand those things? Our IP on the world stage for some of those plants that we hold dear to ourselves like Manuka, what's your role in making sure that we uphold our international obligations, but also get the best deal for Kiwis? Yeah, great question. I can maybe lean into some of that. So, one of the good things about things like plant varieties rights is that there's a generally a global approach to those. So, they're filed in multiple countries,

[00:14:46] which helps and, you know, like effectively a patent for plants at the risk of really oversimplifying it. So, the ability to protect those you've seen in China, for example, where things have been pulled back out as a result of illegal plantings. If you look at the, might be a sensitive topic at the moment, but the India Free Trade Agreement, which we're in favour of, that also sort of talks around the opportunities for us to help improve Kiwifruit quality in India. That's not about us necessarily planting our varieties there. If Zespri want to do that over time, we'd support them, but that's a market facing decision. From a science perspective, we think we can help move people out of poverty by helping with quality, with pest and pathogen management, with productivity. So, those are

[00:15:31] the kinds of things that we work quite strongly in. I used to be in the dairy industry and it was the same with the support we gave to some of the Southeast Asian countries on dairy. So, it wasn't around how do we remove the need for New Zealand dairy. It was how do we help support local production at the same time, while recognising the role of New Zealand in there. So, New Zealand's got a pretty good reputation globally on working with others, and EMPAT in particular, if I can call them out, they're an exceptionally good job of connecting in in a non- competitive way. And we've had a lot of experience working overseas with people, but we haven't run into any, at least from a science perspective, any challenges on meeting any of those obligations. And we always sort of partner alongside the New Zealand companies to make sure we're not in conflict. We wouldn't go chasing revenue that isn't aligned with the

[00:16:18] best interests of New Zealand. Ruben Dootson. Thank you, and thank you for the apples. We always like red things. Change the colour. Yeah. Blue one. Blue one. And can it be noted that the majority here did? But coming back to you talked in the beginning about flexibility, and flexibility is sometimes easier when you have certainty around the parameters. So there was a review into copyright, updating our copyright legislation that's stalled somewhat, but what would be what are the top priorities for you that we should be looking at in that space? Yeah, we don't do a lot of

[00:17:03] copyright per se in our space. We'd probably do more in trademark connections. So to give you an example, Envy as an Apple has a plant variety right that at some stage will expire, but the brand won't. And so actually having that protection of the brand when people go, oh, this is an Envy Apple. It's grown a certain way. It tastes a certain way. I know the quality of it because it's what I've been bought and someone else has to go, hey, here's our Apple that's really similar to Envy. It's a copy, but it's not. And I've got to call it Ruben's Apples or whatever it is. It doesn't have that same cut through. And so that's one of the things we've been working alongside a lot of our customers and partners on is how we support that whole end to end value chain of the brand as well as the product. So that's

[00:17:49] everything. We're quite fortunate as an organization where we're one of the few in the world certainly of our scale that goes from kind of pre-farm gate right through to in market. So we support growing offshore. We do a lot of work on quality, on productivity, on work through supply chain, on pest and pathogen management including in some different countries as things are growing around the world. And that's all about that brand reputation. Yeah. So I can add to that just one more point. I think also important to us with across all these types of intellectual property, trademarks, copyright, PVRs, patients is syncing up with international legislation. Where it really gets sticky for us because we export so much actually having those you know, patent laws or PVR laws and sync with each

[00:18:35] other is very, very important because we have to we have to not only deal with the laws that are here but make sure that that our products actually comply and with the laws in the markets we're selling to because that's the way that intellectual property works. So that's really, really important. So the more that you know this group can help synchronize those intellectual property laws the better. And when we fall behind or out of sync as we were with the PVR legislation we were behind getting it back on in line. And it's really important for us in our products. Yeah. Okay. Go to Dr. Vanessa Venig. Thank you very much. So previously when we've had you for review briefings and other things we've discussed the role of AI and science. Do you see any challenges

[00:19:21] in terms of intellectual property within the work streams that you have around intellectual property? We should. Because hello. Hello. I know, yeah. That's why I threw it at you. More apples. We were fortunate enough to be shortlisted on the recent artificial intelligence hunt for a provider for an AI platform to New Zealand. So we're very proud around that. I think AI is always going to be a double-edged sword and it's going to supercharge a lot of our research. It's going to give us insights, obviously, with all the data being able to be fed into that that we'd never had before. But at the same time, it does cause issues,

[00:20:08] especially if we're sharing data locally, internationally. There's the leakage of, you know, potential for leakage of information that might not be appropriate of personal, commercial, whatever. So absolutely. I think we're all slowly kind of moving through this with our eyes fully open in terms of what the opportunities are as well as what the pitfalls might be. Yeah, and I think the opportunity for it to speed up things is probably real. The one that maybe you didn't specifically ask, but the one we're probably keeping more of an eye on is gene technology around the world. So that's the one that we really do risk in New Zealand falling behind on. Yeah. Thank you. A pretty quick final form. I don't know, he leaps a very quick pause. Oh, right. We... Bring us home. So fast. Look, it's a question about,

[00:20:54] I would like us to say something sensible when we report on to the House on this topic of food systems thinking not about your export markets but in the domestic market here in New Zealand. The role of intellectual property in our food system. What is your restriction on whether it's resulting in New Zealanders having fresh, healthy kai that they can afford? Yeah, we can probably only answer from a science perspective. So from our perspective, we want to see people having fresh, healthy food. And I think that's one of the things that New Zealand does exceptionally well. Whether it's affordable or not, that I guess the role we play in that is how do we increase productivity? How do we reduce loss? How do we get more out of a hectare of land that doesn't create more damage to the land as part of that? So that's really where we focus

[00:21:40] but we don't do the pricing. We don't do any of that. We spend a lot of time really in that background of how do we make it more affordable? How do we use less chemicals? How do we protect from pest and pathogens? How much are we doing? Yeah. So we will... Huge. That's a big part of our work. Yeah. We can decide if we want to send some written questions later on. So we are running out of time. I was really tempted to say that why this app is not read all through but after we've been squished in one moment. There's a good reason for that. I was hoping someone would ask because if they read all the way through they don't quite taste the same. So the infosign is well good for you. You can also create some other things. So that's a good balance. Just has to be mature. Great. Thank you so much to all of you for your time. And for answering these questions. Thank you. Thank you. Always enjoy this.

meetings.description: This is "Justice Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Good morning. How are you there, Nick? How are you there, Nick? Having a lot of sounds, isn't it? Yeah. Nick, are you there? Can you turn the camera on? That's the instruction. Oh, good. How are we going? Good morning. Getting there. Yep. No, can you hear me okay now? Yep, we can. We've got 10 minutes for you. Okay. Thank you very much. Good morning, and thank you for this opportunity to speak. I'm Nick Smale, and I'm making the submission on behalf of the West Auckland Licensing Trust Action

[00:00:46] Group, or WALTAG, as we're sometimes called. We are advocates for competition. We are a group of community members. We have a wide range of political perspectives. We're united in the view that the licensing trust monopoly in West Auckland does more harm than good. We have previously attempted a petition, which unfortunately fell agonizingly short with the required number of signatures, and some of us have also participated in recent licensing trust elections under a WALTAG banner. I understand our submission has been read, and so I'll only speak briefly, and I would welcome questions and comments from the committee. All right, very good. Are you there? Are you finished? No, no, still going. Oh, sorry. Yep, but I will be brief. So our submission poses a simple question.

[00:01:33] Licensing trusts expected to be neutral in their own democracy. And we've provided some examples in the written submission, which I won't repeat today, and the purpose in providing those is not to accuse the West Auckland Trust or anyone of wrongdoing. And actually the crux of our submission is that their actions are probably lawful. And the question we're posing is whether that's right and whether that's what Parliament intended. And that really comes off, this submission really comes off the back of two things. And the first was correspondence with the Office of the Order to General. I wrote to them about the trust using public money for market research and PR advice, which had a specific objective to identify key arguments to combat the WALTAG campaign. And that was alongside an apparent allocation of a fighting fund to meet the challenge from WALTAG

[00:02:19] hit on. Now the Order to General took a look at those and said that, A, they don't enforce the electoral act, the local electoral act, and B, any restricting of trust spending would be a policy matter and not one for their office. So basically the message back from the Order to General was that there was nothing to see there. And the second was a recent Ombudsman investigation, which ended up examining whether there was public interest in disclosing pro-monopoly advocacy by the trusts. And that came down to whether they thought that that advocacy was in any way improper. And in the end, after a convoluted discussion, the Ombudsman took the view that even if their activity had electoral consequences, that it was not improper so long as their motive was to benefit their commercial position.

[00:03:07] So it didn't matter that the mechanism was to influence voters, so long as the motive was a commercial work. And so that's what brings me to today's submission. I see things which strike me as inappropriate for a democratically controlled public body. But when I've raised these with the relevant authorities, they say they're okay. And in fact, in many cases, the public won't even get to see what they're up to. And so thus I come back to that question. Did parliament intend that licensing trusts would be some kind of special kind of democracy where the trust itself needn't stay neutral, but instead can campaign in support of continuing their monopoly? It's not what I choose, but I don't get set the rules. And I'm very interested to hear what you think or to get some questions. So thank you. Right, I've got Mr Todd Stevenson. Thanks, Nick. And thanks

[00:03:52] for your submission actually bringing this issue to our attention, the committee's attention, because I think it does actually kind of highlight a bit of an unusual situation, right? Where, again, you're probably pretty clear on ACT's views on licensing trust monopolies, but putting that aside, even if you say that these trusts are doing a good thing, that they're obviously running a commercial, well, yeah, running an alcohol business basically in their area. But then you've got the requirement for them to obviously then get elected members. And so it does seem slightly odd that they, yeah, they can then obviously use funds from that commercial operation to do some of the things that you've highlighted here. Just around the kind of research they were doing into interviews and attitudes, can you just talk

[00:04:42] a bit more about that and what that kind of showed? Yeah, so I mean, they used those funds. Yeah. So they've done, they generally do annual research. And once, shortly after we formed in 2018, I think they put in some questions around the arguments that we were making and asked had people signed our petition, they asked, they took some statements from our website verbatim and tested whether people agreed with those or not, and tested arguments in response to those to say, you know, which ones resonated with the public to inform them, inform their communications activities. So that we found out about that in 2021 through,

[00:05:32] or 2022, I think, around about then anyway, through an eventual Lagoima release. And there was a little bit of publicity around that. And they've actually toned that down a bit in recent years. And I think the questions they're asking now are a little bit less on the nose. But even just recently, I think we've seen in their research that they've just they've just let a little bit of of it out. They're still asking the public whether they've heard of the West Auckland Licensing Trust Action Group and their research. And, you know, it's a strange question for a local authority to be asking about one specific ticket. But that's an interesting comment you just made there. And you said the local authority, are they a local authority? They're not a TLA as such.

[00:06:18] They are under most legislation they are. So under the local electorate, they are local authority's members. Interest Act, they are Lagoima, they are, but they're not under the local Government Act. So they're not covered by the local Government Act. And tell me, there's others around the country, like Southland, I think, are they or just in Bacargal, sorry, in Bacargal, do they have their own bespoke legislation or are they? No, so they're all governed by the sale and supply of alcohol act. There are four left with their monopoly rights. So in Bacargal and Matura, down south, and Portage and Waitakere out west. All of the ones, there's, I think, maybe another 12 or so that don't have monopoly rights. They're still all governed by that same sale and supply of alcohol legislation.

[00:07:03] Yeah, we're obviously contemplated, but I suspect outside the jurisdiction of the bill we're considering. But what's an inquiry? So not, but because how it works and what framework it's operating is a pretty important thing, I think. But anyway, any other questions, members? No, fascinating conversation. No, just make one comment there, Andrew, on your comment about the scope. So, you know, I think when we see, when we look at councils, a lot of that sort of neutrality stuff is bundled up in the proper purpose and around the purpose of local government. So if it was to be addressed through that way, then it would go to sale and supply of alcohol, but it could equally be addressed in the local electoral act.

[00:07:48] So again, do they have their own bespoke legislation or how have they, what's the framework that sits out? Yeah. So in the sale and supply of alcohol act, sections about 300 to about 380 or so have all the information about licensing and community trusts and that sort of is the fundamental. All right. Hey, thank you for your submission this morning and joining us. Been very interesting discussion. Thank you very much, Andrew. Cool. Right. Now, we've got local government officials. Good morning. I see a couple of you there, Swann. Right, we've got

[00:08:35] 10 minutes with you. Thank you. Looking forward to hearing from you. And I'm a Hinoe Ke koutou katoa. Just checking you can hear me. Yes, we can. Thank you for your time this morning. I'm Manik Davidson, Chief Executive at Horo whenua District Council, but here in my capacity as Vice President for Tatora, which is the local government professionals network for Aotearoa, New Zealand. Firstly, apologies from Joe Miller, our president. She's tied up in a council meeting this morning. So you have me instead.

[00:09:21] But I am joined here this morning by Dean Hefford, who's Group Manager, Infrastructure and Services for Mulbrough District Council, but he chairs the Tatora Electrical Reference Group. And we're also joined by Michael Morris, Manager Governance and Legal at Invercargill City Council, and no doubt was listening with interest to that most recent conversation. And he is Deputy Chair of the Tatora Electrical Reference Group. And I'm sure many of you know the infamous Raymond Hurran, who is our Chief Advisor here at Tatora. Look, I'll just make some very brief comments, but then leave time for you to hopefully ask the teams some questions. Our central submission as Tatora is that the New

[00:10:06] Zealand's local electoral system cannot continue in its current trajectory. Declining postal volumes mean that reliance on postal voting is becoming increasingly costly, operationally fragile and logistically complex. In addition, recent experiences demonstrated that the security and integrity of postal voting can be compromised. And we know that that in turn affects public confidence in the electoral process. At the same time, we consider that online voting is not a viable option in the foreseeable future. Persistent security risk and unresolved public trust means that it cannot yet meet the standards

[00:10:52] for democratic elections. And while we absolutely think that those conditions need to be regularly reassessed, we consider that right now electoral reform should focus its energy and attention elsewhere. But the solution is not just as simple as moving from a postal vote to an in-person vote and leaving the rest of the system unchanged. An in-person vote is a fundamentally different voter experience. And our submission as Tatora is that the whole system needs to be considered. Transitioning to it would require careful consideration around representation arrangements, electoral system design, the provision of candidate information, and even seemingly

[00:11:40] technical matters such as the order of candidate names on the ballot paper. And at a time when local government is going through so much government reform, I think what we need to be mindful of is how the community understand the electoral process when they're not quite clear what their representation arrangements might look like under a simplified local government framework. So for this reason, it's our submission that electoral reform must include a redesign of the institutional arrangements that deliver local elections. We absolutely support the centralisation of local elections functions. We think a move to in-person voting strengthens this case and supports a nationally coordinated approach to things like workforce development,

[00:12:28] voter education, and election delivery standards. And just very briefly, part two of our submission therefore advocates for a whole-of-system approach to local electoral reform. New Zealand must consider not only how people vote but why they vote or why they choose not to vote and what structural procedural and social barriers prevent participation. And so in concluding, I want to note that our strength as Taitoata lies in the collective place-based experience that we have as local government practitioners who have delivered elections in real communities under real conditions for many, many election cycles. And it's in that spirit, I encourage the

[00:13:19] committee to think about how Taitoata can assist in offering assistance as you continue to explore what the future might look like. We are happy to answer questions. Well, thank you. 29 recommendations, a fair number of them. We've got Mr Roseborn, who might have got a question for you. Thank you, Chair. You suggest a six-yearly review of online voting suitability. I'm just wondering why you landed on six years and also who should lead that review and how it's forenees what should be made public. I'll defer to Raymond to speak to the detail on this one. Well, technology's moving quickly, Mr Roseborn. I think six years is kind of a bit of a halfway

[00:14:13] house. It takes account of the electoral cycle being three. It kind of allows for some period of time for technology to develop while not leaving so much time that well, not keeping in the review rolling reasonably frequently. As to who, I think that needs to be something that's done by DIA, among other things. They are working on systems for identity management at the moment. The security agencies, GCSB and ZSIS, the local government sector and the

[00:14:59] Ministry of Justice is keepers of the central government electoral system. And the reason I say that is if you're looking there at some point someone is going to ask why we're not also looking at its suitability for parliamentary elections. Are there any other players we think should be involved? Mr Chairman, just the recommendation from the GCSB was that they needed a long lead in time to do the security reviews and investigations. So they didn't want us to have too short a timeframe between reviews. So that was part of the reasoning for the six yearly role as well. All right. Thank you for explaining that. I've got a question from Hon Dr Duncan Webb. Thank you, Mr Chair. My question is a more broad question.

[00:15:49] There does seem to be a degree of dissatisfaction with local government and elected representatives. And I guess I'm interested in whether you track essentially trust or confidence in local government and whether the lack of enthusiasm for voting and a lack of trust in how the voting system works has any bearing on that. Thank you for the question. I'd encourage the committee to dig deep at a place-based level, to think about in your community

[00:16:37] the things that you touch and feel and see every day that is adding value through the incredible work that local government does. I think we all accept that there is a challenge around trusting confidence of government and that that is about local and central government because that's about the services and the experience all New Zealanders have. And I don't know if our communities necessarily make a distinction every day between what local government delivers and what central government delivers. I know that every council is required to be tracking satisfaction, voice of our rate payer and certainly I know with it from a Haurafenoa District Council perspective we absolutely

[00:17:27] track trust and confidence and the net promoter score is often a methodology used across councils and I'm not sure if you've heard already from local government New Zealand but I suspect when you do hear from local government New Zealand one of the things they could talk about is the reputational survey work that was done a few years ago that tells a story about the need for community to better understand what local government does and I'm sure you will all agree that that part of the solution to that is about central and local government working together to figure out how we can continue to deliver efficient and effective services to our community at place through partnership. All right and now I've got another question from Celia Wade Brown.

[00:18:16] Completely different area. I'm interested in the having that central administration to the local elections and how you see the cost sharing between central government and local government if the Electoral Commission were to actually run the elections. I'll be you make a point in your submission that there's quite a variation between I think it's your submission might be the background paper there's quite a variation in how much different councils spend per capita and so how would you make a sort of a fair play there? Yeah I think that it's a really good practical example of how

[00:19:01] the financial constraints that councils are operating in where we need to consider what unintended consequences of like a ratescaping regime might look like because it could actually limit councils ability to invest in funding this stuff well. I think the opportunity if there is a centralised system is for it to ensure that the small councils are not bearing the cost in the same way that larger councils would be and surely there are some efficiencies through a centralised system from both central and local government electoral systems and so I don't think we're suggesting local government shouldn't pay their share but I think that there would need to be some

[00:19:50] sound methodology to ensure small communities were not disadvantaged. If I look at that not just we'd also expect to have some representation around the commission to input into the design of of the invite promotion to input into the charging methodologies and so on. I just also add that one other I accept the point the member makes one of the key benefits of centralising things is that that there would be a degree of consistency and approach across local authorities as to for example what promotion is done and so on. All right hey thank you for your submission we have heard from

[00:20:36] local government so that question before but thank you very helpful discussion this morning for your time. Thank you. Right we're going to go back into privacy.

meetings.title: Social Services and Community Committee
meetings.description: This is "Social Services and Community Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Hon, Minister, Member, Select Committee, House of Representatives. Good afternoon and welcome to the Social Services and the Exli Committee.

[00:00:52] So we have the Ministry of Sport, Pacific Peoples, this afternoon too, give us some information regarding that long-term insights briefing. And I think we have Jordan and Bernadette with us. We've got a 45-minute here. Time is yours if you want to leave some time towards the end for questions, please, for what we're going to do so. Fafte Lava and thank you for the opportunity to appear before you today. Firstly, if I can just introduce Bernadette Scanlon, our Deputy Secretary of Policy and Insights, who will be responding with me today. And if you just bear with me, I just have some opening comments, which will take about 10 minutes. So can I just begin by acknowledging the enduring kinship ties that bind Māori and Pacific peoples in this nation? These are not new

[00:01:41] connections formed by migration alone. They are ancient relationships shaped by navigation, shared cosmologies, and here in New Zealand, as early as the 1920s and 1930s, by the long-standing role of iwi in welcoming and hosting and looking after Pacific families as they made their homes on these shores and within the boundaries of various iwi around the country. There's no other country in the world that can claim this strong kinship link with Pacific nations. Through the development of this briefing, we heard many stories of iwi exercising their stewardship role, extending their resources, their manaki tanga, and their services to

[00:02:31] ensure Pacific peoples and families were cared for. These stories matter because they remind us that Pacific settlement in New Zealand has always been relational, grounded in whakapapa, place, and mutual responsibility. Over generations, these relationships have deepened into marriages, shared communities, shared churches, shared schools, shared struggles, and increasingly shared opportunities have produced a growing cohort of people who claim rightly and proudly kinship ties to both Māori and Pacific worlds. This is not a marginal phenomenon. It's a distinctive and consequential demographic shift

[00:03:18] underway in New Zealand. The long-term insights briefing deliberately places a ring around this population, not because they are separate, but because ethnic classification can render them statistically invisible, even as the influence becomes increasingly visible in our communities, our classrooms, and our workplaces. If we want good long-term policy, we need to be honest about who's coming through the gate. And what the data is telling us is striking. The average age of people who identify with both Māori and Pacific whakapapa is only 15 years of age. It compares with Māori at 26 years of age and Pacifica at 24 years of age, and then non-Māori and non-Pacific,

[00:04:11] 38 years of age. So the Māori Pacific demographic is half the age of the non-Māori, non-Pacific population. In demographic terms, this is not simply a young population. It's a very young population. Many are children now and nearly all are New Zealand's future workforce. We shouldn't be surprised that they are predominantly based in our major urban areas. Auckland, most prominently, but also Wellington, Christchurch, and Dunedin, while at the same time establishing growing footprints in rural and regional areas, such as Northland, Waikato, South Canterbury, Otago, and

[00:04:58] in Bikago. This combination matters. It positions this cohort at the intersection of both our urban productivity engines and our regional revitalisation efforts. So what does this mean for New Zealand's future productivity and contribution to the workforce? Firstly, quite simply, they will be essential. As our broader population ages, labour force growth will increasingly depend on Māori and Pacific peoples and particularly on those with dual whakapapa who are coming of age in significant numbers. This group will shoulder a growing share of workforce participation, the tax base, the care economy, and the creative industries. The question is not whether they will

[00:05:48] contribute, but whether our systems will be ready to support them to contribute at their full potential. Secondly, this cohort brings unique strengths that are highly aligned with the future of work. Many are inherently biocultural or multicultural navigators. They are custom to operating across worlds, to our Māori and Pacific worlds, home and institution, whenua and city. In the labour market, increasingly shaped by collaboration, relational skills, adaptability, and cross-cultural competence, these capabilities are not nice to have. They are productivity assets. But, and this is where the briefing is deliberately honest, there are also future

[00:06:37] challenges if we get this wrong. We are already seeing persistent disparities in educational attainment, housing security, health outcomes, and exposure to the justice system for Māori and Pacific peoples. If policy settings fail to recognise the compound realities of young people with dual identity, we risk reproducing those inequities into the next generation at considerable social and fiscal cost. There is also a classification challenge. When people are required to choose a single ethnicity, the lived experience of people can be flattened, and the policy responses can miss the mark. Education pathways, employment services, and wellbeing supports

[00:07:25] are often designed for one identity or another, when the reality for many of these young people is often both and sometimes neither comfortably. Yet, the opportunities are just as real as the risks. We can already see amazing glimpses of what this kind of partnership can look like in practice. The relationship between the Central Pacific Collective and Ngāti Taurangatira provides a tangible example through the partnership initiatives such as our whāne, where mana whenua have made available their land for a home ownership approach focused on the delivery of warm, healthy, long-term homes, homes designed for Pacific people to put down their roots,

[00:08:12] rather than cycle through insecurity. These are not homes built in isolation. They are deliberately located in schools, public transport, health services, and community supports, recognising that stable housing, access to education, and proximity to care are deeply interconnected. Ngāti Taurangatira's role as mana whenua brings a strong place-based lens and intergenerational stewardship, while the Central Pacific Collective contributes deep Pacific-led insight into whānau needs, affordability pressures, and cultural patterns of living. The project also takes an inclusive social value approach to delivery, strengthening access to employment and business

[00:09:00] opportunities, and helping ensure the economic benefits of development are shared with the community's most affected by housing need. This approach supports capabilities, skills, and long-term resilience by remaining open, transparent, and focused on achieving the best outcomes for whānau communities and the wider system. Together, this approach demonstrates what is possible when iwi leadership and resources and Pacific community capability are aligned, creating housing that supports well-being, educational continuity, and workforce participation over the long term, rather than short-term fixes. For many young people, with dual Māori and Pacific whakapapa, iwi is really part of their lived reality through

[00:09:51] whenua, marae, education, faith, and whānau collections. Creating space for iwi to engage more closely in conversations about education pathways, employment, well-being, and place-based development offers an opportunity to ground national policy and local knowledge, cultural continuity, and intergenerational wisdom. Just as importantly, this cohort offers New Zealand a chance to foster greater understanding of cross-out communities. Younger people who move with confidence between different worlds have the potential to act in a global sense as translators and bridge builders, strengthening social cohesion, challenging false binaries, and modelling a more

[00:10:38] relational way of being a global citizen. Supporting this does not dilute identity, it deepens it, positioning this briefing not simply as a reflection on Pacific futures, but as a statement of New Zealand's future, the workforce we are becoming, and our readiness to thrive in an increasingly global and diverse economy. So we offer this briefing to the committee and that spirit as an invitation to think long, think relationally, and to work alongside iwi communities and young people themselves as we lay the foundation for New Zealand's communities of tomorrow. Thank you so much for listening and Bernard Edge and I welcome questions. Questions? Members, I might kick off. There's a sort of comments,

[00:11:31] question about the bridges in your point. You may be able to get the connections between Māori and Pacifica. Initially I was lucky enough to visit the Aotearoa village about two years ago and I won and my colleague, Hon. Ōkā Genes Alisa, spoke to me and in initial comments they stopped recently a translation or the translator went to translate. I was speaking in Māori or Māori or Māori if I can't remember exactly, but they stopped the translator and said no no we understand and it was just a fascinating connection, such a long way between New Zealand and Taiwan but it did lead me to look more closely into the Austronesian

[00:12:19] which grows all across the Pacific and the peoples that connects all of us and obviously New Zealand was the last major landmass to be settled but it's this Pacific going peoples connected through language and culture right across the Pacific. So I just a comment and I think it's important my first to understand and reflect on as we connect to the blue continent. Thank you. Thank you for that. I'm particularly interested in rangatahi and unemployment. Is there anything in particular you can talk to around education, employment, pathways into employment

[00:13:08] to that rangatahi specifically? One of the things that was strikingly obvious to us is how young people see themselves as part of the solution, not part of the problem and this piece in particular was designed to bring visibility to the two sides of the coin. Firstly that there is a risk if we don't do anything and secondly the huge opportunity that comes with being more purposeful about how we design the policies. So the main thing here

[00:13:58] is to bring visibility because they told us directly we are not the problems to be fixed. We are the partners in the solution. So this is a commitment we have to the rangatahi. And in terms of those solutions that they might be suggesting do you have any more that you can add to that? I think it's easy for us to focus solely on government responsibility and I'll come to that. The thing that I found very interesting was that when our rangatahi if they're enrolled with the iwi the iwi collects their data as rangatahi Maori.

[00:14:49] So when we met with iwi they were like oh but they're Maori. So even for iwi to say oh maybe we now need to collect more information because we see everyone as Maori and we know that they're Pacific but they're Maori first here. So in the first instance for the iwi that we talked to coming to this understanding that the rangatahi have multiple identities that they may feel a strong affinity for is really important. In terms of the second part government's responsibility if agencies can't see these people then it's very hard to develop solutions for it.

[00:15:37] So in the first instance while this is a think piece to encourage public conversations about long-term issues it is also something that we will socialise with other agencies so that they understand that this dynamic does exist and look to make use of those opportunities.
