It's a joy to hear when you talk about dual hakapa those things they're quite a it's not a common statement you'll hear in a select committee not when our shoulders are soft you know usually it's something else rolling up in it and maybe a little bit more of a contingent issue but

[00:16:24] here to hear it in such a way that you can see the future coming you know it's there post and ages and I noticed when you talk and I was a little bit sort of 12 and 26 and 38 and what that is setting us up for is it is a very young population so how do we organise how do we inspire how do we resource and then get out of their way those sorts of things I I guess I that that has that's a heightened interest for me and then to that end I what are you finding or seeing or feeling when you from Ngāti Tua down the scene and then if you reflect up to the

[00:17:10] Tāi Tokero so you're talking right up and now where Willow and I are from are you finding the same sorts of things from an iwi or an hapū type engagement are you finding those things are happening further north what we found is people migrate to different areas of the country for different reasons the challenge that we heard from participants is they say they're too Māori for their Polynesian peers and too Polynesian for their Māori peers and it can make them feel left out so I guess the visibility issue I can't reinforce how important that is to this cohort and it's important for all the reasons that we

[00:18:04] have discussed around productivity and contribution to our tax base when they can thrive going forward thank you thank you I'm particularly interested in your insight number two which is around the digital landscape and I just wonder what currently are the barriers to you know the Māori Pacific children in getting into that space and developing those skills I'm going to give an opportunity to Bernadette to talk but I think in the first instance it's different

[00:18:50] depending how old you are so for our older generation often it's a digital divide having access to computers knowing how to use computers with our younger generation their ability to access some kind of device is much greater I'll just see our report findings showed that there's still an access barrier as well as capability up if needed there is a difference between being a digital native to being digital literature and so just because you have access to cell phones and you can use it on a daily basis doesn't mean you're going to be able to cloud your future be able to utilise AI to the optimal state so it's about

[00:19:35] trying to provide the access provide the capability uplift and there is a desire for our Māori Pacific youth to have that they want to be self-determinant they want to be able to upskill and actually be digitally literate which I think is amazing and we need to foster I just thought kids were born for knowledge to understand yeah I might supplement actually because we're sort of at a massive inflection point in terms of technology right now we're in it and the ability to code isn't necessarily required anymore but the ability to get the right prompts to an AI to do the coding for you is but it's also that's also language and culture can help inform that

[00:20:22] so there's there's the data sovereignty piece which is probably increasingly important but also challenging because to access these tools which are designed offshore and and the resources can be built offshore don't naturally take no significant account so this is a I'm not expecting any any you know answers that that sort of console all these things is everyone's grappling with this globally honestly the interest in what your insights about are about how young people can not just be consumers but prosumers or producers and this is an incredible affliction point which everyone's grappling with here and and around the world yeah of course it insight who also talks about the specific data sovereignty and money data sovereignty and a key aspect of that is

[00:21:09] take yeah doing exactly what you said taking ownership to develop some of the tools because if we don't we're going to have natural biases embedded in whatever we are adopting in New Zealand so making sure that you've got that diversity of thought in the not just the application but in terms of the actual development and processing of and the manufacturing of these products that we're going to be utilizing is vital to make sure that we are utilizing it best for our people and having that diversity in the development means you remove those biases and if I can just add from personal experience the people developing our policies have huge wisdom and insight and

[00:21:54] experience and unfortunately with that comes a lot of risk averseness and so you know when you're dealing with 15 year olds their ability to find solutions for themselves is they call it their super power and if I think of my own children for example I saw a message pop up in my mail and it's some app to stealth writing or something like this so I need to have a conversation with my son about that because obviously he's needing to write something and knows that that the system will be better so how then do we keep up with our young people and with technology when we're actually

[00:22:42] on a different way sometimes and we come with a lot of wisdom and experience that may prevent us from making the decisions in time for it to have a policy impact and that age difference is so important for us to be aware of as agencies so that we can be mindful of it. Might ask one more something and I don't know if I don't know if another's up but just sort of circling back to my first comment really is just in this sort of incredible acceleration technology and trying to ensure that these are New Zealand's focus and you know and our population groups here you know here and their data sovereignty is important but very challenging

[00:23:33] one to try with how quickly technology is changing but is there an option and or is there any thinking around trying to include you know the Austronesian grouping of a better term across the Asia Pacific you know who have a lot of similar interests and concerns around exactly the same thing. The important challenge into that but no doubt there's opportunity to embrace that as well. We have one initiative at the moment the game on initiative you'll be you might be surprised to know that New Zealand is you know almost world leaders in the creation of games in the gaming industry and we have a project at the moment that is working with the gaming industry

[00:24:20] with some NGOs and with us to provide internship opportunities for young people to learn about the opportunities because they are so creative and so when you give them the opportunity to focus that creativity into an industry like gaming then we think that this is one of the pathways that we can use. We haven't looked more broadly at the moment but New Zealand is great at a lot of these areas that we don't amplify that often how good we are in some of these spaces. And we have the I think the single biggest population groups of Paul Inzion

[00:25:07] who was on the planet right and so we have we have a superpower in terms of that connectivity to the Pacific. I think the superpower is old too like it's super old like you referred to in your open statement so I think it's a revitalised energy really and but anyway so fiberfully yes yes so you know that group and so I you know if we're when we're trying to really understand more in this regard anyway that's at this committee having people that are right on that line doing those things young vibrant and the planned and they've got an idea and I ran into them the other day

[00:25:58] at the airport and they were like running into me and they were just so full of youthful smart driven people yeah these two young stunning women so I mean I just wanted to have you have a group or a firm or organization that you're connected with and would you recommend that we'd bring them in here so they could talk to us about what they think is good a good future going ahead. If I may if I just go back to your first point what are the beautiful things about dealing with this particular cohort is how respectful they are of Matua so they look at age

[00:26:45] with a respectful lens and this is a very different characteristic when dealing with non Māori non Pacific so it is about they embrace wisdom but they also know that because they help their parents and their grandparents that there's a big gap between what they know and so I just wanted to acknowledge the deep respect that they have for whakapapa and kinship. In terms of Fibre Whale they do wonderful work in our community and I've spoken with them a number of times and I really want to support their desire to have a

[00:27:33] really strong business offering so they do a lot of work in the community that offer a lot of and they provide access to laptops and they do AI training they also don't want to give away their expertise for free and they've been really clear on that which is absolutely what they should do so we will continue to navigate that space because as you were aware there are various procurement roles and things like that that we have to navigate with them. Thank you. New question and will it get? Thank you. Sorry I might have missed it in your corridor earlier and in the report but of that cohort have you identified the tribal affiliations,

[00:28:21] the Iwi affiliations? Not all of them. Have you kind of got a picture of? Yes it's complicated, far more complicated so Iwi don't capture all although now they're very interested the Iwi that we talk to. The second is depending on who the dominant person is in the household some of the and it goes back to I'm too Māori for my Polynesian peers and too Polynesian for my Māori peers there's a lot of interest in reconnecting those whakapapa ties and then there's some that are really clear they know who they're iwi is and they proudly manage both so it's not as

[00:29:12] straightforward as that. Because you did identify where they sort of concentrated and of course I think of being Ngāpuhi and one in five Ngāpuhi so I'm assuming there's going to be a lot of them I mean if Ka'a Nau Sepuloni I suppose person was here she would say she has these mokopuna both Pasifika and Māori and Ngāpuhi by the way but I also think like I organised a whānau reunion along with my cousin back in 2006 and to our surprise half of the whare was full of Tongan Māori relations that we didn't actually know existed until the whānau reunion and then you find out

[00:29:59] ancestor went to Tonga and had five wives and there are a whole lot of you know descendants from that life living primarily in South Auckland and first time really making that connection back to the Marae so I was interested in if through census and if we're losing census how do we try and capture this data and those connections and then support that reconnection to and strengthening when we know identity security that sense of identity can make such a huge difference yeah so just wondering because it comes through in the report so much so I'm like what are the mechanisms

[00:30:45] I'll get Bernadette to talk to the data but if I can just say that one of the drivers that we found in doing this work is that when Pacific people marry outside of a Pacific culture they marry a Māori first so that's the dynamic at the moment it may change in the future but at the up until the point that we did this Pacific Mac people marrying outside Pacific will more likely marry a Māori next so this was one of the foundational questions when we decided to embark on this work is what does this look like now we know it's 94,000 people approximately and I think one of the chemists just from the and this altarp is how invisible

[00:31:35] this dual population is within data within existing research and that's primarily because the mechanisms we are collecting using data in the sense that whether it be prioritised ethnicity or that you have to in your interactions with any government agencies all the service providers you have to opt for one ethnicity that's diluting the multi ethnicity component and so when you start asking about specificities or sub ethnicity with tribes or hapu it's just going to get more and more diluted from a from an aggregate view it's already yeah for a data and we are conscious that yeah with moving to a new sense and that's with the existing census by the way like moving to a new census space it's going to make it

[00:32:20] put pressures on still how we collect ethnicity data and it's something that we are often advocating for to ensure that we have good strong ethnicity data so that we can understand identity but also understand drivers behind need as well I was particularly interested in a lot of the comments around the education system and we've just had the Waitangi Tribunal inquiring into changes to the curriculum both the NZC curriculum and Te Maroutanga and I can see some of the same issues that are being raised there coming up for Pasifika children so for Māori 97 percent of

[00:33:05] Tamariki Māori in English medium education how important it is to have within that curriculum and within that system that sense of identity being able to see themselves and what they're being taught and yeah the learning that's happening there it also makes me think about from our ECE level and I know that we have some Pasifika ECE we are oversubscribed in Kohanga Reo and a need for that to grow and you're talking about a really young demographic here is there more that you could expand on in terms of that education

[00:33:51] not only in the language that they say they want to be learning in both languages but also through into the compulsory education sector in the first instance we'd probably use this document to talk about some of the perspectives with the Ministry of Education what I have been excited by recently and I don't know if you've had the opportunity to visit the Sisters United Academy this is a charter school operating out of the Alexandra Raceway already it has over a hundred women in years nine and year 10 formative years where they are led by Pasifika teachers Pasifika Kopapa

[00:34:47] and the opening was they've worked on the opening for the first term as well as the academic achievements but the strength of their identity was really significant and the safety and pastoral care that was offered that was visible between the teachers and their students and made me feel a little bit jealous that I didn't get the opportunity to participate in an education program like that myself not that my parents would have let me do that they probably would have seen it as too too much performance and not enough academic

[00:35:36] but for this cohort I could see you know really visible strong sense of identity and there is I would have no doubt that this would translate into academic achievement so I can't answer the wider question but to point you to places where you see this thriving and I would recommend a visit to the Sisters United Academy and I suppose that highlights perhaps an issue with our English medium schooling system where that isn't provided yet and so you have a community looking at either a charter school

[00:36:26] or a special character school to be able to respond to that need with the early childhood education it's complex because early childhood education and language nests often the focus is on preservation of the language and that's amazing and sometimes the early childhood education that comes secondary to the language so it offers a place for often our matua to find employment and to speak the language but then they put all the kids to sleep at the same time and they may have practices that wouldn't be seen by some

[00:37:17] as being up with early childhood good early childhood education practice conversely then you get a centre that has really good early childhood education absolutely no ability to harness the language and I think this will be a policy dilemma for us as parents needs in some areas the cohort that we're talking about really parents the 15 year olds the 16 year olds they're already parents and this is not to judge them often they come from parents who I interviewed some and was also I would be the same age as your parent and then it dawned on me that I'm the

[00:38:05] same age as their grandparents and you know bordering on great grandparents so um these are people that want their children to align to a strong identity and one that accommodates for the very diverse needs of our community my name um I do think there's a desire for culturally appropriate responses in education it's just going to be a bit more difficult because it's not one culture it's not Maori or Pacific it is morning and Pacific so how when you're talking about bilingualism how do you incorporate that in schools how do you incorporate which values so it's just a discussion that we will bring attention to our counterparts in education I suppose to build on this it's not just what will they say to two cultures but it's

[00:38:57] these multiple cultures multiple languages and the resourcing that's right there's a quote in the report it says I don't want to just identify one ethnicity what about the other seven it's all right pretty well the mind I have as well just it's it is a strength like I think countries or jurisdictions that embrace cross-cultural capability actually as a strength research shows that there is a strong correlation to greater innovation greater trust greater productivity so it's more about how we embrace it as a positive thing oh yeah I can see it amongst our athletes with John Whakapapa many of them are influencers you know and you can just see

[00:39:46] it they are absolutely thriving in all of their Whakapapa beautiful to see yeah yeah I think we've seen of um the questioning phase is sort of drawing to a close but any other sort of key points that we haven't touched on in the oral here and I'd like to emphasise thank you for your openness to listening to our long-term insights briefing and the opportunity to bring some of that to life oh sorry I do have one more go for it sorry we're ready um we have this briefing this morning from the Helen Clark Foundation and I know you'll have talked about a particularly young cohort but they were talking about a lot of the challenges that we've got with our ageing population and given my own experience with

[00:40:36] whanau Whakapapa and this is you know back to four greats grandparents so there are some quite elderly ones in that um they they talked about you know the provision of care um and what Māori and tend to not go into um uh retirement villages rest homes they can be more in community in whanau was there anything that you had around that or did you just focus on the younger group I think um in relation to that report there are two things that really stand out is really important the drop in trust in government um and how do we work towards that and the issues around social cohesion which do include the intergenerational the transfer of knowledge and care

[00:41:26] um we know that Pacifica and Māori tend to look after their elderly if they stop doing that there's going to be a significant cost to our country um and largely because it's not these people that are um going to be elderly I have a supplementary that's all so one of the things that we talked about and was emphasised in that report was that we need a lot more small houses for elderly people and it was something I thought about at the time was isn't that a little bit culturally narrow like a lot of our communities actually share housing and it is very lonely being in all these little houses and so when we're planning

[00:42:19] in that way for housing for the elderly do you think we have to be cognizant of a whole lot of cultures which have kind of got it right and they are more um they do actually need bigger spaces and more connection with each other and they need to be intergenerational that's such a big question I'm sorry for one minute it's been an interesting day yeah yes and I'm reluctant to answer it because I will give an answer that doesn't do justice to the complexity of the issue yeah um particularly because of the dynamics of the community

[00:43:06] so I'd suppose then from us something to pick up from those two reports is that connection around it and I agree with Helen I was kind of like oh I'm smaller single but what about intergenerational and so that we're actually talking about larger and I know that some of our state houses have been built and provide for that so um they have their own space but they're part of a a bigger one but yeah how do where is that need how do we plan for that what does that spread look like across the country so maybe something to look into how this roof that feeds into that strategy that needs to be developed if you get a chance I would recommend having a look at the alfade alfade project which has been based and designed

[00:43:54] around a Pacific village concept and so the houses are designed with multiple adaptable spaces and it is very much like a village with it with a shared family space in Poteira the schools also are like that isn't it the college is based around a Bali system might I add just one point just in relation to universal solutions don't apply they're not useful in general so to be more targeted around service design or whatever you're offering in terms of the production of houses um you do need to take into account um just how people behave and that's driven by ethnicities and it's driven by a dual ethnicity here so definitely one size fits all would not um be an effective way to design a solution

[00:44:42] no one last question for me no yes just and it's just probably um from what Willow Jean was saying in the select committee briefing that we had this morning did in any of your conversations with this young cohort did they talk about their I mean you talked about embracing wisdom and I'm quite sure that is so did they talk about their old ones did they talk about how they saw their old ones and what do they think of their old ones you know I yeah did they talk about them did they see what um what they envisage for their old ones their older far now older probably um in some instances and I can't uh recall anything specifically but

[00:45:38] I guess it was more the way of knowing that they didn't separate themselves out here but um they're likely and what we can do is is have a look and come in because I I guess what I want to say to that is that in in the discovery of more that you discover when there's airing is that they are a critical um uh element to their tupuna so if they're mokopuna the word tupuna which is that punna punna which is a spring eh so they are they are so intrinsically connected they are the source that keeps them alive because my mokopuna I'm going to stay alive as long as I possibly can because they will keep you know there's that practical engagement which is when we're talking

[00:46:27] about intergenerational I didn't even know there was a word I know intergenerational is the word now and it's very popular but we just called it whanaui it was an ordinary way of living so I and I wondered if our young ones recognize talk and see them in that space because their job will be to keep people my own life as long as possible and all that thank you thank you sorry miss thank you very very very well said um thanks um I appreciate it thanks for you and your team saw it thank you thank you very much

meetings.title: Health Committee
meetings.description: This is "Health Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] We will let you start off with some introductory comments and then we can keep those to several minutes. We can go into questions from others. Thank you. Kia ora. Thank you very much. As we've appeared in front of you a couple of times in the last six months, my intention is really in my opinion remarks to briefly bring to your attention a few highlights and obviously leave the rest of the time for questions. Before I do that, I want to take the opportunity to introduce you as with me. So on my left, Deputy Commissioner Dr Vanessa Corrill, Vanessa's responsibility is for the operations team and she has the

[00:00:47] delegations for mental health and addictions and prisoner complaints. On my right, Rose Wall groans as the Deputy Commissioner for Disability and also has the statutory delegations for decision-making and maternity health as well as mesh complaints and for the past 10 months, Rose has also been managing the aged care files. Natata Davidson in the rear is our Chief Advisor and is often starter and will help us with some of the tricky questions when they arise. So just turning to some of those highlights. Firstly, clearing the backlog of open complaints and proving the timeliness of our process has been our focus particularly in the context of funding reduction. We've streamlined our process

[00:01:35] considerably and in 24-25 we increased our number of closures by 40%. Over the past 15 months we have reduced our number of open complaints, so our backlog by 50%, and we have a clear backlog plan to close the older files within our system. We are consistently closing more complaints than we have received for the first time since 2015 with a clearance rate currently of about 117%. This has allowed us to reduce the number of files aged over two years by 52% in the past 15 months. Approximately 80% of our complaints are closed in six months and current forecast suggests that by the end of 2026 we'll be in the position to close incoming

[00:02:23] complaints requiring a formal investigation within 18 to 24 months which we consider to be an appropriate time frame given the complexity of those complaints and given that that is a quasi-judicial process. The Committee will be pleased to hear that on the 30th of March we implemented our new digital case management system. Our staff, given it's only been four weeks, are still in the process of learning to navigate that system that we anticipate that it will embed and deliver further efficiencies. I'm very proud of my team for achieving these results in the context of ongoing increase in complaints volume and the reduced funding this year. Currently complaints volumes are sitting

[00:03:11] around about 12% higher than the previous year. Complaints tend to reflect people's concerns around constrained and delayed access to care, poor communication in the context of those delays and difficulties navigating a constrained and complex system. In terms of our role in quality improvement in 24-25 we made 809 recommendations with a 91% compliance rate. As the Committee may be aware our previous aged care commissioner resigned in August 2025. Erin James has recently been appointed to that position in early April so she's only a few weeks into the role and we'll be working closely with Erin to establish her priorities and recommendations for the

[00:04:00] sector to improve the quality of care for older people. I've taken note of the Committee's recent reports into HDC's resourcing and the petition into the establishment of a patient safety commissioner. I accept that unexpected increases in complaints volume in the context of constrained resources have had an impact on our ability to undertake rapid systemic inquiries and I acknowledge the select Committee's concerns about this. Such inquiries are an ongoing focus for me and as we clear our backlog I will be exploring ways in which we can do this more effectively within the current resources. In the meantime HDC has worked hard to leverage the things and complaints

[00:04:45] for quality improvement through the publication of the matter reports. These reports seek to amplify consumer voice and make associated recommendations for improvement. We have recently released a report on the experience of disabled people in the context of residential care settings. We're about to release another report on the experience of disabled people in the health sector and we have focused on the experience of disabled people noting the inequities that that population does suffer as well as the fact that we have a unique jurisdiction and that we cover both the health and disability sectors and we've been very pleased by the response of the relevant agencies to our recommendations from those reports. Happy to talk

[00:05:33] more about those as we go through. And finally an ongoing focus for me is on ensuring the sustainability of the community. I'm going to focus for me is on ensuring the sustainability of the advocacy service. Under the HDC Act HDC through its director of advocacy is required to contract on behalf of the Crown an advocacy service. The contract currently sits with the National Advocacy Trust. The advocacy service plays an important role in supporting complainants to resolve their concerns. They also reduce demand on provider complaints processes and resolve low risk complaints so that HDC resources can be focused on those complaints that require our intervention. However, static funding of that service

[00:06:21] and that's funded through our baseline has reduced in recent years and sorry the size of that has caused the size of the service to reduce in recent years and currently we're concerned about the multiple geographical gaps in that service. So I'll be looking in the coming year to see how we can address those concerns. That concludes my opening remarks and I'm happy to take any questions. Thank you very much for those open remarks. Can I? Thank you. I've just got a question regarding your recommendations and the following of them or compliance with them. How many sort of, how many complaints feed into a recommendation? Have you got some situations where you've had one

[00:07:07] complaint that's led to a recommendation? Have you got ones that have got a thousand complaints? What's the sort of measures on that? And then I've got a follow-up question. Okay, so whenever we receive a complaint, particularly if it comes into our system, so it tends to be that the more serious complaints or complaints on which we need to seek information, we will always consider the potential for quality improvement from those complaints. So on individual complaints, we can make both educational comments, suggestions for improvement and all recommendations. So recommendations can either be made on those complaints that don't proceed through to a formal investigation. If we see a cluster of complaints, we often leverage previous complaints where recommendations have been made

[00:07:55] to re-emphasise existing recommendations. And for example in our thematic reports, we were looking at complaints trends. We use the complaints trends to identify appropriate recommendations and make them to the appropriate agencies. I also want to make clear that we analyse the responses to our recommendations very closely. And if we're not satisfied that they've been complied with, we will engage with the provider to make sure that the risk to which those recommendations were directed have been appropriately mitigated. That's interesting. So you've got actually more scales than just making a recommendation. Have you got a rip in this report? I didn't see a rip. So educational comment measure of how many of those you've put out versus how many, what was it,

[00:08:43] suggestions for improvement? Is there a measure of that? I don't have the exact numbers on that. I'm turning to my chief advisor because she is. Roughly. So roughly we make around about 800 recommendations a year, which are the ones we follow up on. And then there's probably an additional, I would say, around 300 complaints upon which we make what we call educational comment, which is where we're making one that you could reflect on your communication. And is there a suggestion slash comment or a suggestion another scale above? Suggestion is actually another scale. How many levels do you get? Again, I think there's probably encapsulated in the educational comments, but there is certainly some data that we could follow up. And then the next question that was once you've made the recommendation that

[00:09:31] you've got 91% compliance, you've said you've made the talk to the agencies. I mean, you guys are kind of the police force in a way. Who's the prosecuting agency? Sorry, can you... Well, you guys are kind of the police force. You've found the thing and said this is a recommendation that we often do and 91% are followed from that 9% that aren't. You've got in here about some of the steps you have taken. So, where's the teeth in that? Does it go? Does it have to get political enforcement? Can you make issues go to the ministry that then come down from another direction? What do you do at that point? I think it's really important to understand the reasons why recommendations might not be complied with. So, in the first instance there may be practical reasons. Things may have changed since we made the

[00:10:19] recommendation, which make it more difficult for the provider to complete the recommendation that we've suggested. In those cases, as I said, we engage with the providers to see whether there needs to be modification to the recommendation or in the terms of recommendation. For those complaints where we don't receive a response, for example, we have the option of naming the provider, which is taken quite seriously by providers, and we can escalate to the regulatory bodies. So, for example, if they're a registered provider, we can take the non-compliance recommendations to the regulatory authority who can take action on that. Who's that? So, for example, if it was a doctor, we could raise a non-compliance with recommendations to the medical council. Thank you. Can I go to a primary? Oh, you've got us up? Yes, sure.

[00:11:07] Can you, I see in the report, 809 was the number of recommendations made, and a 91% response rate for the year 24-25 years in your annual report. So, on the ones that are not followed, how often would you identify that the regulatory authority needs to, responsible authority needs to be contacted about a practitioner? I'm just trying to think of the, so we know that of the 66, those that were not complied with included 66 recommendations made across 37 complaints

[00:11:55] in evolving 34 providers, which included 17 individuals. I'm sorry, I don't currently have the... Sorry, can you please come up to the microphone? The individual might not comply, because they've actually retired. So, it might be that after the recommendation was made to that individual to undertake training, they then retired from the workforce. That's the primary reason. I think what I'm trying to get at, Mr Chair, is it's very helpful, because we've had questions about this issue at committee before. So, it's helpful to have it presented this way, that the number, the compliance rate, that's useful.

[00:12:43] But is this a one-off analysis done for this annual report? Or would you be aware across multiple years, if the same practitioner was coming up with recommendations against them, and is there a process by which you initiate action? Absolutely. We always look to see what previous complaints have been made against providers as soon as the complaint comes in. So, we do monitor that. We also have, and I'm sure that our digital case management systems is going to assist us, that we analyse the recommendations really carefully. And if there is repetitive non-compliance, we could also potentially consider reopening a Right 10

[00:13:31] inquiry, which is around the importance of responding to complaints, the right to have a proper complaints process and, of course, as I said, we've previously named providers as well as a consequence of failure to comply. And that is taken quite seriously by the providers concerned. Vanessa, did you have anything further on that? Not as, so far as individual providers concerned, but just to pick up on Morag's earlier point, that we do assess the number of complaints for providers on receipt of a complaint. And we do have agreements with regulatory authorities in relation to a threshold of a number of complaints in any given period of time would trigger us

[00:14:19] to immediately advise, for example, the medical council that we have received that number of complaints. Thank you. Thanks, thank you. And you mentioned during your opening statement that you've cleared the backlog by 50%. So, noting that, and also in your report, there's 25% of opening complaints are older than 24 months. How many of those are older than three years? Five, another, of those 23%, 5% are over three years. So, obviously, you've increased the number of complaints closed quite drastically. What has changed

[00:15:05] to lead to that closure rate increasing? So, there's been a number of steps that we've taken. There's been structural change. So we made, we had a leadership restructure. We've focused on looking at where there have been bottlenecks in our system and we've streamlined the complaints process. We've looked at ways to increase performance internally. We've increased training opportunities for our staff as well. I'm just trying to look, and I'll hand it to Vanessa because she has been managing the backlog. And we've got a clear backlog plan against which we measure accountability at any point in time. So we have, there's been

[00:15:53] some very concerted effort, particularly because we are obviously concerned about not just the number of cases on hand, but the delays in the system. I mean, what role do you think your new IT system is going to play? I didn't quite catch that. What role do you think your new IT system is going to play? I think it's going to, once again, improve our processes in terms of producing duplication or replication of processes. I think it's going to have a really important role to play in terms of communication with our complainants and our providers in the context of more automated responses, whereas previously that was not possible under our old system. It's also going to have a very significant impact, I think, in our ability to report on our data.

[00:16:41] So we're going to be able to mine data more easily. Next slide, please. Help me understand your definition of the backlog and therefore as observers of complaints and reports on complaints, we might see people's experience change. So are you measuring the backlog as from the time of receipt to the time of reporting and therefore does the very small number of complaints over three years mean that we'll cease seeing reports from, I think I read one from 2022 just the other day. So can you help me understand what we should expect to see? So I think firstly

[00:17:29] providers have already reported back to us that they've seen significant improvement in our turnaround of our early resolution files. Their complaints, which we can do immediately back to the provider to resolve to our advocacy service. So we've virtually got no backlog in that space. We're able to turn those around really quickly. There is unquestionably a situation where we're currently working through our older complaints at 2022 files. We're working on our 2023 files and so on. And that has resulted therefore in increased numbers of those older complaints being closed and therefore being more publicly available, I guess. We expect that by the end of this calendar

[00:18:17] year we will have exclude all our 2023 files. That's correct isn't it? And return to a business usual state and I think it's about the time frames within which you could expect a complaint to be resolved and as I said in that more serious end that's 18 to 24 months. In the standard experience of someone complaining to the system, we see that isn't it the more serious end? What would their time to resolution be? Or time to a report being published? We're currently closing 80% within six months in our early resolution space. But there obviously are complaints that we bring into the system that we seek more information on. So that accounts

[00:19:05] probably for another 20%. And we would expect to close those in probably within nine to 12 months. Is that correct? Six to nine months and we have kept that. So part of the backlog elimination plan was to avoid another backlog occurring with the incoming complaints. So since July 2 last year we have kept to all incoming complaints have met those time frames. So closure for our assessed complaints is non-investigation complaints within that period of six to nine months and investigations within 18 months. Okay. Jenny, are you a primary or a

[00:19:53] sub? A sub. Okay, go for it. I'm just interested. Thank you very much. In the complexity of complaints you've been receiving, has there been an increase in the number of more? Are you seeing some of the more complaints coming through in the last 12 months? Again, you sort of cut out a bit there. So you're wanting to know whether there's been a change in the profile of the complaints that we're seeing? Yes. Any greater complexity in complaints coming through? I think in the past a few years it would be fair to say, given the complexity of the provision of healthcare, so multi-disciplinary approaches to healthcare, there has unquestionably been I think a rising

[00:20:41] complexity and new models of provision of care as well. So for example telehealth, we're seeing some emerging issues in telehealth provisions, some of which we do have concerns about, for example internet prescribing of medications in the absence of having clinical notes or knowing comorbidities or other medications that the person is on. In terms of the demographic of complaints though, actually the demographic is moving in accordance with what we've previously seen. So in terms of, say for example GP practice, they're still sitting roughly at about 30% of the complaints that we receive, likewise Health New Zealand at about 40%. What we have seen is an increased number of complaints

[00:21:29] relating to delay and access to medication and around that. Alright, Cam, you had a primary? Yeah, thank you. I just got a quick... Then Kahu and then I should as well. Well, I'm not entirely sure this is in your scope, so that's why I just want to ask. Do you do complaints about under-service? Do you deal with complaints that are deemed to be sort of, this is supposed to be, you've been contracted as an entity or a group to provide our service provided to the contract level? How much do you take in complaints? Or where, if not you, where does it go if someone is concerned that they're not getting a level of service that a group has been contracted to do? Yes, we do

[00:22:17] and so in the context where we are aware that under-service, I mean we measure care against the code of rights. Okay. So if a person isn't receiving a reasonable standard of care, that's potential non-compliance with right for, we look to the reasons why that is the case and we would make recommendations. So for example of Health New Zealand has contracted a particular service and we're concerned that a level of care hasn't been provided in accordance with the reasonable standard of care, we will alert that to Health New Zealand. And so would, like, how many of these concerns about under-delivery on a contract are you discovering and alerting Health New Zealand

[00:23:05] or other contracting services for? And I'm sure this is the case, but is it the only case that you're getting these from complaints? So are you getting them from complaints then you're finding under-delivery of contract? And then who are you saying that to? You're saying that to Health New Zealand, are they monitoring their own contracts? Absolutely. And we escalate concerns too. So if we had an immediate concern about a safety concern under-delivery which was causing harm, we would escalate that immediately and follow that up. So we do... Not so much about the safety, but are people actually not providing, so an area might have one provider for a contract and that's not getting delivered in opposed to the contract. That would seem like a health

[00:23:53] issue, it would seem like a safety issue, broadly, that that contract's not being delivered. I guess I'm asking, like, when you find waste, forward and abuse, are you reporting that in a structure that we could, as parliamentarians, see that there's this much waste, forward and abuse happening in the health contracting system? Are you individually putting that to Health New Zealand and other contracting agencies? How does financial waste, forward and abuse get reported? Hmm. So you're talking about financial wastage in the context of contracted service provision? Well, I'm asking, what do you do with that information when you find it? I'm assuming you do find it. Moira, could I add a bit more? Certainly we'd understand the experience, an individual's experience, so if a provider was

[00:24:41] to support 15 people and one of those people complained to us about their experience, we'd know about that. We wouldn't know if 14 other people didn't receive this contracted service that they should have. So we'd be able to contribute in one perspective, but not in that other. So do you have a final question, if there may? Do you have a sort of sister commissioning agency that provides waste, forward, commissioner that looks at waste, forward and abuse that you can talk to that you've got an open line of communication with, that contract's not being delivered on, contracted, being reported to? Or is that relying on the contracting entity, the Health New Zealand Ministry, health to look into?

[00:25:29] It's reliant on the contracting entity. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah, thank you. I want to talk about the complaints made and you talked about different demographics, so GPs, other services. Do you have the data around like breaking those down so and to say, maternal very post-natal health, youth mental health seniors, do you have though that sort of data and if you do, has there been an increase in any specific area of complaints in that 24 to 25 period? Do you collect ethnicity data, age data

[00:26:17] from our complaints, forms? By the way, we are going to be in a much better position to report on that now that we have this new system. I would say that we haven't seen any noticeable increases that have caused us concern to raise issues in any particular demographic. I'm going to turn to Natasha in a moment. That said, we do see things in complaints. Even if it might be one or two complaints, we think actually there's an issue here and we need to raise it. And it's unfortunate. So I think last time I was here, there was mention around, for example, youth line and issues that we saw in terms of risk to

[00:27:05] youth as consequence of concerns that we had around risk escalation in clinical governance. I'm just going to check with Natasha. Have we got any? No, you're right. So our data is really largely qualitative because it's people's experience, right? So there hasn't been, we do have a close eye on numbers. So there hasn't been a spike necessarily in any area. But what we do pay close attention to is the themes and where we so whether an issue, there's been an increase in an issue. So for example, delays or in certain service areas where we've seen recurring things that is also of concern to us. Thank you. And so in those recurring themes areas, do you then break it down into

[00:27:53] things like wait times? Like that's where that's broken down. Awesome. So I'm getting it. Yes, that's correct. And then do you do any forecasting in terms of where the data is actually quantitative and it's going into looking at the increased crisis with youth mental health. And then looking at how many people are actually complaining and saying, hmm, actually maybe the young people don't know about this complaints process and what advocacy can we do to then get them to have this service. So part of advocacy would be making sure that people know that they can do it. And I know the ombudsman has done a lot of that work.

[00:28:41] So yeah, so I guess that's a too long question. It's about making do you see what's happening out there. So for example, with the maternal mental health peri-imposonatal mental health and youth mental health. And then sort of forecast what you could expect and then look at actually what you are getting. It would be fair to say that we do very closely monitor what's happening, not even just in terms of our complaints data. So you can actually see issues arise through the media. Other agencies might bring them to our attention as well. We don't necessarily forecast, but we can monitor and I completely agree with you. Part of the

[00:29:29] issue is also identifying those populations that don't complain to us. That's actually personally, as commissioner, I do find that quite concerning. And you look to see how can we improve the advocacy or education for those communities. We do rely on advocacy service in the current environment to do a lot of the networking. And they do thousands of networking opportunities a year focused on particular priority populations and educational sessions as well. In the context of our funding constraints we have had to make a strategic decision to focus on our complaints resolution rather than our education and promotion duties because they are statutory duties.

[00:30:17] That said, we also, we do look for opportunities and we engage with other agencies for them to raise issues with us. For example, recently I met with the Children's Commissioner, for example. Thank you. And so I really appreciate your work. It's so important. So just so I understand in terms of say legislation that might be coming through, do you do any forecasting and do ministers reach out to you or do you advocate to ministers or ministries around what you're seeing and where improvements might need to happen? Probably a good example of that was the passage of the End of Life Choice Act because we knew

[00:31:05] that when that legislation was coming into force that HDC had a specific role to manage complaints in that space. As it happened, we didn't receive as many complaints as we had anticipated that we might. That said, we were very closely, particularly at the time that the act came into force with the relevant agencies and we continue obviously as required under that legislation to report to the registrar on the complaints that we are seeing. And continually in our quarterly reports to the ministry and minister highlight those kind of issues. Fantastic. So did they ever reach out to you? Please come through the chair. Oh, I'm so sorry. Could I please have two more supplementaries? Turn off subs. Keep them concise.

[00:31:53] It's a good challenge. Absolutely. So has there been particular wait time and delay to access complaints around maternal mental health and around youth mental health? Access issues specifically, maternal mental health. And like wait lists? Wait list times? Not that I'm aware of in terms of complaints. The maternity complaints tend to relate to the provision of care. Wonderful. Thank you. That does feature that often in maternity complaints there are multiple issues and there may be a feature of maternal mental health but that's not normally the

[00:32:41] main feature of the complaint. Sure thing. And in terms of forecasting, you've talked about budget constraints having an impact on the complaints that are coming in. Have you done any forecasting for this financial year from 2425 into 2627 around what you are expecting with budget constraints? We've obviously been doing some budget planning but we feel confident that we can maintain our current level of service in relation to our complaints resolution. Thank you so much. And that's in the context too of increasing complaint volume. Incredible work. Thank you. Dr Vera. Thank you.

[00:33:29] Thanks for your remarks in your opening statement recognizing the need to take up more systemic inquiries. You mentioned the MATIC reports. Book us through the difference, please. And then going back to the systemic issues, where do you think you'd like to where is the potential scope for your next investigation to go? The MATIC reports we rely on our existing data to look at what the common themes we are seeing in the complaints data that we have. So we've done the two disability reports we are anticipating that we may do one on maternity services later in the year and we're considering also mental health report as well.

[00:34:17] So that's looking at the existing data and looking at opportunities to make recommendations on that data based on the themes that we're seeing. We also do commissioner initiated investigations. I think I mentioned that in my last appearance. And that might be on one complaint but it might be on a cluster of complaints where we look to see whether there are any and firstly resolution for complaints themselves but also looking at often systemic issues that may have contributed to the events that gave rise to the complaint. I think in terms of future we have been quite thoughtful about where you might look to broader systemic inquiries

[00:35:05] going forward. I think what we would need to do is develop some kind of matrix to identify the priorities in the health sector. I think at the moment if you were to do a systemic issue looking more broadly across the whole of New Zealand, one of the biggest issues would be in and around resource constraints, the constraint system, workforce issues. And that's all kinds of provision of service. So it might be ophthalmology, it might be gynecology, it might be surgery or cancer care. So it's quite difficult to work out where you focus it but that's something we are going to consider. Thank you. And one of the

[00:35:53] discussions relevant to a petition the committee received is let's say under resourcing that you just mentioned as an example. Well, understaffing might feature quite prominently in complaints. So that also raises a number of regulatory and resourcing things which traditionally aren't the subject of your reports in a systemic inquiry. Is that the sort of information you can go into or not? Well, whenever we look at a complaint in these issues around for example staffing levels or resourcing constraints within the system that they service that we're looking at,

[00:36:41] we can make comment on those and in fact I have escalated concerns. Let's take ophthalmology, I raised concerns with the minister about service level, where demand was outstruck in capacity at the ophthalmology service at Waitaha, Canterbury because it was having a direct impact on consumers and there was the potential harm to be caused to them. I can't influence or I try to influence funding decisions but ultimately funding decisions rests with the minister but what I can do is tell the patient stories and identify the potential harm that flows in the context, in that particular context and then I follow up with recommendations to see what is being done about the resourcing constraints in that particular service.

[00:37:29] Thank you. My second primary, Mr Chair, small number of complaints that you received but sadly still too many will be the sexual exploitation of patients by practitioners. Can you explain the HDC's role in that and what I'm particularly interested in is how many fora a complainant needs to go through in order to get resolution, is it correct that they go for the HDC, the director of prosecutions and then the human rights review tribunal? It really depends. So for example with the, with sexual boundary violations associated with

[00:38:17] regulation providers, we, particularly for example, let's take doctors have been referring those complaints to the medical council, for example, because they have systems in place to manage essentially a conduct issue relating to a doctor. But we also manage unregulated providers and Vanessa will be able to speak to this. We've had a few complaints relating to unregistered councillors. We conduct those investigations and if we've determined the breach and we've determined the breach seriously enough, we refer it to the director of proceedings. The director of proceedings has an independent role to determine whether or not to bring, they can't bring

[00:39:05] disciplinary proceedings for unregulated providers, they can only go to the human rights review tribunal. The director of prosecute proceedings in this context is a officer who's been put up under the same act that the HDC is. How long would that take for a person to go through that process? Well at the moment they do tend so obviously we'd need to go through the investigation process and under the current system we would hope that we would prioritize that kind of complaint and we would look to have that closed as quickly as possible but as I mentioned before, at which time would be 18 months to 2 years, then there needs to be an independent decision made by

[00:39:53] the director of proceedings. If she was to issue proceedings before the human rights review tribunal, there are delays in that system at the moment. I can't tell you what those delays are but I understand they are considerable. And just the other version of that process that you mentioned where it's a professional that the complaint is against is a regulated profession. That's right. In that instance, does the complaint actually effectively have no time with the HDC because it's referred straight to the responsible authority? Yes, that's correct. And the responsible authority then decides if they take a prosecution.

[00:40:41] That's right. Thank you. Thank you, Mrs. Chair. I look at your annual report. One of the major or common issues for complaint is on communication. 68%. So what advice have you given to the ministry or given to the system to try to improve in this area? Communication is something we raise constantly with providers, not just through the reports that we issue, but whenever we're in a situation where we're giving education to providers as well. It features in the online provider modules that we have available to

[00:41:29] providers who can access for training in that respect. We comment on it often. There are specific issues in relation to informed consent. So for example, we have roughly 16 to 18% of complaints relate to informed consent. And effective communication is part of those rights. It's right five. You have a right to effective communication so we can comment on things such as the environment in which communication occurred, the English as a second language issues and how to interpret it for people with disabilities, need for supported decision making and having supports present. So we do regularly raise these issues.

[00:42:17] Can I just give a brief example? One example is a common issue for a long time was people not getting expected time frames for treatment from Health New Zealand. So we did work with Health New Zealand on the letters that they were sending out to patients to give indication of when they could expect changes in wait times etc. But do you see this improvement in this area? If you look back the past five years, this is the percentage of the company still pretty much the same or you see this improvement in communication in this sector? It's actually stayed relatively static and seems statistic. Something we are seeing and I actually also think you see it in a pressured system. When people are pressured for time, they may not necessarily have the time

[00:43:05] to engage as you might if you had more time. We see that in relation, for example, same day consent where because of pressures within the system, there's more often consenting processes happening at the time on the day of the procedure with consequences that sometimes the communication is not as good as one might have hoped for. But it's something we will continue to raise concerns about. Communication is also what drives complaints. So the reason people usually complain is that something went wrong in their care and in the context of that thing going wrong, they were poorly communicated with. One more primary?

[00:43:53] One more primary. Just first. Thank you very much. I'm interested to know, do you do any data comparison with other countries? How does New Zealand sit in terms of how much is our system complained about for how people feel poorly treated or whatever? Are we at the bottom of the OECD, like we are for medicines access, or where is that place that New Zealand sits on that international scale? It's a really good question. In fact, it's quite hard to do international comparison because we have quite a unique system. For example, the passage of the

[00:44:41] Code of Consumers Rights into law was a global first and in fact it's a very unique piece of legislation in the context of the New Zealand environment. Likewise we have ACC, and that is also unique, which has an impact on how the medical and legal environment works. I do have communication with the Australian commissioners, but once again it's very hard to compare data because the system is and their jurisdiction is so different. They have greater, while we manage unregulated providers, in Australia a lot of their complaints are managed through the regulatory authority APRA, which is the broader regulatory authority over registered health providers. I do engage with the Australian commissioners, but it's very hard

[00:45:29] to compare the data and to make those direct comparisons about whether we're worse or better than other systems. It's often said that the ACC system and the context of how we manage complaints is a good system comparatively to the Tejas systems which exist in other jurisdictions. Thank you. Good answers. Good question. One more. One more. You just want to quick touch on the promotion and education. Obviously I see on your annual report you also achieve over 6000 people are viewing your online, your right video. So I'm just wondering, what platform you use to promote this

[00:46:17] video and do you think only 6000 people only feel that video? Is it achieved the goal you want to do to raise the perfect awareness of their right? Thank you Dr Chung. I would love to be doing a promotion of the code, but the reality is that in the context of our backlog and our current complaints volume, we have got to focus our resources on managing those complaints. As I said before we are very reliant on our advocacy service to do that promotion and to do that education. Have had the number of contacts on that particular video being at a level which we would like? Obviously we would like more. I think it was certainly a first for HDC. I think we need to look as we

[00:47:05] move to a business as usual state. I think we need to look really carefully because we want people to understand their rights. We want people to be able to manage their complaints with the providers and we want people to access us if they are not getting the resolution that they need. So I think there is a lot more work to be done here and I think we should be looking too at more platforms to increase people's understanding of their rights. Is those patients charged from the hospital will look at the video before or would they just volunteer and look at your website and find a video? It's largely a big part of the industry I think and also as I said our advocacy service can help to promote that. You will be aware from your own, I would hope that you would be aware from

[00:47:53] your own experience as a consumer that whenever you go to your healthcare provider there is often our poster on the wall which highlights the rights that people have and the relevant collateral which tells you how to get in touch with us or our advocacy plans managed or your concerns managed. Thank you. The video was playing in weight rooms as well. You know on the screens at a lot of the hospital weight rooms and GP services that hasn't been able to be supported this year but that's another area we can look to revisit once finances allow. Thank you. Thank you very much for your time today. Thank you.

[00:48:41] Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you.

meetings.title: Education and Workforce Committee
meetings.description: This is "Education and Workforce Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Aotearoa, kaitiakitanga, mana whenua. you you

[00:01:02] you you

[00:02:02] you you

[00:03:02] you you

[00:04:02] you you

[00:05:25] you you uh just a note while you're taking your seat to the officials that are sitting in the back, if you have anything to speak to please come to the table, you're more than welcome but we can't hear anything that you say from the seats in terms of the recording. Okay. Welcome. We have half an hour with you for our review briefing. We'll have questions from the floor. If you wanted to do a brief introduction, that would be wonderful. But if you could take that to a couple of minutes, that would be great. And then we'll get into questions. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. Good afternoon. My name is Tony Gray, Board Chair of Education New

[00:06:16] Zealand, Malapur, Keetelal. I'll make some opening remarks and then hand over to ENZ, Acting Chief Executive, Doctor Linda Sissons. Education New Zealand's role is to promote New Zealand as one of the world's most trusted and transformative education destinations where learners thrive, communities benefit, and with the government's international education going for growth plan ensure that international education is a defining force in a prosperous connected to our education. New Zealand sees the opportunity now as not recovery. It is intentional education growth and discipline where ENZ role becomes increasingly central as a system enabler of one of New Zealand's most important

[00:07:02] export sectors. We believe based on the 2024-25 annual review evidence and progress already made in the last 12 months that the momentum is real. The sector is trusted and the platform for measured growth is credible. International education, as you know, supports jobs and regional growth, strengthens global connections and enriches classrooms and communities across the country. And ENZ's work amplifies the value of ministerial diplomatic and trade engagement with partner nations by translating high-level relationships into practical education outcomes. We achieve this by working closely with other New Zealand agencies and sector partners. To support this work, the Education New Zealand Board

[00:07:48] maintains strong oversight of governance, financial sustainability and risk with a standard audit New Zealand opinion and focused agile operating model aligned to fiscal constraints and the potential for effective collaboration. On that hand over to Dr Sissons. Thank you. Thank you to the select committee for the opportunity to provide some supporting commentary to the points made by our chair. We're very pleased that we were able to achieve 99% of New Zealand's forecasted 84,000 enrollments for the 2024-25 year and also that international education gained $4.6 billion of export earnings well on the way to our 2034 goal of $7.2 billion.

[00:08:39] 77% of New Zealanders surveyed for their views on international students in 2025 said they wanted to see either the same number or more students coming to New Zealand and 87% of our international students positively rated their New Zealand experience with 43% of them saying that their experience was excellent, a rise of about 6% of points from the previous year. However, the international education world is moving fast and volatile manner and we're conscious of the need for New Zealand's offering to evolve quickly and I'd just like to note quickly four specific

[00:09:24] operational areas where Education New Zealand is looking to further enhance our effectiveness and efficiency going forward. First of all our offshore presence is critical and I'm very pleased that a fourth regional director this time for India will take up this post in early June. Secondly as a small country New Zealand has to work hard to be visible and even better seen in a positive light. So we're gratified that as a result of our marketing New Zealand is now ranked in the top three of potential education destinations of 22% of prospective international students and in terms of prompted awareness we're on a

[00:10:10] par with countries like Germany and Singapore. Thirdly as the business of international education becomes ever more competitive and complex we're investing seriously in more sophisticated and timely research insights and intelligence for the sector and NZing. And fourthly our go-to-market strategy for the past and coming years balances continuing growth in well-established markets like China with accelerated growth in more recent markets like Sri Lanka along with research into four emerging markets which we do not have mistaken at the moment. So those are the four sort of areas that we're

[00:10:59] developing and working very hard on now just to bring our comments up to date. Thank you. Thank you very much. We have our first question from CalBates. Thanks very much chair and thanks for the work that you've been doing. It's great to see some clear targets around the numbers of certain enrollments from where we started this conversation a couple of years ago. Just interested in the growth numbers compared to the awareness. So the awareness between 2024 and 2034 is to increase by essentially 10%, 40% 44 but you want to have a growth in student enrollments from 83,000 to 119 which is a 43% growth. Is the growth rate in

[00:11:45] the awareness aggressive enough to deliver that actual conversion in enrollment numbers? The way in which New Zealand shows up in the world is the factor of an enormous number of different circumstances. Awareness by itself is simply do they know we exist and that is in a country as small and physically remote as we are that is a difficult nut to crack on the world stage. However, enrollment is itself the product of a great deal of marketing and representation, government to government contacts and so on.

[00:12:35] And I believe that the two are not necessarily a product of each other. Okay. So these are your three primary objectives. Given what you've just said, can you tell me as an organisation what you measure as your primary conversion metrics or the things that you believe drive conversion and how you measure those? Yeah. Conversion is driven essentially by the providers who are enrolling the students. Their expression of interest, sometimes their lead that we provide to them from the events that we run overseas becomes the first step on a process that turns into the conversion

[00:13:27] into an enrollment. So in a sense, we have responsibility for two quite different processes. However, they link together at the range of fairs and events that we are in charge of setting up overseas. So it's a measure that belongs to the providers. For us, I think we would assess its effectiveness in terms of the amount of knowledge that those prospective students have before they make the decision to enroll and the relevance of that knowledge. So whether or not they understand what it's like to be in New Zealand,

[00:14:14] what it costs, whether there are pathways they can follow, say from secondary to tertiary. So the quality of our data and our information is a factor in the speed and the effectiveness of that conversion. So from a marketing perspective and the amount of money essentially invested in your role, which is marketing New Zealand and really important, appreciate that, what do you measure as the key success measures or the things that turn the needle the most that the dollar we're giving you to market New Zealand actually impacts the conversion that Massey University or Yukola Munganui or Auckland University has of that student. I appreciate everything you're saying. I've been in marketing

[00:15:04] all my life, but what I'm not understanding and haven't understood over the last couple of years is what is the connection that your management team sit down around the table and say this number needs to move in order for us to know we're actually having an impact. Okay, let me answer that in two ways. One is the array of measures that you can see in our annual report, which include leads, actual enrollments, proactive research of New Zealand, the brand preference and the brand awareness. So there's a range of factors that demonstrate how effective that we are. The second relates to the market by market plan that we devise in the course of planning for the year ahead

[00:15:57] so that in the process, as you probably know, we have four types of markets that we are in. They're markets where we know there is going to be growth and where we can calculate how big that growth will be. There are markets that we maintain a presence in and maintain our reputation and there are core markets where we have been for a while and have been successful and believe that we should maintain that relationship. And there are emerging markets that we have not entered into. For each of those, we devise a set of targets in terms of ultimate enrollments based on a whole set of metrics from the GDP of the country itself, the population growth,

[00:16:51] the art by mobility in turn on New Zealand. Sorry to interrupt. We have got very limited time and I don't think this is answering the question that Carl has asked. So if you're not able to answer it now, I wonder if you could come back to the committee with some more information. My understanding of the question asked was what work are you doing as a management team to assess the work that's actually turning the dial and seeing enrollments eventually? So if that's okay, we're going to move on because we've got a lot of other questions. We certainly will come back, but I think that I have answered it in the sense that all of those markers are the things that we work on every day and assess every day. Okay. If I could ask it

[00:17:39] in a different way and maybe our final goal at this, but if not then we'll... If you're okay, Lawrence, we've got a long list of questions and we've got about 20 minutes left. So we're going to go to Shannon and we'll come to you. Then it looks like Simon. We can do some cleanup questions at the end. I think this is going to be one of those cases where we send follow-up questions to get more detailed answers. So Shannon, help it. Thank you for the work that you're doing. Obviously, across the tertiary sector we're increasingly reliant on international students to buffer the overall sector and funding challenges that we have. And as you know, markets can switch on and they can switch off. What are the risks that you have identified in this period and or given advice to the minister on that could potentially close off markets

[00:18:30] or that you have identified as risk areas and how have they been managed? Well, there's a very current set of calculations are being taken right now with regards to risks around fuel uncertainty, geopolitical uncertainty. And while as yet we do not believe they've affected enrolments in New Zealand, it is something that is under weekly regard by us to make sure that we are making sure that both the markets that we are seeking are not themselves affected by idea political change, but also that they will be able to travel students from those countries. The second area has to do with the kinds of risks that are things like political upheaval

[00:19:25] in certain countries. Countries that are where fraud is a particularly rife. And in that regard, we work very closely, particularly with Immigration New Zealand, who makes quite detailed risk assessments of the countries to whom they're going to be asked to offer visas and other agencies such as Ministry of Foreign Affairs. So there's a constant reviewing of the risk profile of countries that we might be in and a quarterly review from our viewpoint of where we are sitting in the world. Thank you. Some institutions have raised with me that through government's funding changes, particularly in research, that the flow on effect of that will be

[00:20:14] a reduction in rankings. Is that a risk to our international markets? Have you raised it? Is it something on your radar? So could you just explain what is affecting that reduction? Reduction of research funding overall will have implications for the research that they produce and research informs our international rankings. Is that a risk that you have identified and advised the minister on? It is not. And in fact, when I look at our universities, their growth in international students, their recovery from COVID-19 has been really remarkable. They're now back up to where they were in 2019. And their annual growth,

[00:21:06] which currently is around 15%, is quite remarkable. I think they are also starting to adjust their offerings such that they meet in the needs of the future in terms of innovation, IT, the sorts of partnerships that we see in India at the moment, for example, with IRT. So the answer is no, we're not seeing that at the moment. And I've got huge confidence in the ability of our universities to meet their goals. Thank you. In terms of diversifying the markets, I heard you say that it is volatile, but there's four key areas, other regions that you

[00:21:52] are looking at. What are they? And then at the other end, I'm interested in the distribution of enrollments between urban centres like Auckland and poly-tap areas like Napier that rely on international students. Sure. Sorry, the first part was? The first one is what are the four regions that you're looking at? The four countries are Nepal, Taiwan, Italy and France, maybe four though, emerging at which we don't have either much or any presence at the moment. And how are we going on Auckland and everywhere else? Well, Auckland is approximately 55% of the

[00:22:42] international students are there. Second after them is Christchurch with around 11%. Third is Waikato with around 7%. So between the three of them, that's over 70. And the rest are here scattered all around New Zealand, but not in anything like such large concentrations. Are those target areas for you? Small remote areas that have local polytechnics reliant on international enrollment? We do believe that with the re-emergence of polytechnics as standalone institutions, that there will be a move towards making a regional value proposition. And we'd certainly

[00:23:29] welcome that because we do believe that every part of New Zealand is extremely attractive and beautiful and has its own uniqueness. So we're looking forward to that. Lawrence, can you do the next question from you? Thank you. I've got a few questions. Thank you for coming. I'm going to start with, I know other people may have questions around the Monarchy scholarship.
