My first question to you around Monarchy scholarship, maybe the only one is, could you please talk us through on what happened with application for Monarchy scholarship and why were there programs on there that never existed, what doesn't exist for universities? When I do talk about Monarchy scholarships, I'm very conscious that it is funded by the foreign

[00:24:15] affairs, Ministry of Foreign Affairs. So there's a limit to the amount of detail that I think possibly appropriate for this select committee. They're always willing to be guided by the chair. I'm referring to the funding and more to do with the application process, which sits with ENZ. In the application process, most recently, people going in to apply for a particular course and particular program that were on the list, those programs does not exist. So once they go to the university, the universities that we cannot offer you an offer for this or a Monarchy scholar or any sort of things from our end, because the program that you have applied from Monarchy doesn't exist in our system. The Monarchy scholarships are translating to a single

[00:25:05] portal and a single technological solution, which happens to be called animata, which is being set up right now and which will enable a nationally consistent engine for processing the applications for Monarchy scholarships. It is in the process of being built and just a couple of months ago launched this year's intake for Monarchy scholarships. Having a single national database of qualifications proved to be quite complex, given the complex nature of programs at universities. Sometimes they are subsets of

[00:25:54] one particular degree. Sometimes they have different names and I do admit that turning that into a single national system with the help of New Zealand Qualifications Authority turned out to be not adequate to the complexity of the way in which they are described within the universities. That has very quickly been put right and we have seen so far 2,000 more applications this year than last year. So we are satisfied that people are able to get through the system and get to the qualification they want. That's helpful. Just a few more questions. Thanks, Madam Chief. One of the things

[00:26:39] that is within ENCS Remit is market insight. How are you looking at market insight compared to other countries of similar jurisdictions, Australia, UK, US and Canada being specific areas in terms of our growth international student growth strategy? But then coming up with that following what you just said to my colleague, Shan Halbert, with Nepal being one of them. Nepal was a really interesting case because Australia almost do not accept students from Nepal anymore because of qualification concerns and among other things. Dan, are you looking at to ensure to address some of the barriers that other countries are seeing in terms of certain jurisdictions they no longer accept or have limited, allow for unlimited acceptance when it comes to the four key areas?

[00:27:31] Yeah. Okay, starting with the insights. As we all know, New Zealand is a small country of comparatively limited means. So within our budget for insights and research unit, there is a finite amount of projects they can do. Nevertheless, the community, the international education community around the world is one that shares many of the insights and the results that they gain. So there's a great deal of exchange of knowledge that goes on. We both commission research from established researchers around the world and we conduct some of our own,

[00:28:18] in particular the student satisfaction survey. In terms of sharing with other countries about markets that may not work out or may be on the margins, I'm very conscious that not only Education New Zealand has its network of researchers that we work with, but also our other agency such as Immigration New Zealand is also part of networks of immigration authority. So there's a great deal of exchange about which countries have which problems and hopefully how to solve them. That's also not something we track because offshore student visa agents

[00:29:05] do not need to be registered with IAA. But anyway, that's all good. Thank you so much. My final question is to do with English language schools. Now, as you will know that there's been a significant drop in terms of the number, we have never bounced back to pre-COVID levels. In your report, you mentioned the English language schools. I wondered if you're able to specify further whether those English language schools are PDE specifically looking at English language or that also include English language schools as attached to a university like EIA, for example, because those offer mainly pathways into universities and IELTS and English proficiencies going to university. What I'm not seeing is whether the numbers that you've got for people who come here,

[00:29:54] maybe on a visitor visa, do of eight weeks, 12 weeks English language course as part of their New Zealand experience and New Zealand exchange. Do you have any data specifically on that? I believe that they are not counted in our IELTS. They're not counted when we talk about English language schools. We talk about independent English language schools and I am not that confident that we are able to track the numbers that come in through those other routes. Next question from Simon Court. It's been waiting patiently. So one of the things that really interests me about the Manaki program is the requirement to immediately return to the student's country at the completion of the qualification. His

[00:30:41] Education New Zealand received any feedback from students or their sponsors about whether that is of benefit to the students or whether it may be more beneficial for them to get a couple of years work experience in their field and in New Zealand's setting before returning to their home country? First of all, in policy terms, this will be a Ministry of Foreign Affairs policy matter. Secondly, there are a number of opportunities for internships that are provided under the Manaki scheme that enable those scholars when they finish to do some work experience in New Zealand before they return to their home. And the third thing is with the new national

[00:31:32] enrolment system that we are setting up, a module of that will be about alumni and I think that's going to be of huge benefit to New Zealand and to a wider set of countries to find out what happens and to stay in touch with students after they graduate because we know that many often go on to play leading roles in their countries and could well interact with New Zealand in the future. So my question was, have you had any feedback from students or their sponsors about this matter? I am not aware of any. Okay, thank you. Thanks, Chair. Part of marketing and taking opportunity is responding to current events. What has Education New Zealand done in terms

[00:32:19] of the opportunity with the signing of the free trade deal with India to capitalise on the New Zealand name and focus on New Zealand and India right now? Yeah, thank you. And we're certainly very thrilled to hear of the signing of the free trade agreement. Well, as I said right at the start, we will for the first time add another regional director, which is an important step and that new regional director is going to India at the beginning of June. The reason why that's important is that regional directors have diplomatic status so that they're able to be part of the entire New Zealand process as well as obviously adding a staff member into the Indian team. In terms of the opportunities that the free trade agreement will bring,

[00:33:11] when Minister Reti went to India in January, he signed a number of agreements around research and scholarships, the launch of New Zealand Excellence Awards and the signing of a refreshed education cooperation arrangement as well as 30 virtual internships for IIT Delhi students. So this was, we realised that this was an important moment having as a senior minister go to Delhi and we were very happy to put a whole raft of different opportunities around that.

[00:33:57] Apart from that, I think that what will be particularly of value to New Zealand will be the post-study work visa situation that comes with the free trade agreement so that students who have done a New Zealand qualification will be able, if they have a bachelor or master's in a STEM subject, be able to have extended post-study work rights and I think the sort of ongoing value to New Zealand and to our own economy will be very enhanced by that and that as a marketing tool for New Zealand is pretty important going forward. So yes, we're very very invested. Thank you. If I can, the last minute, ask a quick question just back on your point about

[00:34:46] English language schools. We have seen the track down, obviously the track up, every other sector's bounced back pretty well. Do you see that there is even capacity as part of your 2034 target for that part to bounce back for English language schools or do you see the capacity is going to come from other parts like more into universities, more and so on etc. Where do you see that potential in your goal for 2034 for growth? Yeah thank you and I didn't answer that part. I think there's a wider picture here which is how English language is learned these days because it is often through those other types of engagements and experience, New Zealand experience that English has learned. There are many apps, there is much more English teaching in the country. Sorry to cut you short,

[00:35:35] we're very tight for time. Can you just answer whether or not you see there being capacity within English language schools in the future or whether you see the capacity being in university schools etc. I don't have any evidence either way at this stage. If I was to make an educated guess, certainly the English language schools are working hard on increasing their numbers and they did go up 8% in the most current year that we're talking about. However I think it will become much more varied and I would just finally say if I may that much English is now being taught in countries so in Southeast Asian countries, kids are learning English there and they're not coming completely fresh. Is there more capacity though in universities and schools

[00:36:23] to help you reach that 2034 target? I will ask about that. I don't know if there will be. That would be great. Thank you very much. Appreciate your time. Thank you. Sorry it feels rushed, we had no chance to go over time so appreciate you making yourselves available. Thank you.

meetings.title: Health Committee
meetings.description: This is "Health Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Good morning. Good morning. Good morning, Chair. Good morning, Good morning. Thank you for taking the time to join us this morning. We have you in here for 15 minutes, 10 minutes, 10 minutes.

[00:00:46] I'd just like to welcome you on behalf of the committee. You've both got personal lived experience through this and put together a very helpful pack for us to consider. So I'll hand that time over to you now. If you want to leave us any questions, you may, but that's yours. Thank you. Hello, Maureen, everyone, and thank you for the opportunity to present today. My name is Gerard Rushton, and I'm Chairman of the Meningitis Foundation of Aotearoa, New Zealand. I had with me today Penny Taylor, a meningitis survivor. We presented to this committee in 2022, at which time we had support from all parties across Parliament.

[00:01:33] We also had support from Tafata Ora, Pharmac and the House Select Committee. There has been some progress since then, but our adolescents are still at risk. So why does meningitis, I'll just ask for the next slide, please. What is meningitis prevention and awareness matter? The mortality and morbidity rate of meningitis makes it one of the most feared diseases. It can take a life in a matter of hours, hence the need to protect people with vaccinations, because often treatment comes too late. The current criteria for adolescence vaccinations means the majority of our at-risk adolescents are simply not protected.

[00:02:23] Recent cases in Dunedin in the UK are a stark reminder that without a full and equitable vaccination programme, how quickly an outbreak can grow. And we believe New Zealand is grossly unprepared to deal with such an outbreak. Next slide, please. I'd like now to introduce Penny Taylor. Penny is one of our VIPs, a very impacted person. So thank you Penny, I'd just like to hear your story now. Sorry, I have a few notes, because my brain does move around a little bit. So 15 years ago, I contracted meningococcal, and it was a normal typical day.

[00:03:11] I was a mum of three and a seven-year-old, and the only symptom I had was vomiting out of all of the symptoms that there are. I naturally thought it was a bug from my daughter's daycare centre. Within hours, my mum came past my house and found me unconscious. I was taken to Palmerston Health Hospital, and at that point I was given 30 minutes to live in a 2% survival chance. So I spent one month in a coma. I blew up twice the size of myself, and all my organs had failed. And I had a 42 degree temperature, which I was essentially frying from the inside.

[00:04:01] As a result, with my organs failing, I then had septicemia come in. And as you can see by my pictures, it took hold, and I looked like a very severe case of frostbite over my legs and fingers. After a month, they woke me up, and the Palmerston Health Hospital knew that I would need debriding and skin grafting amputations. Dialysis is a severe range of internal treatment. They were trying to get me into Waikato Hospital because the burns and the dialysis is all in the one hospital. They wouldn't accept me because I was out of the region.

[00:04:46] So I was helicoptered to Hutt Hospital for the plastics, and was in their high dependency ward. At that point, I had my amputation, so I still was at very high risk that I wasn't going to make this surgery. So after 12 hours, they amputated my legs, they amputated my fingers and my hand, and did all my skin grafting all over my body. The next month was probably the most tortuous part of the whole journey that needed. It didn't really need to happen had I gone to Waikato. Morning and night, I was having surgeries, and in between they were transporting me to Wellington Hospital for dialysis.

[00:05:32] So every single bump, touch, I would feel. The dialysis, although it was working for my kidneys, it would dialyse all the medication, so it left me with nothing to come back on. And so I was playing catch-up every single day with pain meds. That was excruciating. After that, I was transferred back to Palmerston Hospital for rehabilitation. The hand therapist was on holiday and they had no one else, and as a result, my hand is frozen, and I have limited function in it. And that is not from the disease, that is from lack of physiotherapy. As soon as I was out of hospital, everything stopped.

[00:06:19] I had no aftercare, I had no physiotherapy, I was there to fight for myself. I had two small children. So I was in a wheelchair. Largely my mum and dad were there to help support me. But it took me, I had to make a decision to financially, I needed to go back to work because I couldn't survive. So the only way I could go back, I was to go back part-time because physically I was exhausted. I was still trying to recover from this disease, run a family and try and have an income. So it was pretty tough going.

[00:07:04] Then came the days of prosthetics. As you can imagine, my skins were very, very delicate and scarred. So when I went to the limb centre in Wellington, they literally looked at me and didn't think they could help. So the five years of working part-time in a five-year battle of prosthetics resulted basically me in a wheelchair. So completely frustrated by that entire system that prosthetics was my only option, the traditional sockets. I went searching for something else, which I found a surgeon in Australia who does osteointegration surgery.

[00:07:49] And that is an implant that directly goes into your skeleton. No socket, my skin would be okay. I qualified for that surgery with my bone density and my internal organs being reasonably healthy. Once I had that surgery, I had that surgery 10 years ago. I then went right back to square one to a wheelchair again and I had a two-year rehabilitation program run through the Australian team. What that surgery has given me back, sorry, on that surgery that was completely unsupported by New Zealand. They thought I was putting myself at risk and they did not support my decision to have that surgery.

[00:08:39] So 10 years of that surgery, I have battled on my own and self-advocated for myself. But what that surgery has given me, I had to fundraise for the surgery and pay for it myself. What it has given me, it's enabled me to go back to work full-time. I am a gym goer. I walk over 10,000 steps a day. I do what everyone else does and so that was worth it for me. I actually got my life back by choosing to fund this myself and make that decision. I have since worked with the limb centre and with the 10 years of history that I have to present that this could be an option for people.

[00:09:33] So this, I guess for me, I have the drive and the motivation to self-advocate and fight through. I had my two children to live for and that was my driving force. However, not everyone has that and they don't have a voice, which is a real kicker for me. The last 15 years has been a complete roller coaster and I have literally done it on my own. A vaccine could have saved all of that for me. I was 36 years of age so I was outside of the normal age bracket. The mental and the physical and the financial impact that has had on me in the last 15 years is significant.

[00:10:24] I am evidence of what it does do and the varying conditions that people are left with if they do survive. Thank you, Penny. I think you've changed the slide. Thank you, Penny. Very brave of you to come here today and share your story. It's traumatic and I could leave my presentation there because there's justification in its own for what we're talking about. Clearly from Penny's presentation, the New Zealand health system is failing us with limited vaccine access, lack of support for survivors. We have no fence at the top of the cliff and we have no ambulance at the bottom either.

[00:11:12] Since the implementation of the infant vaccination programme in 2023, the vaccination rates in their infants have raised and the number of cases of infant meningitis have noticeably reduced. So the vaccine actually works. We're concerned the adolescent criteria is far too narrow. It's confusing. We question the rationale around it. Why is it right that someone that can afford to go to boarding school or be in the halls of residence should qualify for a free vaccination against anyone else who's an adolescent in our country? Our Marian Pacifica population of five times more likely to contract meningitis,

[00:12:00] yet most of them do not qualify for a free vaccination. Why? Meningitis vaccinations applications are penalised in the pharmac process because simply the more people that require a vaccine have access to the vaccine, the more people that need to be vaccinated. So we're concerned that the vaccination rates against the inclusion of that vaccine onto the schedule because of the cost of the implementation. If New Zealanders want it, we should give it to them. These inequalities result in deaths and disabilities, which are preventable. Just change the slide. Thank you. The awareness or lack of it in 2023, the Health Select Committee report recommended an awareness campaign.

[00:12:45] During the period in the absence of a national campaign from Towharaua, the foundation has led a nationwide awareness campaign. It has produced and distributed 12,500 colouring in books, which are in your handout. It has developed a multilingual symptoms QR code so that people can download the symptoms and have it on their phone all the time. And that improves access to key health information for them. We've distributed 8,500 pens and thousands of information brochures throughout New Zealand universities. Just to change the slide. Thank you. The Managitis Foundation has also engaged with universities across the country via social media. We've been present at O-Weeks and wellness events

[00:13:32] at most universities through New Zealand and have engaged with thousands of students during that process. GSK has driven a television advertising programme which has raised awareness. The inconsistent messaging from medical professionals has evidenced in the feedback from parents of students and those that provide student accommodation, which are in your handout, show that there's a complete, still a lack of awareness around Managitis vaccination and the need for it. The underlying theme of all Managitis cases is simply a lack of awareness. Te Vata Ora are absent in all of the above. Next slide, please.

[00:14:17] Just to be mindful, we're a few minutes over time. So our ask is that we need a bipartisan support for a fully funded vaccination programme. At minimum, eligibility for the vaccination programme should be expanded to include all those who are community services card holders. We're asking for the support of the Managitis Foundation for community services card holders. We're asking for better after care for survivors and refer to the research document in our handout from Wellington University in that. We'd also like improved monitoring and faster notification of cases. So we'd welcome any, thank you for your time and welcome any questions. Thank you. Thank you, Gerard.

[00:15:02] We won't have time for questions, unfortunately, but we don't necessarily know that we need them. It's a horrific story, any. And Gerard, I know from talking previously what this has done to your family. Mr Chair, with your permission, could I be permitted just one very quick question to clarify? Yes, you may. Clarify that. Point two of your ask there. Which age will the community service card holders be in that proposal? Our adolescents. That's the second highest at risk. Ideally, you want all adolescents in that minimum community service card? The best time to implement a vaccination program

[00:15:48] is a school-based program when they're actually, they need to be protected before they go out into the community. Thank you. We thank you for your time. I mean, you people have the chance to make a difference, to save lives, to save disabilities, so we're asking to take that opportunity. Thank you very much for coming to Wellington today and presenting to us. It was a really impactful presentation from you both. Thank you. Thank you.

meetings.title: 2026/04/23 EDSI [Part 2]
meetings.committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
meetings.description: Thursday 23 April 2026 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the External Reporting Board External
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Aotearoa, kaitiakitanga, mana whenua. you you

[00:01:02] John and Wendy, thank you so much for your time. We have half an hour with you. So if you would like to start with some opening remarks, then we will have questions for you. Thank you.

[00:02:24] Thank you for the opportunity to be here today. I'm John Kensington, the chair of the External Reporting Board. I've been on the chair since last August, and I've been a board member by eight years prior to that. I'm joined by Wendy, our chief executive, Wendy Venter, along with Dr Amelia Sharman, our director of sustainability reporting and Dawn Bowden, our general manager corporate services. Now, the XRB sets financial reporting standards, assurance standards and climate reporting standards that provide consistent, internationally comparable information people can trust and use to make decisions. And those standards apply across the entire economy from large listed companies, public sector organizations through to charities and corporate societies and small owner operated businesses.

[00:03:09] It is important to note, we do not regulate or enforce those standards, and we do not decide who they apply to. So Parliament sets the legislative requirements and regulators such as the FMA or Charity Services are responsible for oversight and enforcement. But what we do underpins confidence in New Zealand's economy supports access to global capital, strengthens transparency and accountability across both the public and private sectors. So high quality standards are not an end in themselves. There are means to better government, risk management and capital allocation. Now, as the world becomes more complex and transactions become more complex, reporting becomes more complex. So it's proportionality really matters. And that's why in New Zealand we use the tiered system, so that the large entities can report the information they need to be able to meet international expectations, whereas smaller organizations report information more appropriate to their size.

[00:04:06] So I'll hand over now to Wendy to outline our performance to date and our priorities. Thank you and good afternoon and thank you chair for the opportunity to appear before the committee for this financial review. Just building on what John has said in terms of setting our standards. What we really are trying to do is to make sure our standards are internationally aligned, but also locally relevant and fit for purpose in the New Zealand context. So what does that actually mean in practice? Two things. We try to influence and we do actively influence the international standard setting. So we are relationships there and then make domestic modifications to fit our legal and as John was sort of saying our economic context. I guess the in terms of the 24, 25 year the year under scrutiny, I just wanted to sort of highlight that we did deliver a quite a significant program of work.

[00:04:57] Notably, we sort of amended the climate reporting standards to provide relief to entities facing implementation challenges with some aspects of the reporting and we issued a new standard for the audits of less complex entities. It's called both these examples illustrate our strategic priority of developing fit for purpose standards. The other thing that we do quite a lot of and it has been increasing over the years is supporting guidance and supporting the implementation of our standards by providing guidance. As good examples of that would be over the course of we started it in the 24, 25 years that continues into this year. We are really supporting the rollout of a new standard, a standard that will apply from next year to for profit entities which will change the way profit and performance is measured in financial statements.

[00:05:47] And we also have done a lot of work within corporate societies and helping them understand the requirements of the legislation that was introduced in 2022. Looking looking ahead, there's probably three main strategic priorities that I just wanted to highlight. One was the review of the accounting standards framework that John sort of talked about just now. The others later this year we will be consulting and engaging with our stakeholders on a draft climate reporting roadmap for New Zealand to provide certainty about the future direction there. And the third priority is really the world of emerging technologies and AI is absolutely front of mind for international standards setters and for us. And we just are really focused on understanding the opportunities risks and implications, particularly with the auditing standards has been a focus for the for the current year.

[00:06:41] And this is the final point I just wanted to acknowledge the XRB team. Clearly that's sort of and that's the staff, the boards, the technical boards and really importantly the very many stakeholders who basically contribute their time and expertise to our work. So we could not do this job without that, that engagement. And, you know, in that context really proud that our 24 25 results show that 83% of our stakeholders were overall satisfaction with that we contribute to our purpose of trust and confidence in New Zealand's external reporting. That's it. That's it for me and very much look forward to the committee's questions. Thank you. And that was very nice of you to acknowledge everybody.

[00:07:26] And we have a first question from Marina Williams. Do you want to call to thank you very much for coming in and welcome back Wendy. Just hoping to hone in on the climate related exposures regimes brought in and then face a significant change from government. Firstly, on your role as system steward and exercising some soft influence within the market, how have you seen the market respond to like a level of change and disruption? Yeah. And how do we, how do you sort of measure that? What kind of impact is that having on, you know, the ability to get the right people in the room with these entities, the ability to have a workforce that is prepared to audit for climate?

[00:08:12] What are the flow and impacts? Yeah. No, it's a good question there. It's very much has been, I guess, our focus on the setting the standards with the climate reporting was really sort of understanding how what we could do it lines up with that supporting adoption and implementation. So we've done quite, quite a lot there. We sort of work with preparers assurance right across the ecosystem to go issued a huge, huge amount of guidance over the last ever since the standard came out and Amelia and the team have been very busy with that. What we have seen is essentially any new regime comes with introduces change. There is always that that's the aspect of change and this was a brand, this was a new regime in terms of being mandatory.

[00:09:02] I think it's fair to say it's also really steep learning curve. So that's that's the the targeted changes that I sort of highlighted in my introduction was very much a response to this is actually really tough here. What can we do a standard set to provide a bit of relief and provide a little bit more time for people to adjust. So that's what we were seeing getting data getting the people. What we are seeing what more recently though is people as they start to essentially understand the requirements get to the value of what the standards are there to provide which is in set out in primary legislation which is how you fundamentally thinking about your risk and opportunities from climate change. So we just that that's what we've seen in the in the in the market. It's an ongoing thing we speak anecdotally we get sort of quite a lot of examples of how people are and then respond respond to that.

[00:09:55] So I think that's probably the sort of the overarching comments I would make but haven't quite got got it. Yeah, let me know if you want me to the thing I'm interested in is the quality and the consistency of this. I mean that is your role within the system. And you know have do you have some observations about because really it's not you know you're not reporting on a single figure. A lot of the time you're asking people to respond to questions that are qualitative about their ability to respond to climate challenges. So they need to buy into that. Yeah, so what are you noticing at the moment about the quality and consistency of disclosures is that different from a couple of years ago how do we ensure that when we set a standard that standard is met whatever the standard might be.

[00:10:42] Yeah. So I think the quality you're quite right to highlight that you know it's not like financial information and it is a little bit different we have experience and performance information that's not dissimilar. So what we are definitely finding is that it's really people sort of understanding there are judgments to be made here about what is material. And then for preparers and markets to understand and to do that. So I think it's it's it's really about getting the right metrics that people can have that quality and consistency we are have been in terms of looking at understanding what the changes I did measuring that impact. So I think we started through our work on post implementation review looking at evaluations of how that will roll out so that's where I think we will get a little bit more than anecdotal.

[00:11:31] But yeah, I think that's probably the key thing I would highlight there. Yeah, so pretty much. Yeah. I'm just interested in the audit workforce for climate. Yeah. Is that a mature workforce in New Zealand? Are we training up the right number? You have a girl? Because I might get John to respond. Yeah, my day job. Excuse me, I am an audit partner. So I think back to your first question. One thing I would add is that people have been through it three times more comfortable doing it the third time. There's a learning curve and we've seen them move up the curve quite quickly and quite well. In terms of the audit workforce, it's a very interesting one. There's a lot of training we need to do within the firms to get up to the same skill set levels of an understandings that we have for traditional accounting. If you like to call it that.

[00:12:17] But I think the really interesting thing is certainly the graduates that we're getting now and a large number of more senior staff are actually quite interested in it and want to do it because they might be a little tired of doing the run of the middle order work they've been doing. So we've really had less of a challenge and I think we thought we would getting people to want to look at and be involved in climate reporting. And we've been able to pick up a few experts along the way. And that would be, I'm sure, similar across all the firms. Yes, and I appreciate this question might actually have taught you appreciate previous line of questioning sorry was focusing on environmental reporting.

[00:13:02] I suppose kind of including that actually more widely. How do you measure with the standards? You were your standards are actually improving the quality of reporting not necessarily just the compliance with those standards, but actually that she reporting for the general public for government is actually. Yeah, that in terms of the measurement of the quality of the standards. Yeah, in terms of the outcomes of the reporting. There's probably different things that I would highlight one one that I guess two things the standards are there to sort of say there are some requirements about what information you think is the most useful for the primary user and

[00:13:47] and it's defined usually as the investor and the lender. That's something that's thought about very hard in the design to actually achieve the objectives of, you know, that that reporting. These are most of our standards I should add our international standards. And so what we are really working there to do is actually talk to the users. I think the first the answer the question would be to design it into the requirements of what what what is going to be quality. When when they actually implemented then it starts to get a little bit more broader into actually the whole ecosystem working to go. Okay, what is what does quality look like in terms of compliance. The preparers the assurance the audit that happens actually gives you basically a report on whether it's met the requirements and and then you can go back to that.

[00:14:37] And then the requirements and, you know, the fairness of the financial statements as an example. And I will also emphasize the role of the FMA in terms of monitoring and enforcing it because they sort of give these monitoring reports that give you insight into quality. These disclosure are not quite right here. So all of those factors are sort of factors that basically give you insight into the quality of the reporting. I suppose the question was, I suppose it's a little bit easier to work out whether a standard reporting standard has been met, whether there was but actually what's the quality of the actual standard different to actually whether it was met or not. Right. Are we actually improving the quality of reporting in New Zealand.

[00:15:22] Right. I think by adopting an international standard which is which is principally we are a standard what we call a standard taker. So adopting an international standard that has had the best global minds think about is it should it be done this way. They've gone out, put it out for expression of interests and sort submissions back. And then we do a similar quick process here. I think that probably helps a lot. I think to your point about is reporting improving. That is then the work of the regulator where they then look at the reports and see is the right information getting out here. Are we avoiding financial disruption in the market or were still scandals in the country through the reporting and are people able to invest with some certainty that it's a level playing field for all investors.

[00:16:15] Thank you, Madam Chair. You mentioned in your opening statements around AI and standards in regard to that. Does that mean setting standards for AI or the use of AI and auditing or what's the role there? Yeah, the role not setting standards for AI. The role is very much considering the opportunities and the rest that AI poses for how people go about reporting and the assurance standards. So it's a very much a focus on the assurance standards of our international auditing standard set. And we've been working very closely with them. And we are on a small set of small number jurisdiction advising them going sort of probably three things that that focus is basically embedding a technology focus in any new standard setting.

[00:17:05] Okay, how is technology used today? How can it be done? Going back and looking at some really poor auditing standards as a classic audit evidence and risk response. How do you actually get insight into getting audit evidence risk response when technology is just fundamentally, you know, the, I guess people talk about black box nature. And then the other third thing is actually keeping abreast of what's going on. You know, whether it's the global firms who obviously have, you know, what are we understanding in the market, if you like, in terms of how our reporting organizations using AI, how our auditors using AI. What are the opportunities of this is essentially the innovation, you know, you can get basically lots more insight very rapidly.

[00:17:56] And we're just beginning that. So that's the nature of the worker. That gives you a little bit of a flavor of how we think about AI and technology. Good. Moves, are there any other questions? Very briefly. Yep, I don't know. The availability of what it is with rural problem government two years ago. I'm interested in what you're saying around trends in workforce, not climate. And AI will be a part of that. But is that in fact speeding things up? Is that helping us do more? So I can give a firm perspective here. I think what we've seen is over the last five years. The automation of our audit approaches has meant that we can take last year's file, roll it over, for example, delete all of the.

[00:18:42] Keep the audit program of the works to delete all the information. I think that has resulted in us in us needing less people to do the job. The availability of auditors, it was definitely a problem when it was COVID and no travel. But actually people like to come to New Zealand and see New Zealand for what it is. And the global networks are very much interested in making sure their staff get on these international exchanges and see the world. So it's never going to be easy. There are more glamorous jobs than being an auditor. So I think we're probably back to a stage now where that supply of staff constraint is less of a problem than it was. Sorry, one more thing I would add. I know the professional services organisations like Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand,

[00:19:29] they are looking at sort of widening the pipeline at the bottom, going actually who are the people we're trying to attract into the profession. And actually can we think about the sorts of skills that future accountants and auditors need in a far more holistic, the technology implications, all of that. So I think it's quite a big focus, the attractiveness and retention, I know, wider from a focus. So that's something I just said to what John said. Do we have room for one more? So yes, are you going to give a quick question? Yes, so, Dr. The public sector has gone through this whole process of moving towards outcomes-based performance and now kind of reflecting on yourselves. There's a nice comment in here around looking at non-financial transactions

[00:20:18] and how you assemble information, performance information, that sort of thing. I'm also interested in the VFM side of it, so the financial side of it. So from our perspective, are we getting value from your organisation and the amount of money that we spend in terms of that? So the outcomes, which is what Hamish was referring to, I think, in terms of not only who you're working for, but also yourselves. How do you see that working through your work programme now that you're looking ahead? In terms of non-financial, I just wanted to clarify. Value for money. Value for money. Yeah, absolutely. There's two things, I guess, us as an organisation, which is essentially, and that's been quite central to, you know,

[00:21:04] probably ever since the XRBs established. I've only been Chief Executive since early last year, but I think we are a sort of a current organisation, a current entity. We get public money through our appropriation. Like any organisation, we are, any organisation dealing with the challenges that we've had since COVID and since is what can we do differently? Where do we need to prioritise? What's the most valuable thing we do? We've had real examples over the last few years where we just turned our mind to really focusing on what is our core role here and who can we work with? Who can we leverage, if it's not quite right, collaborate with to achieve even better outcomes?

[00:21:50] So I would say, and we've done very implemented sort of things like using productivity tools in our organisation, rolled out co-pilot, really strongly encouraging people to, you know, how can we just do things and just really embrace the benefits that we can get to just, to get to, as you say, a more efficient, effective outcome? You know, I guess I'm just sort of reiterating where Hamish was going on this, and that is that we want to ask the question, are we getting value from this organisation in terms of the outcome and the output from it? You know, our sense is yes. But some good information around that would be good. Yeah, I guess in a funny way, the counterfactual

[00:22:35] is probably important too. If you didn't have good quality standards, New Zealand's been lucky and we haven't had a financial crisis of the magnitude of any of some other countries. And I think that is probably down to the fact that there has been disciplines in place and required reporting in place, and that reporting has gone through boards and been considered and challenged. So, you know, the counterfactual is take away the accounting standards, let industry use whatever they like. It's a mad max vibration. Yeah, so I think what I'm saying is we need information around how the standards are, as Hamish was saying, being utilised and implanted. Yeah, no, absolutely. No, and it's essentially that point around the performance, how do you get impact?

[00:23:20] Actually, there is an element of what are the broader indicators. We might not have direct line of sight of exactly what is that standard. What can we do to collect that information? I don't understand the point. Okay, Dr Hamish. And probably following on from that, you operate in a rich ecosystem with many different players from the FMA, the Auditor General's Office, Treasury, and will probably produce guidance. How do you ensure that are you working together in this no duplication of effort between different organizations? Yeah, look, for example, probably the best example that is with the FMA. We have an MOU of them. We probably put it in place about three years ago. We talked to them about, hey, please don't,

[00:24:07] you try and set standards. If you want a standard to be set because you think something's not there, come and talk to us and we can do that, and we won't cross across into regulation. And I guess we regularly meet with the players in the ecosystem to make sure we are doing, effectively staying in our lane, I suppose. Yeah. And I suppose that's kind of internal in New Zealand. Talked about being a standard to take care of a regulation taker. Is there a stage where, actually, it's ridiculous New Zealand has its own entity to do this rather than actually just fully taking standards from Australia, the UK, other jurisdictions?

[00:24:53] Yeah. New Zealand has a fairly unique TED system, which ensures the large entities. So banks insurers from Terra, they're reporting all the information there, compatriots overseas. We've also got at the bottom end of that TED system, reporting requirements specifically designed for New Zealand entities. So the risk of going to a jurisdiction and relying completely on another jurisdiction is you'd be stuck with their tearing, which may not really consider some of those bottom-end entities that New Zealand has, or size-wise. Yeah. Great. Looks like that's it. Thank you so much for your time, and thanks for answering questions. And you might have more questions later on from us.

[00:25:39] Thank you. Thank you. Thanks for the opportunity. Thank you very much.

meetings.title: 2026 04 22 - HE
meetings.description: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission Karen Orsborn, Chief Executive Hayden
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Good morning, everybody. It's very good to have you here. How are you? Jim Caron Osborne and Hayden Wainow and Dr Barbara Disley. I'll let you introduce yourselves as well, but I understand we have you here for two hours today. I'll let you open up with some introductory remarks, such as an introduction, and then I'll have the two members to ask questions over to you. Thank you for making the time. Thank you. Thank you for the opportunity to allow us to present our last year's work, and perhaps

[00:00:47] a look into what we're looking into in the future. As many of you will know that we were set up following the inquiry into mental health and addiction back in 2018, and we came into form in February 21. So six years of existence at the time, the Commission was set up to be a force for change, helping to lead a shift to a mental health wellbeing system that is accountable and is experienced as joined up, respectful, responsive, by tongue to fire order, their families, and whānau. The system is serving a population now that is poorer in mental health and wellbeing,

[00:01:32] an increasing need for mental health support compared to six years ago. There have been improvements. More staff in place due to filling vacancies has been a key driver to improve access rates and reduced wait times. Over 236,000 people have seen access to the Access and Choice Program in 2024, but less than the expected target of 325,000 per annum. However, for young people, access to specialist mental health services reduced, they faced longer wait times, had more referrals, declined. This is particularly concerning given the rate of psychological distress that is increasing for young people. In 2024 and 25, was the coming over age for

[00:02:22] Te Hiringa Mahata? Our system performance monitor showed a system new linking outcomes for people with contributions from the Government. Our first system performance monitoring report outlined what a good mental health and addiction system looks like. The report shows that while there are pockets of positive change, there is a significant way to go to achieve the outcomes people expect. Our annual stakeholder engagement survey told us there is an overall positive deception of Te Hiringa Mahata. Stakeholders rated us highly on our engagement, reliability of advice, reports and information that we publish, and speaking up on important

[00:03:07] mental health, addiction and wellbeing issues. Māori, iwi and lived experience stakeholders report positive engagement with us. We can see the impact of our work in three key areas, the Access and Choice Program, the work to repeal and replace the Mental Health Act, and the new Mental Health and Wellbeing Strategy. The launch of the Access and Choice Program represented a major investment in mental health and addiction. Our monitoring has provided transparency and accountability. In response to our findings, we saw greater investment in targeted action and improvement in data quality

[00:03:54] and visibility. Since our establishment, we have advocated for a mental health and addiction system that places people and whānau at the centre. Central to this has been our support for the work to repeal and replace the Mental Health Act. We support legislative and practice changes that uphold the rights, people's rights, to make informed decisions about their care and treatment. We have advocated for stronger recognition of mental health and wellbeing within legislation. Our evidence-based advice made the case for a dedicated mental health and wellbeing strategy anchored in research and shaped by the voices of people with lived experience. Our engagement has been acknowledged and actively sought with the Minister for

[00:04:39] Mental Health supporting our ongoing involvement in the development of the mental health and wellbeing strategy. In the coming year ahead, our 2526 is our first year under our new strategy and statement of intent. We have used strategic priorities focused on mental health and addiction services for tamata whāia ora and outcomes for people with lived experience and on improving the performance of the mental health and addiction system. We're focusing our efforts over the next three years through making three strategic shifts on how we work, placing people with lived experience at the centre of our work, being future-oriented, promoting alignment and

[00:05:28] leveraging knowledge to influence positive change, and being a bold advocate. The first Hauora Heningaro Tiara Tu Rua forum was a success with over 240 attendees. Feedback was positive, particularly about the timely announcement regarding crisis response funding. Most delegates surveyed found the program relevant, topical and useful. The publication of Urupare Moreria Crisis Response Monitoring Report was a significant milestone. Analysis of media cover showed 23 articles across major outlets in an estimated reach of 2.8 million impressions. We published the assessment of progress

[00:06:15] implementation of kōtimata to Haringa recommendations. This is the first time that we have assessed progress with recommendations that we've made. Of the five recommendations made in early 2024, three have been completed by Health NZ and the government. Two others are then worked on. This year, in 2026, the Minister for Mental Health will undertake a review of our factors that was built into the legislation from the beginning and the operations of the Haringa Mahara. Our case studies highlight the system is shifting. We are a trusted voice in speaking up on important health, a mental health addiction, and well-being issues. We recognise much more needs to be done

[00:07:05] and will take time, sustained leadership and action. The new mental health and well-being strategy and our ongoing oversight, monitoring and advocacy will be critical to seeing the change people and whānau want to see. Thank you. Thank you very much for that. Some questions and good lyrics. Yeah, thank you very much and lovely to see you all. I'm interested to pick up some of the themes that you mentioned in your introduction and clear that when you say a trusted voice, that certainly is the perceptions in the sector. I'm curious to know some things from a systems perspective that your commission has highlighted. One of the things we asked you about a number of

[00:07:53] years ago was leadership in the mental health sector. I'm wanting to get a sense from you whether you feel that the leadership is there and I'm thinking also about accountability given that we have more agencies who are supposed to or have a statutory duty to provide some accountability. But when we ask them all, fingers go the other way or they just isn't the legislative teeth. And in fact, when I look, I see accountability is only really at a contracted level, which are very output-orientated. So there's two systems levels questions there. One is the leadership there. Secondly, are you satisfied that the accountability are baked into the system in the way they should be around mental health? Let me start. I think we can all probably have a bit of a go at that question. It's a big one.

[00:08:42] In terms of leadership, we've seen some change in improvement, particularly within Health New Zealand. And we've been able to actively engage at the governance and operation, senior operational level. So we've seen a deficit, I suppose, of leadership in that space that has been filled in recent times. In terms of accountability, I think there's still ways to go. We think that the mental health and wellbeing strategy is an important one, that it has an overlay that includes within it milestones and accountabilities that you refer to. I can give one example of where system change leadership, we particularly paid a role in

[00:09:34] that is in the crisis response space. There was a lack of clear leadership across the various different agencies, police, justice and health. And we managed to fill avoid. And I think as a result of that, there was some progress made and then governments subsequently made some investments. So change is happening in pockets, but I would still say in terms of overall accountability and a joint up system, we have long ways to go. And as I said in that introduction, it's going to require time and sustained leadership for us to get there. That's a starter, but all Karen. Thank you for that. And I guess that leads me into a pivot, if you like, or a focus now on youth. And I know that in your latest report, we're still seeing youth having

[00:10:22] longer wait times to access specialist services. And in fact, we're seeing longer wait times for addiction services as well. So I'm interested to know whether the focus on youth is, whether you're able within the current architecture to include some of the really important things like social determinants and other elements that might make a significant difference lifting that access and engagement. I think there's two parts to your question. In terms of this part of your question. Sorry, can you clarify that? So the best part is around the wait times for 19 to 24 year olds. So if we're unpacking, we're unpacking it up, that is probably the best one line statement we can

[00:11:10] say. What we're finding for young people is the referrals are being made, but they're being declined. And so understanding what that's about is important to us. Is that work happening? Do you know that the tracking of the wait times? Okay, I can comment on that. So we've had a long-term focus on rangatahi and young people looking at both what are the drivers for the increasing rates of distress in that sort of determinant space and reported on the well-being outcomes rangatahi and youth as well as access to services. So both is really important. So we are, you've seen youth reflected in our crisis and other work, and we are doing a deep dive this year to really try and understand those pathways across the system, but also the access patterns.

[00:11:58] So what we have seen is, as Haley said, a continued decline in the numbers of young people accessing specialist services. And there are more options in primary and community, but we're still hearing a lot about the concerns that people have about young people not being able to get access when they need it. So we need to understand how that whole system is working. Oh yeah, but I want to stop on the same line. Thank you. Just final stop, thank you for that. Last night when I asked the minister about this, he said that people are being referred to different organizations or different specialists. That's why there are these declines. Have you seen evidence that young people are actually

[00:12:44] being deferred into services and receiving the treatment, or is that a statement that needs some evidence behind it? Yeah, look, we have heard that referrals are being declined because there's more appropriate services. And we know there are more options in those primary and community services, particularly like the the youth stream of access and choice and other options. We don't fully understand the detail that sits behind that, which is what we're hoping to understand more in the deep dive that we're doing this year. So there is something happening in terms of that 18 to 24 age group who are accessing adult services. So wanting to understand what's happening within services and why young people, that age group has continued to decline while adults has

[00:13:32] increased. So there is something under there that we need to understand. So we're looking at it. Which is what my sub was going to be about, is that transition from youth to adult and being able to understand what is the gap or the blockage for that transition, if you can have any comment on that? Yeah, look, we haven't particularly looked at that transition. And so that is part of the work we're doing this year as part of the deep dive looking at young people and how that transition works. Because we have heard many young people are all different. And so for some, they may be more ready to transition into adult services. Others might prefer to actually be continued to be supportive in the services they're currently receiving. So that as said, that's what we're looking at this year. Right, so you're going to

[00:14:20] create to understand what that blockage or yes, yes. And so in our work, what we do is we have the board brush approach to monitoring. And then we highlight the areas where we need to have a closer look. And last year, we was really clear that crisis responses was an area we needed to have a closer look. So we did that deep dive last year. And this year, it's really having a look at those youth services. And that's how we work as an entity is to say, where do we need to have a closer look? And so that there's a gap in that transition. But at the moment, yeah, that's what we need to understand more. And that's what we add as value to the system is being able to look at those deep dives, bring some international evidence. What is working well in New Zealand? What can we learn across the system? So

[00:15:06] that's that's what we're doing. Thank you, Mr Chair. And thank you for being here and for your Mahi. I know that Arangatahi have some of the worst statistics for mental health and the OECD. And I want to talk about your advocacy role, particularly with other ministries and ministers. So we know that the new minister stamp for its new curriculum, particularly the health of physical education curriculum, does not mention the words mental health at all. How have you advocated to the minister or the Ministry for Education to have mental health within the curriculum? We haven't had any specific advocacy

[00:15:56] when we have those relationships across government. And certainly we know that other government entities agencies draw from from our work. And so we certainly advocate for youth voice so for young people to have a voice and whatever changes are being proposed. But also for young people to have greater knowledge about mental health and wellbeing, what they can do to keep themselves well and safe. So yeah, we certainly advocate for that. Thank you, Supplementary. And so do you think it is vital for youth and Anata to get support and understanding through their education? At school? Yeah, this is personal response. Yes. Have we done any

[00:16:45] particular work in a specific area where we haven't? And supplementary. And so does the Ministry for Education or the Minister Education reached out to you for that advice? I'm not aware of that. Thank you. And one more. If that's okay. It's okay. Thank you. So has there been any discussion or thoughts around having a cross ministry group that meets, like the group that was set up after Te Aurete Kura for the Prevention of Family and Sexual Violence was set up where they came together, talked about mental health, particularly around youth for this case? Has there been any work or thoughts around that? With the focus on education? With the focus on youth mental health?

[00:17:36] I'm not that I'm aware of that. Do you think that, yeah? The closest piece of work that we've been involved in there would be in the process response space. And there have been some short term ministry-led initiatives, but not specifically on this co-poper. There has been work across government and in previous terms of government with drawing to either different ministers with different portfolios to focus on, so the child and youth are well-being. Yeah. Thank you. Very much. That's a break. Thank you. During the year you've updated your strategy, what is the main shifts in your priorities and

[00:18:24] what are they based on? Good question. I think a number of factors came to play. I'll start ball rolling, but a number of factors came to play for us. One, we found our straps, I suppose, you could say, in a startup 60 years ago. So we were clear about what our purpose was, and we were clear about the resources that we had. The other part, of course, is that we utilised public service to assist us in engaging with stakeholders. So we listened to a lot of people in effect and helped shape our strategy. So knowing what we were about, knowing the resources that we had, what we heard, that brought us into identifying

[00:19:12] six areas to shift where from to, and I think, as carers to cover off on those three areas, but they're quite important for us in terms of driving the decisions that we make, or take on an elsewhere side of the organisation. So last year we did undertake what's called a future excellence horizon, which is common across government. So we interviewed a range of stakeholders and also conducted a survey to get feedback from people about if we were the best organisation we could be, what that would look like. And so what we heard was that we needed to place people's experience much more centered in our work. We needed to work to improve a fragmented system.

[00:19:59] So we'd heard a lot about the fragmentation and the lack of leadership. We needed to lift our profile. So we heard quite a lot about our visibility and that we needed to look ahead and think about the demographic changes that we're happening. And so that then led into the refocus of our strategy and our 24-25 annual report. Reports against our previous four strategic priorities. So what we're now working to is a refined set of strategic priorities, which is about focusing on people with lived experience, people who are using inter-health and prediction services, so much more focused on services, continuing to have a focus on broader well-being outcomes and determinants. And the new one is really how we

[00:20:46] look at the performance of the mental health and addiction system. And as we've mentioned previously, we did release our first system performance report last year. We are about to release our second one this year in June. And that is where the six shifts that we identified. So we said that the system needs to shift in these ways. And that's really informed the mental health and well-being strategy. So happy to take further questions on that. Just a quick step. In multiple of your answers, you've talked about the stakeholders. Who do you see your stakeholders? Who do you see those people or stakeholders? Who are they? Oh, they're really broad. We have multiple stakeholders. So of course, it's people who have lived experience and the whānau families who support them. And that's a really broad

[00:21:36] and diverse group. We have obligations to Māori and whānau as part of our legislation and how we engage with Māori and whānau and prioritise equity for Māori. We have some really important sector stakeholders. So working with people who are providing services, commissioning services to achieve the changes that we want to see across government. We also have stakeholders in terms of what we describe as the priority populations. So in our act, there are a list of 12 populations who have higher prevalence of distress. And so it's important that we connect with those groups as well. So Māori for such like young people,

[00:22:22] veterans as they're at list. So it's a really broad group and we're a small entity of around 20 staff. So we have to really think smartly about how we connect in with organisations that represent those populations and with the providers. So both Health New Zealand and NGOs and other providers. So it's a big challenge for us how we keep that commission. I appreciate you've got your priority populations. And I think last year when you're here, I did ask about middle-aged males. They're the group that most are most likely to die by suicide. What are you doing to reach out to stakeholders in that group? Because I suppose one of my fears is that some of these people aren't accessing

[00:23:12] mental health support. And so probably very hard for you to pick up because they're not they're not engaging with services. Yeah and I think that's the relationships we have with other agencies and organisations who are connected with those groups because it's hard for us to have the direct reach into those communities given the nature of our organisation and our size. So we do have you know that intentional engagement with the groups that represent. So the rural sector or if it's Pacific peoples. So how do we have those connections and then also more broadly like the mental health foundation and those organisations.

[00:23:57] Hayden during your initial briefing you mentioned that case studies show that the system is shifting. Is that in relation to these priorities or were you meaning something different? I was referring to the areas that we've seen in the legislation. Taking up of the strategy for mental health and well-being. Focus on youth and we're also talking about access and choice. So those are the areas that we particularly where we've worked on where we can see some contribution to where those areas of work have shifted. Just to clarify the system shift you're talking about are mainly inputs in terms of

[00:24:50] ledges sort of change and systems change rather than outcomes which would take some time to measure. Of course. Thank you. Thank you chair. I read your statement of intent that was published in last year July but I couldn't find anything that talked about the needs of the FX community. In the past five years the risk of mental health issues like depression have been significantly increased in Asian communities. Do you think this is a serious concern that lead more attention? Yes we do. We're not reflecting necessarily all of that work that's been done

[00:25:42] but there's some work that's been done through Karen and the team and that will in time surface but that's Karen's in a better position to articulate what we're doing. I mean if you think it's a very serious concern why not a single wording or single strategies have been addressing the needs of our Asian community? It probably reflects the fact that we weren't there at that particular time but it's not to say you know it's it's basically saying we're not we can't get to anything at once so but having said that we're now in a position and we are starting to move into making connections in that particular area. So you're trying to tell me that you don't think there's a needs for Asian community before? We knew that. You knew but

[00:26:32] you didn't react? Well again with the small organization like trying to do the best that we can in terms of reaching if you look at all 12 population groups that quite extensive and require quite a lot of effort and resource to engage in in a appropriate way and follow through. So at that particular time it wasn't we clearly weren't in a position to articulate anything. We are in a better position to do that now. All right so what do you think are the main reason affecting the mental health in our afflux community? Like how does the mental health and wellbeing commission support a speaker for them? So I've had a lot of engagement particularly with Asian family services and so we have supported the summits that Kelly and her team have hosted

[00:27:23] both in Auckland and then the one in in Parliament so and we do look at how we reflect in Asian communities Asian population in our substantive reports. So for example in our access and choice report we highlighted the needs for Asian people to have access to those access and choice services and in our other reports we also highlight one of the needs for Asian people alongside Maori and Pacific. So we certainly have done work to connect with both Asian family services and other key leaders from the Asian community and to reflect that in our work. Have you removed the barrier for the Asian community to access to mental health services?

[00:28:09] We removed the barrier. Any barrier or you just don't you just believe that there's no barrier for for any afflux groups or Asian communities to access to the mental health service? Well our role is to highlight what's happening in terms of access and need and then to advocate for that and in terms of for Asian the Asian population we have been reflecting those needs and doing that advocacy in our work and I think this does highlight the challenges I've been said you know there are 12 priority populations and it's hard to reflect each of those populations specifically particularly in a statement of intent which is around us as an entity but in our substantive work we certainly do everything to bring forward those voices in

[00:28:59] our work. If you think if you just mentioned that your main function is to advocate you know advocate for people or give advices what advice have you given to the minister so far to address the needs of our Asian communities and also are you happy with the result you have been implemented in the last 12 months so far if not which areas do you think it needs to improve? We have raised the issue with the minister we haven't got a clear pathway forward but we have raised it as an important area that needs attention and specifically in relation to the Access and Choice Programme and their report at the end of five years and we did highlight the need for a service stream that was specific for our Asian population and the minister responded to

[00:29:46] that in a positive way. Just a quick comment and then I'm going to ingrid for a suck and Cam also has a suck. Just let you know I'm afraid this up. How long has it been? And then Kahu has her primary. Oh sorry was a suck. I'm actually not a primary thank you. Quick comment. Just let you know I'm afraid this is a point of the answer you put by today and I expect a better outcome in the next 12 months. Thank you. Thank you for that speaking to Dr Kelly Fing it seems that one of the problems is that you don't know what you don't know when it comes to the Asian mental health issues and gambling in particular so first is that a piece of work that is when you talk about the work that you're doing Karen is that what you're looking at because that baseline

[00:30:36] has to be missing and that's probably a big part of why they don't get funding that I think Dr Chung is alluding to is not proportionate to the problem we can't count it. The second part is with access and choice are you aware that the access and choice solution provided by the government is is not the same as the other strands of access and choice is convoluted and therefore makes it really problematic for Asian communities to actually access access and choice and if so what would you like to see done about that? Yeah so just on that the second part I'm aware that the the solution for that that Asian stream access and choice is different from the other streams and that reflects that

[00:31:21] those other streams we're right from the beginning and this is a a solution to improve access and I know that's um I haven't talked with Kelly recently but you know that some aspects of that are quite challenging and those do need to be worked through I think ultimately it is about people being able to access the services supports in a timely way so it is about the specific streams and it is also about the appropriateness of all services for people who are accessing those services um the um you know the Asian population is is made up of different types of population as it is for Pacific peoples as well so it is how do we yeah as said have the specific streams and services that meet everybody's needs in the most appropriate way and so is the

[00:32:07] baselining of the how big is the problem which is a question and is difficult if the data is only being collected from small data sets of who is accessing and what we're seeing on the ground is a much bigger problem is that a gap in the system and is that something that you are working at all? Yeah that goes to the prevalence study so we have advocated for a proper sort of study of the level of need in communities and we're speaking particularly about the Asian community. Yes well that would that would include what's the level of need for um for Asian communities as well as other communities so the the youth stream is underway and that should give us a really good insights into what is the level of need for um for the all of those populations and then how can services be targeted to meet those needs and particularly for the youth does pick up um that's

[00:32:57] part of those changing demographics so um you see that particularly in the at that age group so that will give us some really useful information um but not only us for the whole system so we have a role to play um but we're not the commissioners and funders of services so we're bringing that oversight so we have views and insights and we can feed those in um I think you I think you can say that we are increasing our presence in this area and you will have some uniformness thank you. Cam. Oh thank you chair. Something can be true. Chair, there's a supplementary for your primary. The questions that we've had in this light of question in this light of question we have focused on groups of people um specifically you know heard some some um strong advocacy and questions from um Dr Chulman Asian um Dr Campbell on middle

[00:33:50] aged men do you think that labelling listening categorizing the way it's done in this act when you're enabling piece of legislation is actually um making your job harder when a population is divided up and everyone starts fighting who do you think that it's actually that the amount of trust in what you're able to do is being impacted because of the dividing people up and clear the breach of a social contract where we all come together to support each other. Order you think politicians are dividing us. Yeah look I think is it making your job easier or harder? I think it's helpful speaking to the to be to be you know to be I think if we're looking at it from a general how we apply

[00:34:38] up the resources that we have um that gives us recognizes that not one shoe uh or one solution fits all so but it does provide a challenge for us to when it comes to applying that in practically there are some challenges for us to be able to move effectively across all of those bases. Is that because there's contradictions in the act between things like I think it's section 10 where you can as a commission can decide populations you want to focus on but then also you've got clause 13 schedule two with these lists of populations you have to justify um actually looking at wider population basis like is that is this making I'm guess I'm trying to figure out like you've heard some emotion I would say there's a lot of people who would read this legislation

[00:35:25] and yeah you can say it's politician and say are you actually able to look at the population and identify based on what you find what do you feel directed by to these population bases. I had a comment because it is one of the shifts that we've made in our strategy so when we started you know in the early period of time we did do a report focused on the well-being outcomes for Pacific peoples to focus on a particular population group now that report was incredibly well received by the Pacific community um but when we've looked at our resources and as you know we're you know five million dollars and and 20 staff we and the we what we went through with the future excellence horizon the advice was that we needed to be much more focused on people

[00:36:12] with lived experience and people who are accessing services and so we have made a shift to um and it was a challenging shift for us to make because we would like to be able to do a report like that on each of the priority populations but we simply don't have the resources to enable us to do that so we have focused on the people who are um accessing services who are using services and the people with distress and trying to bring the voices of those different priority populations into that work so it is hard to disaggregate because it's not that um you know you can have people have more than one of those or can be categorized into more more than one of those categories so it's not just a straight distinction um so it is a challenge for us that we've had

[00:37:01] to grapple with thank you mr chair um well we know too many asian people and arty or don't feel welcome and have um mental health and my my colleague from the national party dr callous chung um talked about the need for culturally appropriate services and getting support populations um who are disproportionately affected for example people living in rural areas like farmers um when we're talking about asian family services uh it could look from the outside like they get quite a lot of funding um through other um services um but we know that those that funding is ring
