[00:37:49] fenced for gambling um and that that means that other um mental health services um which maybe are more needed um in different areas are not able to be funded because it is being fenced for gambling and so my question is around that access and um peer support and what are your suggestions for more flexible contracts um and reactive um contracts for for the need um what's your advice to funders of um mental health services yeah and look i that generally we see a lot of our advice um that

[00:38:35] we provide is his own debt policy makers and decision makers and we think the levers of commissioning are really important leaders for change so we don't have our hand directly on those levers but what we can do is shine the light by bringing information to to uh to bear so that hopefully that influences an impossible way and i think it's important that we're not seeing as the fixes of issues because that's just not what we're made no we're designed to do so uh commissioning is a really important area of for change as i've said and i think the kinds of things that you're looking for usually particularly if you want community-based responses is high trust uh flexibility and and ten year term clear on being clear on the expectation

[00:39:28] and clear on the outcome that you're looking for really clear steps and measures that are put in place uh and um allowing that community to determine the best way so you're not using that type lose type principle in clear on what you're looking for being clear and what you're measuring and allowing that community to shape but what the response needs to be i'll just make another comment there but in terms of the way we are looking at data or the systems looking at data um in terms of being clearer about the impact across different ethnicities age groups are the factors that might um play into this space it is that that's really important to allow us to be able to look down into those various um variables about what are the presenting

[00:40:20] needs what's the difference being made what are the things that would make a difference for particular populations or age groups and what might we be doing about it in commissioning space and i guess what we're seeing is um a higher level of focus on some of those different categories which may not have been there in the past but does allow greater information and so as access and choice and those community programs roll out we're in a better position now to see who's getting access and who isn't and what might need to be done about that and i think that's the area we'll be in in the next few years thank you mr chair i'd like to ask some questions which

[00:41:09] i intended uh to get from you a model for me to better understand your um your and other mental health data in the future i notice that your um letter of expectation includes analysis of current resourcing against population needs to inform future areas of focus at a system level often when we talk about health service planning we have a conceptual model of how we might measure need in the community and therefore the service response when we read reports like the one you recently published 202.9 percent of people age 15 to 24 experience psychological high or very high levels

[00:42:01] of psychological distress can you please explain the conceptual model that sits behind that measurement or tell me another measurement but the measurement of need and what the services could or should be how do you link that so what we've used in terms of that 22.9 percent is using the health survey results so that as you know has limitations so what what we so that's being used as a proxy to say what what do we um what's the best information we have about the level of need but it's the

[00:42:47] prevalence study will give us far better information about the actual level of need and prevalence i don't want to get a quick into a discussion about on Mr Deputy Chair um if i may uh i don't want to get a discussion about measurement and methods um what i'm asking is whatever good measurement you use yeah how will you match that or link it to service need does every person who reports experiencing distress need a clinical service um or not and can you explain that link to me so what i would say is our work is um in terms of uh that system performance view is is and i'll

[00:43:40] refer back to the system performance work as we had our first report last year which is where we're and it might not be the one that you're referring to but we're trying to say what are the population level outcomes for for all people and you know we're the well-being for all people in RTI and how do you then draw connections back into the system and into the service contribution and and there's not that direct connection through but what we're trying in terms of attribution you know as many other factors that sit outside of the mental health system um financial crisis um you know wars all of those things actually influences influence people's mental health and well-being so we're trying to draw that that um connection but

[00:44:29] the report from last year was year one um and that work will develop over time but it's trying to develop those insights and knowledge about um where we need to do more work and to start to paint the picture so i can um i think i understand we're trying to go with that but it's we've got a long way to get to before we can really say what is the level of need and what is the appropriate response be it in a all-of-government type approach in terms of improving education employment or is it about um what are the services because it's not everybody needs a service in that way it could be more um preventative action some of the well-being support that can be provided so that's the complexity of what we're trying to understand

meetings.title: 2026 04 22 FEC
meetings.description: This is "2026 04 22 FEC" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Welcome back, Sarah and David. It's great to have you. We've got you for up to 30 minutes. We'll just maybe let you kick off with some introductory comments and things and we'll hand over for some Q&A. Okay, thank you. I'm Teina Kotakatua. I'm David Woods, the Chair of New Zealand Green Investment Finance and I'm joined by our Chief Executive, Sarah Minhinik. We were established to accelerate investment in New Zealand's decarbonisation and emissions reduction to help address a perceived market failure. One of our key aims was to secure co-investment of private capital to address the scale of the climate investment challenge. As you're aware, in April 2025, our shareholding ministers announced that the government had decided to wind down ends it gives existing portfolio of investments and ultimately exit

[00:00:46] those investments over time. Ministers' expectations were and continue to be that we will meet all legal obligations and operate in a financially sustainable and self-funding manner during the wind down period. We're well advanced in that process and are achieving good results. We've returned total capital to the crown of over $500 million since our wind down plan was approved by our shareholding ministers both through cancelling uncalled shares and through cash distributions of $216 million. There is still work to do as we have some remaining investment positions to exit. These are complex structures that take time to manage. However, the board is confident in the team that we have and their ability to deliver the best outcome for the taxpayer in the circumstances. We will answer all questions from the committee as fully as possible

[00:01:32] but we request members understanding that we must protect legally and commercially sensitive information. Thank you. Thank you very much for the work you've done. Obviously the committee is really interested to know more about your wind down process, what that looks like, particularly around existing investments, what's happening with those and also I think looking through the questions from you, there were 16 staff in terms of the transitional arrangements and what's happening with the staff just to start with. If I start with the staff, there were 16 I think when we were here last year, there are four today. The board is concerned to keep those staff

[00:02:20] in place because of the complexity of the exit process from the remaining investments. Thank you. In terms of detail, I can't really talk about individual investments and how we've exited individual investments. We've sold about two-thirds of our total investments since the wind down plan was approved. We have a small number of both equity and debt investments. The total commitment still outstanding is about $80 million if I take undrawn commitments and investments. So of the investments that you have been able to sell, how many of them have been sold into New Zealand ownership? Sarah, do you want to? Almost everything. Okay, that's great. In terms of the hole that's going to be left in the market,

[00:03:13] do you see that being built by other entities or do you think there will be a gap? Sorry if that's too difficult for you to answer. I think it's a policy question that we the government has to address. Fair enough. Someone else can go and look and beckon. Thank you, David, and to yourself for the work you've done in winding this down and to your team. I know it must be a testing time for them as they go through the process. Just in terms of timelines to get that hopefully $80 million or so, what's your expectations or the minister's expectations around when that would be liquidated and returned to government? We're in discussion with

[00:04:03] Treasury and with the shareholding ministers. We expect the divestment stage of the process will finish during the next financial year. So the year to June 2027. We'd like it to finish sooner, but it's really a question of the process we have to go through. For instance, most of our equity positions have preemptive rights and that takes a time. So we're working through it as quickly as we can. And are you able to give us indication of I guess your investments? You'd have like a book value on those investments versus the realised value. Is there much difference in between those two? And if so, give us a sense of that. Let Sarah take the detail,

[00:04:52] but the board is very satisfied that exiting as close to power as we can and the rest of them. Yeah, the exits have been pleasing. We were concerned this time last year when we were in the planning phases of the process that we could achieve elegant exits that would deliver value back to the Crown, but also be done in a way that was palatable for the portfolio companies. And I think we've helped that balance quite nicely. If we put solar zero to one side, because that's still ongoing, our blended recovery rate has been around 83 to 84 percent on average. Some of them have obviously been much higher. The recoveries on the debt have been very good. And I think that that is appropriate given that the portfolio was not in breach.

[00:05:45] And for these assets, we are a required seller. So there will be a discount naturally. Yeah. Thank you very much. Todd Stephenson. Thanks for being here. Have you given any thought obviously over the life of the organisation and even in the windout? There's probably quite a lot of learnings and insights, et cetera. Have you given any thought to how that could be passed on, whether it's to Treasury or another central agency, so that what you've learned over the life of the organisation isn't kind of We're archiving all our records and we're happy to, at an appropriate time, have debriefing sessions with various people. But at the moment, I don't think that time is now. No, but you've obviously given us a thought, which I think is good. Because I think,

[00:06:31] yeah, one of the things, again, quite often, the insights and experiences are lost across governments. I think just even giving that some thought is great. And we can follow up on that as well. I was actually going to ask a similar question. And you have a huge amount of knowledge about what is going to be a really important investment sector in New Zealand's future. So I'm really pleased to know that those insights are being captured. And I'm aware this might be the last time you come to the SLEE Committee. So thank you very much for your work. Just in terms of the wind down plan, just like what are the significant tasks that you say in that row ahead? And what do you see as some of the more significant tasks you still need to

[00:07:16] complete? I think we're still in the mode of selling assets one by one. When we started, could we sell the whole portfolio? That wasn't an option that was available at a realistic price. So we have been divesting assets individually. We will probably not be able to sell all of them. So there will be a remaining set of assets. And we're in discussions with Treasury about what needs to happen then. Apart from the physical investments, then we have to wind down all our premises, our lease costs, silly things like subscriptions. But the whole thing has to be wound out and that will take a couple of months. Yeah. So I mean, obviously you

[00:08:04] can't go into the details of specific assets. And I get that. But in terms of those discussions with Treasury about what will happen with the remaining assets, are you able to give us some options in a broad level that have been canvassed? I think it's really something that we need to ask to address. We are in detailed discussions with them. Yeah. Just going back to one of your earlier answers, Sarah, it was you, I think that said you'd kind of been sort of 84, 85% recovery on the value. It's fair to say that people know you have to sell, isn't it? So that there is a discount. In terms of the way we could have done this, was there, do you think there were all two different ways? Obviously, you bypassed the portfolio approach

[00:08:54] because you saw there was going to be too much of a haircut taken by taking that approach. But if we've done this over a longer period of time, obviously holding on to the assets in different ways in different parts of government, do you think we could have actually preserved some of the equity or if the equity were done it differently? To be honest, I haven't really gone there in a great deal of thought because it was a policy decision and we were just tasked with executing it. These are decisions for the minister, so I appreciate that. But thank you for your work. Thanks, Megan. We're going to go to Dr Dave Lawson. Yeah, just a learning question really, giving your reflection on it about scope and complementarity with other funds,

[00:09:40] such as the Provincial Growth Fund and of course then it morphed into the Regional Infrastructure Fund. Sorry, David, we're back here. Can you talk into the microphone? Yes. Thank you. Did you hear all of that? Yes. So yeah, that's the question, the complementarity and scope question because there's some learnings there, I'm sure, where we're crossing over each other or there certainly are learnings. We have had detailed discussions with Treasury about different areas and complementarity, but again, I'm afraid it's a policy question. Yeah, I understand that. Really, it's from our perspective, it's a learning question. What did you learn from that environment? I think on a more general level, we're very friendly with all of our

[00:10:30] opposite numbers at all of these places. So David and I regularly talk to our counterparts at ECAR and NZGCP and Kanawa and our RK, various other places, and there's quite a good spirit, I would say, amongst the execs and the board members. So I suppose to the extent that's helpful, that has been an ongoing theme of people engaging with each other. Right. It's really following on from your question earlier, Megan, framing it slightly differently. Maybe a question for me, possibly for you as the Chair, David, with regard to some of the entities which you're potentially going to struggle to completely wind down, do you anticipate a transfer of that value into another government entity via Treasury's guidance or something like that?

[00:11:20] I think that that is clearly an option, but it's a, we will continue selling divesting assets until we're told to stop and we'll then deal with whatever's left over. I'm just mindful that you've got an end date on June 30th or June 26th, that's that runways running short. Is that? No, I think. But you speak to everything to be materially completed by the 30th of June? I think that's what we thought a year ago, but our new SPE has it running until the end of this year, potentially early into the calendar year next year, but we would like to have it finished by the end of this year. So we may in fact see you again next year.

[00:12:05] I don't know. Well, thank you very much for that. We do appear to be at the end of our questions. So thank you for the ongoing work and the updates. Do they really appreciate it?

meetings.title: Education and Workforce Committee
meetings.description: This is "Education and Workforce Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Aotearoa, kaitiakitanga, mana whenua. Thank you. you

[00:01:02] and Michael Kilkalli joining us. Karen, we have 10 minutes with you this morning.

[00:01:57] That includes time for questions. Pass it over to you now. Thank you, Maurena Koutou. We're both members of the National Employment Law Committee of the New Zealand Law Society, Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa. I'm a barrister in Wellington. I've got over 30 years' experience in employment law, which covers two holidays acts and two decades in large law firms. And Michael is an associate with Meredith Connell in Auckland and prior to that he was a judge's clerk in the Employment Court in Auckland. The approach taken by the New Zealand Law Society when commenting on proposed legislation is a neutral one in terms of policy. So we're seeking to provide you with feedback on the workability of the legislation, identifying potential issues and drafting.

[00:02:43] And as law is our natural view, our natural review mechanism is to think about how we might explain it to our clients. Our written submission makes a number of comments that are directed to the predictability and certainty of the proposed legislation, but we'll focus only on three of those points today and then leave a few minutes for questions from you. So I'll start with a standard hours point that we make in our submission. The bill is premised on standard hours being specified in an employment agreement, roster or notional roster. And that's the baseline or foundation for annual leave, public holidays, bereavement leave, sick leave, domestic violence leave, et cetera. So it's really important as a concept in this draft legislation.

[00:03:28] So just to start with a practical issue, at the moment, standard hours isn't required in employment agreements. The employment relations that brings in more flexibility than that and it refers to a requirement to include an indication of the arrangements relating to the times the employer is to work. So you see some quite flexible and fluid clauses at the moment in employment agreements. So there's going to be some transition needed if we go with a standard hours notion from what currently is quite flexible and where many employment agreements won't have standard or set hours specified. And it's not quite clear how that transition is going to be addressed. This leads to the second issue about standard hours, which is the bill requires that but doesn't provide a fallback mechanism if the hours aren't specified in any of those ways.

[00:04:21] So where the employer and employee simply don't agree and they stick with their current employment agreement, what happens then? The third issue is whether the standard hours truly reflect what the employee works. And I listened earlier this morning to some of the industry submissions which have explained the implication of that for how the time off part of leave is calculated. For the Law Society, our concern is two-fold. First is that the standard hours might not reflect the current reality. But second, the shall we say less honourable employers might deliberately set low standard hours knowing that the employees will happily, voluntarily work additional hours in order to boost their pay to that point. But that means they don't have annual leave and the other actual time off entitlements that accrue at the rate that they're actually working.

[00:05:13] The second point I'm going to touch on is what we colloquially know as time and a half for payment on public holidays. If I can be frank, the clause relating to that one, two, four, it's quite long. It has cross references in itself and references between some clauses that are quite difficult to follow. It's one of those clauses that you read and you wonder how you might actually explain that to anybody in terms of how it's meant to work. So it is complicated. The second point about it is that there are two mechanisms for the time and a half. So there's one for time and that's what the employer is required to pay the employee for each hour worked. And then the half has a different calculation and that's greater of 50% of the employee's ordinary hourly rate and any identifiable amount.

[00:06:06] So you're doing one calculation for the time and then another one for the half and by no means a payroll expert, but I think that's going to complicate things as people try to put this in practice. So those calculation parts might not be the same, which risks the calculations being very complex to do. So I'll hand over to Michael now to talk about our final point, which is the remediation issue. Thank you, Karen, and good morning everyone. So I'm just going to speak to you briefly about the proposed remediation process for outstanding liabilities under the Holidays Act and those provisions are at Clause 156 in Schedule 3 of the bill. It's proposed that that process will have retrospective effect and could be used to remediate underpayments and payment failures,

[00:06:57] which for whatever reason have occurred up to four years prior to the bill receiving the Royal Ascent. We're concerned that the material relating to the bill does not discuss whether the retrospective provisions are appropriate. There does not seem to be any consideration of whether retrospectivity can adversely impact either employers or employees, for example by reducing or extinguishing some employee's statutory entitlements. Second, whether the extent of the retrospectivity is appropriate or if it requires adjustment. And third, whether this process could upset or lead to relitigation of settled matters. We also have concerns as to whether it is appropriate to prescribe the substantive content of those retrospective provisions through regulations,

[00:07:48] rather than through primary legislation, because regulations would not be subject to the same level of public and parliamentary scrutiny. Those concerns are heightened as the bill does not require the Minister to undertake public consultation on the draft regulations in circumstances where statutory entitlements are impacted. Finally, we have concerns about regulations that would specify a minimum amount payable, a term that is in that undefined category that I just spoke about, would be defined through the regulations, because the regulations would not be subject to the same level of parliamentary and public scrutiny. This would mean an employer would not be required to make a remediation payment to an employee whose entitlement is below this threshold.

[00:08:37] It raises fairness concerns as the threshold could bar an employee from receiving a payment. They are otherwise entitled to receive, no matter how small the amount. Those concerns are heightened because we do not know at what level that threshold will be set or how it will be calculated. A similar concern arises around the concept of reasonable compensation, given that also appears to contemplate something less than their existing statutory entitlements. We advise you to seek advice on those matters and to consider whether amendments are necessary to address those concerns. I'll hand over four questions if there's any time. Thank you very much. We do have a short amount of time, but we do have one question.

[00:09:22] That doesn't appear we have any. We have a question from Jan Taniti. Thank you Madam Chair, and thank you very much for your submission. It kind of backs up in many respects what some of the industry bodies have been saying as you said. One thing I want to get there, you talked about if there is not-so-savory employers as such and what they might do in this situation. Put it in simple terms, and I'm trying to get my head around this too. Does that mean that they could be incentivising part-time and casualisation workers in this particular case? Because it could mean that they're paying out less in the annual leave? To answer that vaguely, yes, possibly.

[00:10:10] But also you could have full-time employees who are put on say 35 or 30 hours a week. I mean it may be that you're thinking of that as part-time, which is probably right. But work greater hours than that. So they might say work-have the standard hours of 30 or 35 in their agreement, but work much more than that on a regular basis. I think there is a possibility of some, and I'm hesitating to use this word, but some abuse of the system, particularly by unscrupulous employers who are trying to limit how much time off people actually have, particularly if they have to bring in replacement employees at a higher cost.

[00:10:55] Great, thank you. Thank you for that. And a question from Lauren Tsunan. Thank you, Madam Chair. Just a very quick question regarding the standard hours as well. Do you have any data or would this be an understanding of the current average hours that employees actually worked a week? And potentially is it greater than the standard hours that we're seeing now? The reason I ask this question is, you know, we hear anecdotally, but I want to check if there's any data to say that people are working longer and longer hours that are unseen or unaccounted for in their contract. That's not data that the law society would hold, so the answer to your question is no, we don't have that data.

[00:11:41] It might be held by the Victoria University Employment Relations Centre. They do an annual analysis of collective agreements and what provisions are in those. So the data may be somewhere, but it's not held by us, I'm afraid. Thank you. We might look into that. Thank you so much. All right. Thank you very much. We appreciate your participation in the process. You're very constructive and helpful as a mission. So thank you again. Thank you. On now to have a similar theme, Simpson-Greason. We have Rachel Judge and Mike Mercer joining us. Rachel and Mike, we have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for questions.

[00:12:27] Thank you. I am Rachel Judge, a partner at Simpson-Greason, and I'm presenting with my colleague, Mike Mercer, Senior Associate. We have both been heavily involved in advising on the current Holidays Act and assisting clients through their remediation processes. And we thank you for the opportunity to present our oral submission today on behalf of Simpson-Greason. The submission has been informed by feedback from a number of our clients and also in line with what Karen's just spoken to, our own observations as lawyers as to where complexities may arise based on the wording of the current bill. At the outset, I do want to note that Simpson-Greason supports the core objectives of the bill.

[00:13:15] We have been actively advocating for many of these changes for a number of years, in particular the move to an hours-based accrual model and the simplification of leave payment calculations. That said, there are some aspects of the bill that are likely to pose some compliance issues for employers and particularly those with complex workforces. So we do consider that there is room for the bill to potentially include some options to address some of those issues. And we wish to comment on three key issues from our written submissions where we have made recommendations. Those are the notional roster, the mandatory standard hours versus additional hours leave compensation payment approach,

[00:14:05] which we'll refer to as LCP, and the issue of strike action during the transitional period. So just turning to the notional roster framework to start with, this applies to determined leave entitlements where an employee's standard hours or days of work cannot be readily identified. And while the policy intent is understood, we submit that the framework as drafted may not be workable in practice for many employers. Clause 9, for example, requires that a notional roster is agreed in writing and kept up to date in workplaces where hours are variable or changed frequently, that will be difficult if not impossible to apply.

[01:37:52] committee just before we move on we have a member Phil Triford that wants to acknowledge some guests before they leave Thanks Chair colleagues this is a delegation with us who are from Cambodia and Laos program it's like a fellowship program for six months funded through the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade We're waiting for you to be clear We're waiting for you to be clear Thank you very much for observing our submitting submitting submitting submitting I all want to be in peace Right we will move on now to the young workers

[01:38:38] resource centre we have online Matariki Roche Welcome Matariki Thank you so much for being with us today we've got ten minutes with you so we'll hand over it to you now Hi Matariki I'm Matariki I'm the comms and engagement coordinator at Young Workers Resource Centre we also hosted the wonderful delegation of young leaders from Cambodia and Laos so big shout out to them as well

[01:39:24] for coming along today First I just wanted to thank you all for your time and I'm going to take our submission as read I've got a couple of other points that I wanted to make just to build off our read submission Firstly I just kind of wanted to paint a picture for you actually about the reality of being a young worker at the moment Young workers are more likely to be in part-time casual or regular work we're more likely to be working additional hours often we feel that we cannot refuse those additional hours for fear of missing out on future shifts or for the need to catch up to our more experienced co-workers we have a vulnerability to getting sick

[01:40:10] there are often environmental factors like shitty crowded housing that means that we're more vulnerable to getting sick we have less negotiating power when it comes to negotiating our individual employment agreements we're more likely to be in low paid work we face the financial insecurity and the instability of things like youth rates, training wage and 90 day trials and along with that we're in a time of our lives where we the impact and the financial burden of unexpected service circumstances hit us harder and it's harder to absorb that so with that in mind

[01:40:56] we've deeply, deeply opposed the changes to the Holidays Act through this proposed employment leave bill we think that the proposed bill will exacerbate those inequalities that young workers already face that I just mentioned and we know that young workers will be missing out on leave particularly sick and you'll leave through those additional hours that they work we also know that they're going to have a harder time negotiating the bear minimum more than the bear minimum because of these changes moving from a days or weeks system to an hourly accrual makes it really hard as a young worker to negotiate that you know

[01:41:42] it's much easier to say hey can I have one more day of sick leave added to my contract rather than being like hey can we change the negotiation from 0.385 to whatever it might be so we also know that not only are the minimums going to be reduced but also the possibility of getting more will also be reduced we mentioned in our written submission that young workers are often misclassified as casual workers when the reality is that they are part-timers and we're also really concerned about the pathway forward for those misclassified workers because of this bill we often tell workers

[01:42:28] the young workers that come to us about the benefits of arguing for a permanent contract even if they're on low hours and they have to balance the benefits of that with the immense amount of fear and the very real possibility that they might lose their job or not be offered future shifts they raise that issue and now with the leave compensation payment what we will say is that those extra hours that we often say to employers hey just put them on a five hour a week contract even if they're more likely to be doing 10 or 15 if you put them on a permanent contract you can make those hours lower and in the current system their leave annual and sick leave will still accrue on that however in the proposed

[01:43:14] system that will no longer happen and so those benefits of going for that more permanent and secure working agreement is also lower because of this we also just frankly believe that that no entitlements no leave entitlements should be reduced because of any proposed bill we should be improving things as we go along making our working lives better for the generations coming up beneath us but this bill does not do that and we will say that in fact leave and general rights of workers particularly young workers will be eroded because of this young workers also have not been in the position to be able to contribute or have a say

[01:44:00] in or shape the employment system that we currently exist in we face all of these inequalities that makes it harder for us to have thriving lives that include work but we have not been able to have the contribution to shape the system that we're in whether that's because of you know the fact that the system changed so drastically before we were born or even just thinking about the workers that have come into the workforce since the last election but in saying that that means that you the committee actually had the real opportunity to think of young workers and keep us in mind when shaping what our employment system looks like just because we haven't had a say doesn't mean that we don't have the option to create

[01:44:46] something new and lastly with that I would just like to request that the committee supports the pledge proposed by the PSA that no entitlements or leave balances are reduced as a result of this bill we also support the recommendations and the submission made by the CTU as well as PSA youth. Thank you Thank you very much we have our first question from Carl Bates Thanks very much chair thank you for your submission I'm always a bit skeptical when submitters submit on a bill and are essentially of the view that it should entirely be got rid of so I just want to understand from you if you understand that your young workers

[01:45:32] also include pregnant young woman yeah and if you read our submission you would see the points that we supported of this bill so I'm just making sure in your verbal submission you're reflecting that you're not entirely against this bill and this bill does improve the outcomes for some workers not blatant you know get rid of the whole thing again if you read our submission you would have seen the points that we did support such as pace lips being mandatory leaving available from day one however we as we said in our submission we hold the position that you know improving some parts of our employment system

[01:46:18] should not come at the cost of reducing leave or reducing those rights for other workers it doesn't have to be this gives some take some we should be increasing to benefit for all No I appreciate that I'm just making sure it comes from the corridor live as well and the third part of that is doesn't this actually mean for young casual workers that they will receive their allowance at the 12.5% essentially meaning that they would be better off than if they just got sickly when they actually needed it during their casual work Yes and no the majority of the young workers on casual agreements that we come

[01:47:04] through one have been in the casual role with regular hours for more than six months so they do have entitlement to sick leave and while we differently support the recognition of the peculiarity of casual work that the LCP reflects we also know that it does put those who are misclassified particularly misclassified by actually all casual workers in a precarious issue where if you don't have that money that you received from the LCP in your last pay cycle doesn't actually mean a whole heap when you are sick in the current week and you need to take leave but you then don't have sick leave to take and can't pay your bills that

[01:47:50] week so while there is some benefit for that there is also there is still a cost and we are still going to see workers either taking unpaid sick leave or more likely I assume coming to work second and spreading the illness around So just to be clear rather than trusting that people can manage their own money and end up with more in the pocket because they receive this entitlement regardless of whether they take sick leave at all during the period your view is let's not give that to everyone and let's have a system that says only those that use their sick leave are the ones that get it

[01:48:36] I'm not sure if that's exactly what I'm saying I do think there should be some recognition for the precarious nature of casual work but I also think all workers should have the protection of sick leave it's a protection that benefits us all and it's a safety net that catches us which is particularly important when young workers are in precarious and vulnerable situations that already have a weakened safety net. Thank you and a question from Tiana Tiana. My questions around you gave an example where workers will end up having to calculate actually what their leave entitlement will
