Kia ora and Tiana. Yeah 100% essentially it's going to make things clearer for workers. I've also had conversations supported by solidarity members here at YWRC and a few of those are employers a couple of them have reached out to me because they've said oh you know we've heard about how this is going to impact young workers and we want to give our workers our employees more than the minimum can we just give them you know extra 5, 10 days

[01:50:08] yada yada yada will maintain the 10 days and I was like yeah except you're still going to have to do that conversion and that was really unclear to them and these employers as well as these young workers yeah there was a lot of confusion harder to find a pathway forward it's less clear in short. My follow-up question is the comment she made around about around how people are up there told the casual workers but actually they're part-time workers I wonder if you could give some concrete specific examples that would be very clear for the committee Kia ora, yeah for sure so we had an ice cream worker at one of our workshops a couple of weeks ago and this young worker

[01:50:54] had been working at this ice cream shop for I think it was nearly two and a half years they worked consistently every Saturday you know frequent and those hours would increase during the summer times as you can imagine they've been working regularly at least every Saturday for over two years they were on a casual agreement when we described the working patterns like in our workshops we go through and we talk about employment types and when we explain what a part-time permanent employee looks like in comparison to a casual worker this young worker had this like penny drop moment of whole shoot I'm supposed to be a part-time worker they were really really scared when they learnt that they actually the reality of that means that their boss

[01:51:40] can just stop offering them shifts and that stability that they've gained over the past two years that reliance on this income would just instantly drop sorry I must stop you there because we've got to move on to our next submitter we've gone over time with you but really appreciate your contribution to the process and you're making yourself available for us today so thank you Kia ora we move on now to business south who we have online we have Shona Brown, Katarina Slade and Wilford Paul good afternoon

[01:52:30] members of the committee and chair I'm Shona Brown, advocacy advisor at business south and we are part of the Otago Chamber of Commerce and the shareholder of business New Zealand representing around 1400 businesses across Otago and Southland the Holidays Act has been a genuine pain point for our members for years with payroll examples like payroll errors costly remediation and real uncertainty about whether they're getting it right so we would like this reform to succeed and support the direction of the bill I'm sitting here not as a technical expert our role is to bring the lived

[01:53:16] experience of businesses on the ground and our submission is built directly from member consultation we were fortunate to have Minister Van Velden join a members only webinar only recently to walk through the bill and take questions which is directly shaped what we've raised in our submission so rather than speak to the content in our submission I've brought along a member I've got Katarina Slade and Wilford Paul from PurePack they're based in Cromwell and Central Otago they're exactly the kind of business this bill needs to work for they have a seasonal workforce with variable hours and weather dependent operations they can speak to both what's working in the bill and where the practical problems

[01:54:02] sit and possible solutions I'd encourage the committee to direct any detailed questions to both of them as they're far better placed than I am to bring those issues to life so thank you to the committee for your time and I'll hand over to Katarina Thank you Sharma I'd like to introduce ourselves a little bit further we're a cherry growing and pack house business in Cromwell our harvest season runs specifically from around well not quite specifically from the middle of December and that can shift around within a two week period due to the high uncertainty of it the harvest period runs for about six weeks sometimes seven we at peak employ 250 to 300

[01:54:48] people to work both on the picking and harvest side and in our packing operation many of our employees are backpackers on working holiday visas we've also got a reasonably good cohort of returning semi-retired employees and a few local school kids we support the bills direction overall and there are a lot of great components to it that we definitely agree with our verbal submission will pull out the parts that are concerning to us or that we think could be tweaked to work more appropriately for seasonal employment the first

[01:55:36] kind of area of concern I guess is around the LCP and sick leave obviously if you're a reverse engineer it's based on a 10 day entitlement or the equivalent thereof firstly that increases the cost to employers to the full 10 day entitlement which is more than what most permanent employees would cost because many of them don't actually take their full entitlement it's there if they need it and so it should be but it makes it slightly weighted towards the casual secondly and probably most importantly is a food handling organisation where we are processing cherries and there are 70 people coming into contact with the food

[01:56:22] granted there is a lot of PPE involved we strongly prefer that sick leave is actually used for genuine illness to prevent people coming to work when they're sick and coming into contact with cherries that are then going out into the world for consumption so paying out and cash that sick leave removes the incentive for an employee to call in sick on the day when they're generally sick they may feel able to do work but we still don't want them contaminating the product so that's why we're against cashing out any kind of sick leave and would prefer it's used bereavement leave is another one that we're a little bit worried about the potential for misuse so

[01:57:08] oh actually sorry I've skipped ahead yes we'll talk about the uncertainty factor in the LCP so there's a bit of rounding or uncertainty factor woven into that which would just like to raise that as an employer we equally experience that uncertainty factor we can offer someone when they're on a truly casual contract we offer them a shift and they don't have to accept it so that uncertainty is experienced both by the employee because they don't know if they're going to get off at a shift and by the employer who doesn't know if the employee is going to accept the shift and we have to build that variability into our business as well so we don't support there being extra compensation for that bereavement leave particularly in a short employment period

[01:57:56] there is not that built up trust as there is with a longer term employment arrangement and it is open to misuse if an employee two days into their contract they just need to be some kind of ability to make a reasonable request to check the evidence so that the integrity of the leave's purpose is protected I guess the largest concern for us is the assessment of an otherwise working day and we really appreciate the attempt to increase the clarity around that that is definitely needed the current

[01:58:42] legislation and interpretation is a real significant source of friction in our workplace and I think in a lot of seasonal businesses so definitely can acknowledge and agree that clarity is needed where the concern comes from is when we employ someone in the middle of December right when our harvest period starts for six week seven-week contract in that period there is a disproportionate number of public holidays or that fall and under the proposed assessment of looking at up to 13 weeks history but less if less exists you could end up in a situation where someone has only worked two days in that period

[01:59:28] and they decline every other shift that we offer them and they are entitled to then four days of paying public holiday leave so yes it would be very clear on what the entitlement is but in practicality it's waited too far equally on the other foot the opposite could happen someone could work every other day except that Friday and Saturday beforehand and not be entitled to a single public holiday but they actually worked more than someone who might be so it's a tricky I can see it's really tricky a potential suggestion which we have come up on how to

[02:00:14] tackle this issue is in the situation where a worker has a really short work history but or less than 13 weeks is to assume that over a 12-month period if they are doing casual work but they perhaps qualified for half of all the public holidays and include that in the LCP and have no other holiday entitlements that way it's clear what the entitlements are you don't get it you get an extra piece of payment for the fact that to compensate you for not being able to take the public holidays and it waits it across the workforce at every time of the year

[02:01:00] it's not disproportionately hitting businesses that like ours which are forced to harvest our crop during that summer period I believe that's Wilfred did I miss anything yeah we just want the burden of cost to be fair more equally spread and to remove in that short term where a really short work period exists to make it more equitable in a way that keeps the clarity that the bill aims for that's all I've got for you right thank you so much I just want to say really appreciate the constructive

[02:01:46] and helpful submission that you provided us just the detail and you know live examples are really helpful for us to work through so thank you for that and we'll move on now to our final submitter for today which is Unite Union Manitopu and that is Tali Williams with us online we'll move on thank you Welcome we have 10 minutes with you that includes time for question and we will leave it to you now Kia ora koutou I'm the Assistant National Secretary of Unite Union

[02:02:32] Represents Unite Union represents 5,500 members in fast food, hospitality, entertainment, hotels, cinemas and the call centre industries. We are opposing the Employment Leave Bill that would slash the pay and leave entitlements of tens of thousands of working people, mainly lower paid working people who are already struggling through the cost of living and the fuel crisis. Under this bill, the work completed outside of contracted hours would no longer accrue sick leave or annual leave, a change that would disproportionately affect park time and casual workers. These workers are our members in low paid industries. And further, these workers are already in percurious employment situations. These changes will only

[02:03:21] make life worse for them. We don't accept that the bill is about simplifying complex law. The findings and recommendations of the Holidays Act Task Force, which was in place to address complexities and reduce complexities has been largely ignored. The exploring of the hours based accrual system was discontinued by the task force part way through when they realised it financially disadvantaged a substantial number of workers compared to the current act. That seems to be no concern to the current government. As an example of affected industries, a substantive number of workers in fast food have a set amount of guaranteed hours. And then hours beyond that are considered additional

[02:04:09] hours to find a proposed bill. So over a sample of a 13 week period in 2025, to give an example, a third of hours worked over that period were not guaranteed under one May fast food brand that we cover. A worker within this fast food brand worked an average of 36 hours a week. However, only five of those hours were guaranteed with the others being classed as additional hours. That means under the new bill, this worker would only accrue leave based on her five guaranteed hours, which would be 0.38 of an hour per week accrued. This is unfair and untenable.

[02:04:55] To get one week's leave, this worker would have to work for two years. This for a worker who averages 36 hours and contributes to our economy pays her taxes and so on. The proposed 12% in lieu of a leave only confirms lack of understanding of the hand-to-mouth nature of those working in precarious and low paid employment. One in such a position cannot afford to put money aside to allow for leave to be taken at a later date. People will invariably need and use the money now, particularly in a cost of living crisis. If you don't have savings because you're trying to cover weekly costs, you are punished again with not being able to afford to take the leave.

[02:05:46] Then, of course, there's the reduction to sick leave. The same group of fast food workers that I referred to earlier under this bill would accrue sick leave for only two-thirds of the work that they do. Of course, the major issue with less sick leave for these workers is that they will come to work sick because they can't afford to take unpaid leave. This means passing on illness resulting in more sickness not only on site with their fellow workers, but the broader community because these people work with food and prepare and serve food. As we all know and have experienced, people are getting sick longer and more seriously these days with viruses such as COVID, RSV and

[02:06:34] so on. We know that under this bill, employers seeing what seems on the face of it to be a financial advantage will seek to offer the less amount of guaranteed hours and then simply top up hours as they wish. This means the less they will have to afford fair annual and sick leave. This risk of increased casualization will have a compounding negative effect on the community. Of course, the more precarious the work, the worse off the workers' lives, but also the less resources people have to put back into the economy. Precarious work also results in more exploitation and risk exploitation as people clamber for the dregs of hours their employer makes available and those workers feel compromised to raise

[02:07:23] various workplace concerns and even fear of raising serious health and safety risks. These are people already struggling on low wages and low hours during the cost of living crisis. It is shameful that the government is not only refusing to help those most of them need, but actively seeking to legislate to make life tougher for them. Working people deserve holidays and time with their families and they deserve to take the time off they need when they're sick and when their children are sick. Unite Union strongly opposes the attack on leave entitlements of the most vulnerable workers. Thank you. Thank you very much. Tali, we have our first question from Jan Tineety. Kia ora, Tali, and thank you very much for your submission this morning or this afternoon.

[02:08:12] And can I also say thank you for your example that you gave your case study that you titled the SS. I felt that was really, really helpful. When I was going through and we were determining Labour's position on this bill, our key rationale was that nobody would be worse off at the end than at the beginning. And obviously that's not the case. And when I first started, I was told it's only a few workers. But I'm getting the sense that we're talking about a lot of workers here. You say you represent five and a half thousand workers. How many of them would be in this situation or roughly how many of them would be in this situation if having guaranteed hours but then having to work way more hours on the on the roster? I would say about half

[02:09:03] conservatively. Yeah, so it's quite a big amount that we're talking about. And the question that I asked the Law Society before, is there a fear that the way that this is written at the moment incentivises that to happen more? Yes, absolutely. That is our fear. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. And question now from Tiana Tuoyono. Kia ora, Tali. Thank you very much for your very clear, clear submission and with a lot of really useful detail in there. I wanted to come back to your point around the hours of cruel system. And you were referencing, I think, the task force in 2018. I wasn't anywhere near Parliament then. I wonder if you could maybe tell us a bit more about the problems with that approach, why it was abandoned and what that impact of that will be on your members.

[02:09:54] Can I just, before you do that, Tali, just let you know you've got one minute left in your submission. So if we could make that brief. Okay. Well, in summary, I don't have a whole lot to say on that other than my understanding is that the findings of the task force without that hours based on cruel system wouldn't work. That it wasn't appropriate for all forms of work, particularly those part timers and casuals. And therefore that was abandoned. I don't have any more to say on that. Thank you. I really appreciate your participation in this process and you making the time to come and join us for our hearings today, Tali. Thank you again. And we'll now move into private session with our officials. Thank you.

meetings.title: 2026/04/02 EDSI [Part 2]
meetings.committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
meetings.description: Thursday 2 April 2026 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Retirement Commissioner Office
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Not just how they felt about the training, but also the teacher, how the staff go away from changes. So we're looking really hard at how to get a better impact for this, which would be good for funders, providers and learners. That tool's rolling out now. Fourth, the review of the retirement villagers' actors proceed well. Legislative drafting has commenced with the bill intended to be introduced mid-year. This is the culmination of work we've led since 2020, working with Housing and Urban Development to rebalance consumer rights with operator rights. And fifth, KiwiSaver, recommendation to action. One of the highlights of the year was the government adoption of our recommendation to lift KiwiSaver default contributions from 3% to 4%, and to extend employer and government

[00:00:47] contributions to 16 and 17-year-olds. We calculated that KiwiSaver will now last 30% longer for median earners, which is a transformational outcome for many future retirees. Within two days of the announcement, there's a 2,500% increase in active users of the sorted KiwiSaver calculator, which tells you something about the appetite-trusted, independent information. So the breadth of impact of our work across government and the sector were the very small team. But the message I must want to leave with you finally is that a holistic long-term view of the retirement income system is essential.

[00:01:33] Tinkering with settings simply weakens the structure of the system. The latest is allowing access to KiwiSaver for farm ownership. A farm is a business. This is poor policy and weakens the fundamental purpose of KiwiSaver being a retirement scheme, not a general savings or business scheme. KiwiSaver is, of course, voluntary. Another tinker earlier allowed total remuneration, where the employee is actually paying both their contribution and the employers. This was not in the original KiwiSaver scheme, was permitted by a law changed by a national government in 2008. No subsequent government has repealed it. It means people have less take-home pay,

[00:02:19] and employers are not doing their bit. Short-term thinking with the retirement income system is a real threat to the well-being of future older New Zealanders. That's you. We delivered our substantial 2025 review of retirement income policies in December. Its main message is that system change will happen. And a cross-party approach is the best way to develop a principled 10-year plan to consider strategic change for both KiwiSaver and Super together. Both are essential mechanisms that work together. So I leave you with this thought. This committee, itself a cross-party mechanism,

[00:03:06] has an opportunity to support that key recommendation, to promote a measured cross-party approach to considering long-term system change. This committee could review the mechanism suggested by the Commission and our report and doubtless improve it. We know collaboration is possible. The system will fail New Zealanders if we can't find new ways to think long-term, as New Zealand grapples with increasingly complex issues. Ngorera, te ina koutou, te ina koutou, te ina koutou, kato. Thank you so much. First question from Dr Lawrence, you know. Thank you, Chair. And I just want to first say thank you so much for all of your work, Jane. And I particularly appreciate your briefing on aging population and the flassie and social services and communities as well.

[00:03:52] My first question, so it's focused on one part of that, which is retirement village. Now we know we have seen the cabinet paper when it comes to retirement village and you mentioned your report that you were involved in that. In terms of the actual cabinet paper that we've seen and what you've heard from retirement village residents, do you think that the recommendation and the perspective has been reflected in that cabinet paper? The proposed legislation moves a long way ahead from where we were in 2020. Some of the changes have already been implemented by the operators, which is amazing. Some of the changes are yet to be implemented are still good and there are still a few areas that will continue to be the subject of debate,

[00:04:38] which will be a very good and interesting select committee time. As part of the review, supplementary chair, what do you think would be the impact for retirees if retirement village doesn't look into capital sharing? That's probably the remaining number of the issues, right? And the real question then is when repayment might happen and how much repayment happens from the original capital sale, right? So those are really crunchy business debates and resident issues. And I think probably further the modelling needs to be done on this, but no matter what happens, the bill itself is a major advance

[00:05:24] on where we are at the moment, which is dealing with 20-year-old legislation. That's well out of date. Just to go to the chair, I have a new question on income. All right, so we'll come back to you later then. We have a supplementary problem in good leadership. Yeah, thank you for the work that you've done on this with HUD and noting that it appears that the capital repayments will be mandated to a year, which in my view and in my members' bill is not enough, minus three months. Lawrence Sunao and I did ask for an exposure draft of the bill and haven't received it. But I'm wondering, do you accept that exclusion of existing contracts will actually disadvantage those who are already in retirement villages, because they'll be the last cab off the rank

[00:06:11] to have the incentivisation for their licences to occupy to be sold by operators, particularly in an environment where we've got fast track building consent happening and expansion of villages. And if so, what actions would you like to see the government take around, rather than excluding retrospectivity, what further action could they take to fathom out a way that is fair to all retirement village residents so that we avoid a situation where those on current contracts will actually be disadvantaged by the new law? Yeah, again, I think this is the last crunchy hurdle to be jumped. You've got a longstanding legal principle that says contract, they just sort of changes aren't retrospective in most cases.

[00:06:58] You've got a good argument from the current residents going, yes, I know, but. And I think what will need to happen is some really good legal research on precedence. If there is a precedent for retrospectivity, what does it look like? Is it as strong as this one? I don't know. I don't know whether that work's been done by hand yet, but I can see that it's going to have to be to help resolve this issue. Just one more suffer, fine, yeah. And acknowledging that we had the Residential Tenancies Act, which could be considered an analogue, we've had changes made during COVID to commercial tenancies, which were retrospective in application and we've recently had statements in the House by Honourable Brock Van Velden that question the nature of particular provisions of contracts, saying that if they are just provisions,

[00:07:43] they may not be retrospective. So in all of that context, absolutely endorse what you've just said about further legal work. That's right. Again, every example that could be found for an equivalent approach would be helpful to that argument. Thank you. That's a commentary from Dr David Witsman. Yeah, a supplementary on this. Never talked about capital share, and we're talking about timing really, aren't we? Has there been any international comparative analysis of where our sector sits as proposed to others? More opposed? The international comparators are not that simple, actually. Our side is quite different from others, which is no great surprise to anything in New Zealand, really. I've forgotten what the Australian situation is. Do you remember? Six minutes. Yeah, I think it is, but it varies at state and federal.

[00:08:31] But again, it's a very different coverage of license to occupy this as quite different. Yeah. So my instinctive response is always to find the answers to bring that home. But we caught up, which is why we had to go. To identify what the problem was here. All right. New question from Dr Vanessa. Thank you, manager. And thank you very much for all the contribution that you've made over the time as commissioner. It's been wonderful to watch and see from this committee how it's progressing. Previously, you've had reports around retirement age and basically recommended that the retirement age not be adjusted or lifted. One of the things that we have to struggle with as a country

[00:09:18] is how we pay for our superannuation scheme going forward. If that's not the solution, what in your view could be a solution for the sustainability and ability of our superannuation scheme going forward? That's a really good question. And it is one of the core questions around the system itself, which is why we say tinkering or one-step solutions are not the way to deal with this, right? If we can get commitment to developing a long-term plan, then at that stage, all those competing tensions can be evaluated cross-portfolio, cross-party,

[00:10:03] and then where will we want to land as a country? It really needs to focus at a higher level on vision. What kind of country do we want for older New Zealanders and then work down from that? Affordability is absolutely a question. The answer is, are there other elements of government spending that could stop to help make it affordable, or is a good question? There are other mechanisms and system settings that could be looked at as well. For instance, means testing between 65 and 70, something like that. But none of these things should be considered an isolation without having a bit of an overall game plan. If we want to change things, then can we do it in a meaningful and holistic way that gets the best set of results for what is a series of quite complicated problems?

[00:10:51] Just a supplementary on that. What work have you done so far in that area, or is there any recommendation for further research or public engagement that you would make? The recommendation is to develop a plan. It's a very large document, and it's excellent bedtime reading. Yeah. All right, I support you, Trom. Yeah, thank you, Commissioner. I'm really concerned about a particular cohort of New Zealanders who don't fall into the traditional thing we've relied on around home ownership and KiwiSaver funds. And when I look at our current superannuation, it feels to me like an efficient universal basic income at an end of life where targeting would be inefficient.

[00:11:39] What is your concern for that cohort who are now couch surfing or even homeless if we were to continue with incremental change or just changing settings rather than comprehensive reform? It's, again, another good question. This is almost a forgotten cohort, which is why we keep saying we cannot do individual system change in isolation. So if you are of the view, for instance, that if you just put NZ Super age up, end of problem, job done, that's the narrowest of narrow views and does not take into account the impacts on a series of different types of people. Those impacts need to be analysed carefully. So those people that you're talking about now,

[00:12:24] we are predicting that this is going to rise considerably. We're predicting a 100% increase in renters by 2048 and NZ Super is designed for not incurring considerable housing costs. It's designed for being mortgage-free or being a well-subsidised council or state housing. Those models themselves are under enormous pressure, as you know. So my plea is that every time we get questions like this, we go, it's part of the great bucket of problems in this space and we can't ignore the big ones. That's one. All right, supplementary from Lawrence, you know. Thank you. And this also overtakes my primary question. Later. Yeah, we look more...

[00:13:09] No, no, no, I've seen... I don't have a primary anymore. This is the supplementary. So on that, two supplementary. First one is what modelling has been done on those who are over 65 were purely looking at pension plus maybe accommodation supplement who are still renting at a market rate, not in public housing. Do we have any modelling on what proportion of their super or pension is being used towards rent? That's one for my colleague in the policy and research team. It's not something we have directly modelled, but we do know that about 40% of people on New Zealand Super just rely on New Zealand Super and pretty much that and nothing else. And we see that that group of people, particularly who are renting, is being overrepresented at the poverty statistics

[00:13:56] of the over 65s. And I think what's striking is we actually have seen quite a remarkable increase in poverty in the over 65 groups. It used to be almost practically zero, but now it's actually slightly higher than the working age population. So we are seeing emerging concerns there. We haven't specifically measured the degree of which people are having to spend on New Zealand Super on rent, though. And second question is one of the things we heard recently from, for example, Housing First Ototahi is the fact that over 65-year-olds has had the most drastic increase, a 300% increase on the social housing register, and they're waiting for that. While you're saying there's a long-term, non-partisan solution we should all be looking at, what would be a recommendation for immediate solution? There's no immediate solutions for housing.

[00:14:44] That is what is also one of the things I've learned in this job. But one of the things I wanted to do during my tenure is to ensure that in the housing debate, which was all about the problems of first home buyers when I started, and it sure was a problem, that the housing debate needs to think more broadly and absolutely consider what older New Zealanders need because the stereotype of us all sitting in nice comfortable little class houses fully paid off is indeed that a stereotype. Okay, Hamish. Yes, thank you. Just following up on that, with your modelling, over the last couple of quarters, we've seen the highest level of first home buyers as a percentage of real estate sales, within 29%. Has that been taken in consideration

[00:15:31] when your modelling future demand for housing in 65-plus because it's something that has kind of flown under the radar? The first home buyers, you know, even flowed depending on market conditions, right? And so that when the interest rates dropped and the housing prices dropped, it became more attractive. But yes, they're considered with an overall picture, are they not? Yeah, quite different segments of the housing market. And one thing where we have done research was to interview quite a number of landlords just to try and understand what the private rental market looked like for over 65s and there was basically very low awareness of, you know, sort of the ageing of the population and the fact that the ability to provide

[00:16:17] good rental properties is going to require some sort of changes to sort of layouts of bathrooms and those sorts of questions. So we're not really, the private market's not really delivering a rental stock that's suitable for the over 65 population. So that's probably where we have the larger concern rather than worrying about sort of implications with the sort of first home buyers. First home buyers. All right, I'll go to Ruben Davidson. Do you have any family? I do. You've been asking for a while. Yeah. And I just wanted to acknowledge your role as commissioner and caution anyone who has the words retirement and Jane Ritzen in the same sentence. But my question is around KiwiSaver, I'm seeing anecdotally through my office, but also electric office, but also through conversations

[00:17:05] with JPs in the area, record numbers of people accessing KiwiSaver funds early. You spoke about the shifts in default and the potential for KiwiSaver to last 30% longer. My question is really around what data that's relying on and at what point in time based on the huge search we're seeing in people accessing KiwiSaver funds earlier. Yeah, the overall percentage of people that make hardship withdrawals is about 1.5% of the entire KiwiSaver population. It's absolutely true. It's rising at the moment because of the cost-e�iving crisis. No question. You've got two competing tensions here, right? If people really need the money and they're on the bread line,

[00:17:52] then it's best they take some. Just best at it they do. The question is whether they're getting sufficient advice at the grassroots, sufficient budget and mentoring assistance, whether their provider is establishing that their grounds are real and whether they can then get back on the KiwiSaver horse the moment their financial situation improves. So I don't have a problem with the ability to withdraw per se. What we don't know is the real reasons for withdrawal and that's a piece of work that my office will be doing over the next little while because there is lots and lots of anecdotal evidence. If it's all purely hardship, good. There's a little stream of thought that says people still think it's a savings account and they should be able to get their money whenever the time they feel like it.

[00:18:37] And that's the concern of that is true. And then there's lots of stories about weight loss surgery and all the rest of it. We just don't know any of this. And so the best thing my office can do, I think, is get the data because policy and evidence in this space. One thing. Do you sense of how long when that picture can be more accurate? Well, I know that Public Trust, for example, are doing a lot of good work in this space and so we're talking with them. So how long will it take? It is hard to provide a specific timeline, but it is actively being worked on right as we speak. Yeah, I had a direct conversation with Public Trust a couple of months ago because they're introducing, as it turns out,

[00:19:24] a whole new platform, which is going to be able to give them much more data than has ever been properly seen before. And they're one of the largest supervisors in the country. So they will be a really good litmus test, I think, of what they can see in terms of their provided customer stats. And from there, we can extrapolate it out. I'd like to think there'd be some data available next year. That is preliminary. Yeah. So, I supplementary. Thank you. Just going to the issue of how the market is going to accommodate a higher levels of disability. Wondering what analysis you've done on the best policy instruments. My intuition would suggest incentivising.

[00:20:12] But have you done a return on investment around what incentivising say-through tax breaks or other things would do to that market? And the second one is, I'll start with that one first. Thank you. The short answer is no. No. We're a very small commission, so we have to be really tightly focused, as I said at the beginning, so there are so many issues in this space to be thinking about. So we set out our priorities every year and our statement of performance expectations and really happy to see feedback over time about whether there's a particular area that you think is serious and within our scope and under-researched.

[00:20:57] We think we've got most of them. Could potentially be something that HUD could be looking at. Yeah. Secondly, just around that, gosh, do you have a view on with new social or public or community housing, given all of these issues, whether we should be adopting a particular higher standard of disabled access rather than the current standards? It would be logical, but we don't have a formal view. This is faikaha, a space, not really ours. Sure, yeah. Lawrence, you're done. Thank you. Quick supplementary, but may not be a quick response. Last year in the briefing, you identified in terms of KiwiSaver and it came as a surprise to the committee that women and self-employed are some of the most affected and not acknowledged when it comes to

[00:21:44] how substantial KiwiSaver is savings and women in particular are most at risk of shock events. You identified a problem last year, but what's the solution? The solution has been very aware of the gender disparities and policy. I mean, the root of this is the gender pay gap, which means that employers and legislators can have a view on this. The solution can lie with the individual and the individual's whānau. If we can consciousness raise enough so that women for a start understand it's an issue, which I didn't particularly, I have to say, when I started, and then can have conversations with their whānau about what might happen if they go on parental leave, what might happen if they're doing other caregiving, what might happen if they're out of the workforce

[00:22:31] for other kinds of reasons, and more specifically, the mean in their lives, the husbands and family, and so if they, and partners, if they can understand that this actually is real, then if they're good at meaning their lives, they will want to try and do something themselves too. Just also add in the review, this is something we did quite a lot of work on and on parental leave, we did make recommendations around, because currently, if you go on parental leave, the government does step in as your employer, but take up of that program is very low, it's about 20%, so we did make some recommendations around strengthening that, and we've done a lot of work with Henry, the company, and we're continuing to work with them on sort of trying to understand the self-employed

[00:23:17] and what can be done, but it's all in the review, so yeah. Marvellous. Looks like there are no more questions now, so thank you so much, Jen, for your service, best wishes, heaps of best wishes from the entire committee to you. Are there any final remarks that you would like to make? No, it's been a pleasure, as always, to come and talk with you about our work. My whole team is very passionate about what we do, because we are working for the greater good of New Zealanders, which is always very satisfying, but I would end by reiterating my comments that you are a classic cross-party mechanism. There are ways, I think, that committees can help drive a conversation that gets out of the silos and into what's better for long-term thinking. Thank you so much. Thank you.

[00:24:04] I appreciate you, thank you.

meetings.title: 2026/04/02 - JU [Part 3]
meetings.description: Thursday, 2 April 2026 | Justice Committee Inquiry into the 2025 Local Elections Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Transparency International New Zealand Inc
meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Behind now, are we? Yeah, we are, so. Hello. Transparency International, they're in by Zoom. Hello, how are you? Yeah, sorry about that, I just... No, let's go. Now, we've got 10 minutes with you. The committee, just for people beaming in for this, please leave some time for questions. Otherwise, I just have to cut you off, and that's not very helpful.

[00:00:46] The committee does like to ask questions. We've all read your submissions, so we don't have to keep going through and laboring them, because we've read them. Okay, the time issuers, thanks. Sure. I mean, I think taking that the submission is read. I just think there's three main issues, and that was one is the integrity of electoral processes generally. The second is oversight and accountability and reporting, especially on things like special votes. We don't think there's anybody who's got an eye on that, and I'm afraid it doesn't seem to be the second committee either. And the third one is education and information for new candidates, because then there's misinformation as well and disinformation, but those are the ones, the main ones. So what we think is, and I think other people have spoken on it,

[00:01:33] is that the kind of rigour that's enabled by the Electoral Commission running the central election is not then enabled in the local government elections, and that's putting stress on everywhere. We've recommended to move away from postal voting, because that's clearly not working, and I appreciate there's a lot of complexity in there. But also, we're really concerned about recent events in the last election. I realise that none have got to the police prosecution yet, but they were certainly significant enough to void an election result. And that was a serious concern, probably the largest. Whatever it was, somebody took those votes out of people's letterboxes, somebody faked the votes.

[00:02:20] And that's a hugely serious issue that needs to be dealt with more than just by the police. So I would have thought an inquiry would have been the best approach to that. Anyway, I think that's all I'm saying. We've got sort of what we've said, and so I'm happy to answer questions. All right. Thank you very much. I've got the first question from Mr Calvates. Thanks very much, Chair. Thanks very much for your submission. Found it really an interesting read. Was interested in your point made under 2B1 about conduct of elections and processes prior to election day for raising issues, and just interested if you had any thoughts about how that could occur? Yes, I would be trying to nut that through as well. How would you actually manage it? I suppose you could have the initiation of an inquiry

[00:03:07] at the point at which it's raised. It's possible that I don't think there was any ability for people to, for the Electoral Officer to have initial inquiries, to have an inquiry inquiry. It certainly is a problem, and it's not for me to work out because I think it needs a good legal brain to work out what's fair and due process around that. After all, you are in the middle of an election. It's possible. You don't want to attract people attacking others just for the sake of putting an election off. You don't want to smirch the name of any, but on the other hand, it was such a serious issue, and it could only be dealt with afterwards, and I think there has to be a mechanism. If there was an Electoral Commission role, that would be the place for it to be considered. So it would be lodged with the Electoral Commission

[00:03:52] during the election process. OK. OK. Ms Celia Boyle. Thank you. Thank you very much for your submission and your concerns about transparency and democracy. You've mentioned the report of the local government electoral working group, and you would generally believe that having local elections run by the Electoral Commission would be more transparent, and it would be more transparent. Would be more transparent than by two private companies that we can't use the Official Informations Act on.

[00:04:37] That's correct. There's a number of reasons why we think it would be better, and that education element also goes to that. We tried, and also transparency, and we tried very hard to find what the elements, what people were being told at different sites, seems to be quite variable. There does not seem to be any consideration in many of them, that they're not actually talking about what you cannot do and what's ethically not able to be done or legally not able to be done. You know, trying to appraise our voters, for example, should be something that's 101 really and a candidate's briefing. That's the problem with a lot of new candidates being new on every local body election.

[00:05:22] You've got the potential for many more people to really not understand what the electoral process isn't about, and I think that was seen in a couple of other examples. A couple of other examples there. Thank you. All right. Hey, I've got to thank you for your submission. It was a fascinating submission, particularly going through all that analysis. So, really appreciate you bringing that to attention. Good. I do think the problem with special votes is also it's still not available on the Government of Internal Affairs website. So, that kind of lack of transparency about what's happening with special votes in those local elections is a real problem. It means that you're not able to do your job properly either. So, I think that's a significant issue around the elections and that's partly because you've got private providers doing that work.

[00:06:09] Okay. Thank you. Right. Now, we've got the Green Party at ByZoom. Mr. Summable, are you with us? He's on folks. Just going through it. Okay. Mr. Summable, if you're there, that'd be great. Good morning, Mr. Summable. Now, we've got 10 minutes with you. Right. Thank you very much. I'm Alan Summable. I'm co-convener of the Greens Local Government Caucus.

[00:06:55] Local Government Caucus represents 28 elected members around the country who were endorsed by the Green Party in the 2025 elections. I'm a regional councillor with the Otago Regional Council and I'm here in in Autoporty, Dunedin. I'll concentrate on a few parts of our written submission, the parts where I have the closest personal knowledge and experience. I guess our starting point is the belief that it should be straightforward for people to vote and that goes back into the principles. I can refer to the principles of the local electoral act in 2001 where it says all qualified persons have a reasonable and equal opportunity

[00:07:40] to cast an informed vote and it talks about public confidence and public understanding of local electoral processes. So I think my first point is that the postal voting system is well past its use by date and it effectively excludes many people from casting a vote. I'll relate some of my experience from Dunedin last year. In July last year we did a Green Party members did a round of door knocking in different parts of Dunedin with the intent of asking people are you enrolled and getting them enrolled to vote. One area we concentrated on was North Dunedin an area with a high transient population with many student

[00:08:26] tertiary students there. What we found was mail composting in letterboxes down the street including pile of orange electoral commission envelopes and naturally of course we directed people to the voting Z website to get them enrolled. We returned to the area in September and found in North Dunedin the majority of people had not received their voting papers that was either because they had moved flats from last year and not updated their address in spite of our best efforts or they were enrolled at their parents address in another town and so did not receive their voting papers. So the postal voting system it's certainly not the whole explanation for the low voter turnout

[00:09:12] but I think it is part of it. As a solution I'd go with the old GNZ proposals I think you've heard from them this morning and they've gone into that in great detail so please let's retire the postal voting system and have something better in place before 2028. Another important point for us is to say let's use STV for all elections in all councils be consistent and use the more democratic STV approach. Again using Otago for an example the Otago Regional Council and the Dunedin City Council use STV the other councils in the region use first past the post so it ended up

[00:09:58] with the situation that voters in Queenstown Lakes District Council Central Otago and Clutha had two voting systems to contend with in the same set of voting papers I see this evidence presented to you in their submission by the Greater Wellington Regional Council saying this is robust data across numerous Australian elections clear evidence of a higher level of informal votes where electives are asked to apply different voting systems in multi-member elections so let's not put those additional barriers for people let's use the one system and also propose to increase government resourcing to aid the participation of people with disabilities. One specific

[00:10:44] suggestion from us is to apply the Electoral Access Fund also to local body elections so going back to the local Electoral Act 2001 all qualified persons have a reasonable and equal opportunity to accept nomination as a candidate and that use of the Electoral Access Fund would assist with that you'll have seen that a part of our submission was on handling of role data there are some specific recommendations that's not my area of expertise but that's still useful stuff I'll be pleased to take any questions now Right well rather unsurprisingly we've got Celia Wade-Brown who wants to ask you a question Cura Alan

[00:11:30] thank you for presenting this I'm interested in the area of consistency between local and central government election processes and the postal versus in person and the commission running the education makes sense presumably you would want to see some alignment in the enrollment rule so at the moment as I understand it the local government you can enroll up to the day before until recently central government you could enroll on the day and now that's been rolled back should we settle on something that is common to both systems I think that consistency is very

[00:12:16] helpful and in that example that you have given our very very strong preference would be to be able to enroll right up until election day but yeah there are a number of places where that consistency can be brought in and one way of achieving that would be to have the electoral commission managing local body local council elections as well thank you can I ask you a question you said in respect to overseas voters they should get their voting papers earlier how many people actually vote living in London or New York or Singapore in a local election in the old New Zealand we have difficult again for a national election do you know what the numbers are

[00:13:02] look I'm afraid I do not know the answer to that I know specific question I don't have any other questions thank you for your time this morning it's a good submission thanks for the opportunity to present right now we're going to Joe Daly from the Christchurch City Council by Zoom are you there I presume it's oh good morning just yeah we're still going now we've got 10 minutes with you we have read your submission but the time is yours you know you know you know you know

[00:13:48] I'm Joe Daly I'm the Council's Governance Advisor and I'm also our Deputy Electoral Officer I do have an apology from our elected members who are currently sitting through annual plan hearings day two of you've obviously got the council's submission and I'll just reiterate a few key points from that including those related to your terms of reference and I'll also just introduce a couple of points from some work council's staff have done post-election in analyzing the voter turnout and from survey responses to some election-related questions council's consistently submitted in support of changes to legislation to allow for the modernization of voting

[00:14:34] increasing civic education and awareness nationally accessibility including to those overseas and improving central government support and funding for local elections like probably everyone you're hearing from we are concerned at the decline of voter participation and the ability to attract good candidates and these matters are in line with the national trends the Justice Committee in the time I've been working closely with elections has made many recommendations relating to these matters which disappointingly haven't been progressed. In particular the consistent theme from those recommendations is regarding modernization and alternative voting methods, review of postal

[00:15:20] challenges including alternatives for overseas and the review of voting systems and methods so we're really keen to see some of those recommendations and recommendations you yourselves may make actually progressed rather than staying in a report just regarding the voting process the key a question you asked regarding disallowed special voters we had 82% of those that returned special voting documents allowed and the majority of those disallowed were because they weren't able to be verified on the electoral role so that could have rolled into the next point regarding the electoral roles we didn't have any significant issues with them

[00:16:06] the majority of electors who enrolled or were on the unpublished role who enrolled past the close of the final role didn't receive clear or full information on what they needed to do to be able to vote so we see that that is really an opportunity for change particularly those unpublished role electors we've got over 3000 votes and only 26% of them received requested special voting documents because they received correspondence from the Electoral Commission but there's not much detail in there apart from contact the council if you want to make a vote

[00:16:52] there's an opportunity to put more information in there be strongly supportive of that just regarding the vote bins we had special voting in all our council facilities including our recreation centres we also had them at the airport at the university library and in supermarkets so they worked really well and neighbouring councils used similar and we were promoting that voting documents could be returned across those boundaries because we all used the same election service provider and we also had special voting and onsite voting available from 12 sites across the city so that was Monday to Friday and we also had

[00:17:38] sites open specifically for that on the weekend prior to election day and election day so there's quite a lot of effort made in making it as accessible as possible we didn't have any significant issues raised regarding integrity in Christchurch or not that I or our electoral officer were aware of but reports from candidates about online activity from other candidates in the public which I'm sure you're well aware of those kind of concerns we did receive a number of queries regarding the requirements entitlements or qualifications necessary for a person to stand as an elected member which was something very we have an external Electoral Officer now

[00:18:24] and that has worked well with our staff and with regard to the Electoral Commission as you'll see from the submission strongly support an increased role in them with good consultation on how that works myself and our communications team work really closely with our local Electoral Commission engagement team we attend events together we make sure there's always voting information in our sites and things like that and we have a partnership and a really good example of what perhaps could happen nationally and just a couple of comments on our participation that you would have seen we had reduced participation down to 38% we've got 16 geographical

[00:19:10] wards across the city and the turnout varied from 53% in banks a smaller community to 27% in Rickerton we source some information from the Electoral Commission to match with data provided by our service provider on those that voted the results weren't surprising only one in four electors from 18 to 24 voted over half the electors from 65 to 79 voted demographics covered 25% of electors with Maori descent voted compared with 39% without and many of the wards with that lower turnout were also wards with higher

[00:19:56] instances of socioeconomic deprivation and we had our life in Christchurch survey which is an annual survey we do, it went out to 40,000 people and we had nearly four and a half thousand surveys returned and these are people that have engaged with council or put themselves on this panel and the key statistics from that report on responses which we're still finalising is 90% of those people who responded indicated they voted 89% of those respondents indicated they found it very easy or easy to vote and the biggest identified reason for not voting was they didn't get around to it they couldn't find enough information about the candidates

[00:20:42] or they couldn't find a candidate that appealed final comments on May that they told us were what would make it easier to vote over half the respondents indicated being able to vote online as you'd imagine we had a lot of comments about postal being out of date unhelpful, not user friendly we have 42% of those respondents wanted more information about candidates and what they stand for and 28% of those candidates of those respondents indicated they were more active in the community during the election thank you for the opportunity questions at this time we've got one from Mr Dan Rosevon

[00:21:28] you mentioned here about voting documents being delivered by New Zealand Post I was wondering with New Zealand Post warning back a lot of the services across Church and Canterbury how that might affect the next election if we go continue with the status quo with our voting system we used a mixture of New Zealand Post and DX not aware of any significant issues with that delivery but obviously a reduction in services raises concerns about the viability of Post and just referring to the comments from the previous submitter regarding use of letterboxes these days and how many people

[00:22:14] actually do get posted mail can I can I just ask you obviously seem to run a pretty good operation what are your institutional arrangements some councils use third parties do you as yours basically in-house the promotion is that what I understood from or implied from your comments before we've got an external electoral officer and an external election service provider but promotion and sort of the general operations excluding the processing of votes and distribution of votes is done by council special by allocation all right thank you I've got Celia White Brown

[00:23:00] thank you Joe I do have a question about how presumably you support the Government New Zealand electoral working group thing that wants electoral commission to take on a bigger role how do you see that best if we were to go down that route at some stage how do we combine that national reach with a more local focus on who the candidates are where the meetings are that side of getting people to really think local about who they're voting for I'm just acknowledging I'm presenting a council submission so I may have some more detail views but in short in our submission we've referenced

[00:23:46] you know the need for consultation on how that's going to work and you know potentially that overarching national messaging and things like that with local promotion being led by the councils whether or not I would see that should be the partnership with the commission but sort of aspects I think would need council leadership Thank you Thank you very much for your contribution this morning and joining us from Christchurch Thank you for the opportunity Right now we've got Councillor Richard Hill from Auckland Council by Zoom I think we've seen him on there Mr. Hill's are you there Good morning we've read your submission

[00:24:32] so leave a little bit of questions please Thank you very much I'll sort of read a lot of what I'm going to say just to make sure I get it right but yes you've read our submission but largely obviously the 29% turnout for Auckland in my opinion disaster for democracy but it is very concerning for us We do believe there are range of factors that have led to that decline of participation but we do consider that postal voting is part of that I think it was around 38% of the people who said they didn't vote saying the reason was they filled it out

[00:25:18] they didn't take it back they forgot to and that's quite a big section of the community who just didn't pop it back so we think that postal voting and the issues there it also extended the timeframe this year because of the postal service situation we had to have an additional week or so because of the time the leaders go out and the leaders can come back in we consider that greater national leadership and coordination of the administration of local elections is required in particular the council reiterates its support for the electoral commission to take responsibility for delivering local elections given its experience administering parliamentary elections and the operational capability and independence required to manage the elections at scale Council would welcome strengthening the

[00:26:04] integrity framework surrounding elections current processes rely heavily on the unsuccessful candidates initiating court proceedings to challenge election results and council recommends that you could introduce automatic recounts where margins are small such we had some margins that never got to a recount as low as seven votes in that we acknowledge that voting method reform alone will not reverse declining participation a broader nationally coordinated approach is required to improve public understanding of local government encourage participation in local elections include strengthening civic education increasing visibility of local elections to a level comparable to parliamentary elections and consider structural reforms to enable greater greater alignment between

[00:00:00] We are in New Zealand and we can see you there, Danette. Welcome to the Select Committee. Look, you have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. Thank you. So, Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to speak. My name is Jeheta Danette Wedderter. I'm representing the Animal Justice Party Aotearoa, New Zealand. We are a political party committed to advocating for the rights, welfare and protection of animals. So we oppose the Environment Disestablishment Amendment Bill, but firstly, I will acknowledge the government's intent to make improvements across housing, infrastructure and the environment. They genuinely do have complex challenges and integrated thinking does have real value. So this proposal is just not

[00:00:47] an administration restructure. It is a decision about how environmental protection is prioritised within government and whether it remains a clear, independent voice. So removing that independence and placing those responsibilities within the broader ministry does create tension. Addressing housing and infrastructure challenges cannot come at the expense of environmental protection. So you're asking the same ministry to build the motorway and protect the wetlands that the motorway destroys. That's not coordination, that's a conflict of interest written into an org chart. These portfolios are not neutral. They are among the primary drivers of habitat destruction, freshwater and ecosystem decline. So the government has suggested that this change will improve coordination.

[00:01:34] However, coordination already occurs and can be strengthened with process. So disestablishing a dedicated ministry is not required to achieve that. As an experienced senior leader, a cannabis structural change does cost time and a lot of money and doesn't fix problems. I recommend identifying the root cause of the issues and providing solutions to address them. Now, importantly, environmental protection and animal welfare are inseparable. So animals rely on stable ecosystems for survival. That's their food, shelter, breeding and safety. And when these systems are weakened, it's animals who pay the price. We've already seen this. Aotearoa is facing ongoing biodiversity loss and environmental pressure. And globally, we know that ecosystem decline is one of the leading

[00:02:21] drivers of species decline. And there are broader implications. So New Zealand's reputation is tightly close to how our environment, how we care for our environment. And that reputation underpins trust in our clean green billion dollar image. Equally important is accountability. So a standalone ministry provides a clear line of responsibility when things go wrong. So 70% of freshwater fish are threatened with extinction or at risk. 78% of native lamb birds and 93% of native frogs and 94% of native reptiles are threatened or at risk. Nearly half of our monitored river sites fail to meet E. coli safe drinking water standards. And half of our lakes are in poor or degraded health due to nutrient contamination.

[00:03:11] The ministry has been responsible for bringing this kind of clear evidence-based reporting to light. And when that voice is absorbed, there is a real risk of this level of transparency and visibility being diminished over time. So we urge you to protect this proposal. And instead of broad structural change, the focus should be on targeted practical improvements that strengthen environmental outcomes. We have a responsibility to do better. Thank you. Thank you very much, Dineet, for your submission. Appreciate your time. It appears we have no questions, but we have got your written submission as well. It's very clear submission. Yeah, very clear, very passionate. So we appreciate your time this morning. Thank you.

Look, you've got five minutes with us. So in the next time, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. I began my written submission by explaining how important MFE's technical knowledge, independent voice and international standing were for me, a resource scientist with Tasman District Council, which is a small unitary authority, covering mainly rural land

[00:04:48] and the raft of complex contaminated historic sites. MFE scientists established the contaminated sites remediation fund for the cleanup of the Mapua fruit grows chemical site. They also provided nationwide technical advice across and access to high temperature incineration in France through the Basel Convention for the disposal of hazardous waste such as DDT, dealdron, PCBs, from the redundant agrochemical collection. And without this technical support from MFE, farmers and industries would not have supported the strive by councils to remove toxic waste for liability issues. The parameters for environmental reporting are complex.

[00:05:36] Ozone bioprotection, waste minimization, all require special technical knowledge which is provided by MFE staff. Now a question, would this still be available if MFE staff were buried inside a mega ministry? And I seriously doubt that for the following reasons. A well funded standalone ministry has a louder, stronger, more authoritative voice and it can take environmental concerns to the cabinet table. And this makes it possible to recruit and retain high quality staff and preserve the status of the ministry, build institutional knowledge and with the status and professional relationships have access to overseas experts for the emerging

[00:06:23] issues. Those unknowns which are arriving here soon. And this status and manner will be lost if the secretary for the environment functions are given to the CEO of the mega ministry. And then there's the climate work. The staff at MFE already have a battle to provide advice and policies to meet our climate responsibilities for emission reduction. When the climate commission's advice is not accepted and the ETS has been ordered in such a way it's no longer effective. We need to move to a low carbon economy and protect the carbon sinks and we need the ministry staff to guide us. Work on climate adaptation plan is very complex and politically

[00:07:11] challenging and with the process and with the actual process of merging the ministry that will take the staff's focus away from these projects and without a clear national directive. Local and central government staff are managing the recovery from climatic weather events in a very ad hoc manner. And as you know this is unsustainable. It's very expensive and it's inherently unfair. MFE is far too important for our future to merge and weaken its voice for nature and our physical and natural environment. We need its independent oversight to support, protect and improve our economy and well-being. And as a last note I hope that the NEB and

[00:08:01] planning bills are changed to include a goal for emission reduction targets, goals and plans under the Climate Change Response Act 2002.
