[00:46:12] the roles of mfv as seen in making the more invisible in a super ministry there are two big problems to address one of which that i would really emphasize is making as much as possible here reversible so when it's not seen as working or a different government comes in it's relatively smooth to fix the parts that aren't working and let the parts of the merger that maybe are working continue um one of the most important pieces of those for you to understand is the role of the chief executive and we've now seen a chief executive appointment and that person as has come out in the media just in the last day does not give me confidence that this is going to proceed as planned or was successfully um there's a little more to say about that so what i'll move

[00:47:00] to is the problem with ministries you've probably seen this before um i'll certainly leave it as an exhibit this is the unscramble and government diagram from the new zealand initiatives and you know how does science or reality negotiate this i would hand it to you that a lot of the problems we have can be fixed by restructuring but the restructuring might look more like this which is norway and in this model you might see things like climate environment and conservation put together rather than environment as we call it now um put in with um planning and building and transport uh i think there's some very different functions there and i support the pce's notion that these need to have a competition at the table within cabinet

[00:47:49] um the last thing that i worry about and i will encourage you to think about my looking around the table i think you can understand this without me explaining it further it's it's mentioned i know or is consistent with the new zealand initiatives unscramble and government report and it's one of the other concerns about restructuring like this that generate super ministries they generate accountability sinks which make government function worse rather than better so with that i think that's really what i wanted to cover and we're close to five minutes anyway yeah thank you troi look we do have a question from francesco cool thank you chair uh can you elaborate on what you mean by how this new ministry could

[00:48:37] create an accountability sink with a concrete example hypothetical example again wow um yeah so the the typical way these accountability sinks work is that um a decision is made and it's not clear who's responsible for the decision so let's say that that's has to do with the dipser loop of monitoring the quality of for example freshwater and then making decisions about land use that relate to that um if in fact we are losing track of who's supposed to be monitoring freshwater and considering what that actually means as well as who's responsible for the return to policy that's an accountability problem and it's already a problem because that dipser loop

[00:49:26] does not connect back until we implement the changes the pce recommended which i think you're still ongoing thank you very much troi for your submission today really appreciate that so thank you yes thank you um if you could the class in a diagram is that Norway one's interesting yeah and then the other one i think it's the ministry reports that we already have um we now welcome our next submitter that world forest and bird society of New Zealand welcome Richard Erica hello how are you you've got a great memory for you look we um you have five minutes with us so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission so thank you thank you thank you for the opportunity to appear again before

[00:50:15] my name is Erica tolman i'm acting CEO of forest and bird we are out here all is new out here all in new zealand's independent voice for nature and we have more than a century of experience advocating for biodiversity freshwater in the natural environment look our position is that we do not support this bill we recognize the value of better coordination across portfolios but disestablishing the ministry for the environment and embedding its statutory functions within a large development oriented super ministry creates risks that outweigh any potential administrative benefits so we have a couple of key concerns first off is dilution of environmental leadership so the bill transfers MEP statutory functions to a secretary within a consolidated ministry

[00:51:05] as you're all aware in practice we say that that risk that will risk diluting time focus and institutional weight for environmental stewardship another issue is the conflicting mandates and weaker advice so embedding environmental statutory functions alongside mandates to promote development transport efficiency and urban growth creates structural conflicts and we're particularly concerned with some of the drafting here so calls 12 of the bill amends section 32 of the environment act and it requires that the secretary considers those environmental matters listed as far as practical now I recognize that that wording is already in the ministry in the environment act sorry but the problem here

[00:51:51] is that it's only going to apply to the secretary not all the officials that sit under that person and also it is going to be weighed up in the ministry against all the other competing directions so in our view that that will mean environmental considerations are diluted reduce transparency and contestability you've already heard about that so I'll just skim over that and again the defunding risk you've heard already from the last two submitters about the potential for that I'll hand over to Richard Hi, Kia ora everybody, I'm Richard Cappy on the Chief Advisor for Forest and Bird it's nice to see you all again I think one of the things that we would flag for the committee is that there is some significant timing and implementation risks here that need to be taken into account this reform has been

[00:52:38] proposed as you will know from the mahi that you've been undertaking while the environmental system is already undergoing massive change we've got generational changes happening to the resource management system including two new bills and two and national directions those things are still being designed and will take a number of years to actually embed and secure furthermore we have significant mahi taking place where much of the rubber hits the road the regulatory environments in our local and regional government where we are looking at potentially some significant reforms in that space as well the risks posed by increasing this instability should not be understated there's that old adage that forms should follow functions we haven't actually determined what those functions fully are yet let alone tested them

[00:53:26] yet we're looking at reshaping and fundamentally shifting a lot of the expertise that exists in this space the institutional disruption that could take place at the very time that you probably need that stability in order to make RM reforms and regional and local government reform successful should not be understated you will risk losing special staff you'll risk losing capability and you'll risk losing institutional knowledge if you want RM3 to be successful and endure then you should think very carefully about whether doing this at this particular time is the right ideas I think it poses real risks to effective implementation of significant changes that this government has already signaled that it wishes to take

[00:54:13] forward so if the bill proceeds what should be the minimum safeguards I think we've already said there should be statutory clarity and as Erica and others have said it clearly established the role and authority of a dedicated secretary for the environment there should be strong obligations including explicit statutory duties on the Consolidation Ministry to give effect to environmental principles not optional or as far as practicable there should be reinfenced funding transparent dedicated funding lines for environmental functions and there should be visible contestable advice mechanisms to ensure that environmental advice remains independence in practice and clearly visible to ministers parliament and public

[00:54:58] finally I think at a practical level there are there are drafting improvements that would need to be made and would you know suggest that the work done by EDS and around this makes a good starting point in summary I think we're pretty clear that the committee should recommend against the bill in this current form I think that risk around being able to drive forward RM3 is really critical and one that I haven't seen picked up or given due consideration if you were to proceed I think the PCE is correct keep MFE out certainly at this point in time we would agree with Parliamentary Commissioner on that but we would have concerns about the two pills being split across the ministry so keeping part in with MFE and the risk of insert or insert or the could

[00:55:49] because we've already flagged quite deep concerns about actually the the problematic nature of having two pieces of legislation for RM3 in the first place the reality is and I guess I'm talking to the members of the coalition in a sense but if you're looking for efficiency and you're looking for affordability and you're looking for effectiveness there are better ways to achieve that you can strengthen environmental leadership rather than subsume it there are intimate interdepartmental boards and strength of coordination mechanisms that could be put in place thank you for your time happy to answer any questions thank you Richard and Erica for that very clear submission Rachel has a question yeah just if you can spend a tiny little bit more

[00:56:37] time on the relationship to what you're calling RM3 and that is the planning bill being separate from the natural environment bill and the integration of the resource management system and environment in the system and how things with this sure so we have serious concerns about the ability or actually inability to to do integrated management with the way that the two bills have been separated biodiversity is one issue that is very tricky to follow through the two bills and ensure that actually protections are going to be one effective for the environment and two actually efficient for resource users and that is we can't see a way to easily fix that we weren't

[00:57:31] able to suggest any any fixes in our submissions on those bills because of the in our view arbitrary split between land and environment they are the same thing and in our view should be managed as one the PC had suggested I think if I understand that submission correctly that those two bills could be managed by different administering agencies we would not agree with that for the for the reasons that I've just okay well thank you very much and Lan actually one quick question go for it I don't know if it's quick question I'm not sure if you caught hearings earlier this morning but we had

[00:58:17] Professor Bronwyn Hayward from University of Canterbury talking about she really struggled to find international examples where these kind of mergers have actually taken place and then had better outcomes for the environment are you aware with your kind of international colleagues and agencies around the world where I guess things like this might have had better momentum outcomes it's not something I'm aware of so I've worked in a number of different jurisdictions and I've looked at a number of different other jurisdictions and their approaches it's not something I've come across I don't think it's been flagged in any of reports by officials okay no thank you thank you very much thank you very much thanks so we will now move to our next

[00:59:05] submitter Zoran Rakovic who is joining us online and Zoom online yeah oh there we go we can see you Zoran welcome to this committee this afternoon we'll hand it over to you you have five minutes with us for your submission so over to you thank you madam chair members of the committee thank you for the chance to speak my name is Zoran Rakovic I'm a retired structural engineer from Selwyn and my submission is less of an experts view than more of a common it's more of a common sense than layperson's

[01:01:25] make sure there are still strong environmental experts in the system make sure decisions are transparent so people can see how trade-offs are made and make sure that if development is sped up the cost of things like drainage flood protection and water systems is properly accounted for not pushed onto existing communities later because they are the reality is very simple water still flows downhill pipes still have limits flood plains still flood changing the structure in Wellington doesn't change that if this reform helps things line up better great but if it leads to cutting corners selwyn will be the one paying for it thank you thank you very much Zoran for

[01:02:14] that submission we appear to have no further questions on that but we appreciate you taking the time to submit to us today so thank you very much appreciate your time thank you clear submission so thank you look we're now going to move to southern young labor there we are we can see you there welcome to the select committee this afternoon thank you madam chair here five minutes with us so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission thank you gotta go to and thank you for allowing me the opportunity to speak to our behalf of southern young labor my name is Liam Calder and I'm the vice president and on behalf of southern young labor we strongly

[01:03:02] oppose the environment or the disestablishment of the ministry of environment amendment bill as representatives of rung a tahi across the lower south island we speak for a generation that will live with the long-term consequences from the decisions that are made within the select committee room environmental governance is not abstract to us but it is the future that we will inherit this bill proposes to disestablish this disestablish the ministry of environment at a time when coordinated long-term environmental leadership is more critical than ever the ministry of environment plays a central role in providing independent advice coordinating across governments a coordinating across elections even and ensuring consistency and environmental policy

[01:21:50] environment and the environment hasn't got anyone who can say well actually i own the environment and i can give you my view you need a ministry or what if you're going to have this mega one you need a very clearly defined entity that can provide specific advice on the environment and if i ever just underline this point again if you can't even tell how much money you're spending on that in a in a blurred ministry i think you should be concerned thank you and francesco thank you chair um would you agree with the advice that the government has gotten around this um that given the proposed agency would be development oriented there's a particular risk of environment functions and perspectives being lost and if you agree with that how would you recommend

[01:22:36] them mitigate that inevitably there will be that that tension brought in house that tension exists i remind you right now around the cabinet table where ministers are discussing things what as i've just said what you're doing by pulling this all together is that you make the tensions in the trade-offs less transparent there isn't uh an agency which can give clear advice just from an environmental point of view we all accept they have to be trade-offs and we all accept that the development will involve trade-offs we need to know what they are it needs to come from an agency that can provide that uh clearly and transparently i think it's easiest done if you have a separate ministry for the environment thank you thank you simon look we'll go to grant now

[01:23:23] yeah you get a simon um you highlight the for example um well the new ministry uh the superminister understand rural new zealand okay if we end up this is where we if that's where we end up with the new ministry which is where we're heading how how what advice could you give to help prevent that happening any thoughts in that space i don't have advice for that because i think you make it very difficult for yourselves um you will have an entity which is heavily focused on the spatial planning side of things and the urban side now look that is important you've heard me be critical of two acts but you know one good thing about spatial planning is that we can bring stuff together the problem is that you have put that in an agency alongside

[01:25:00] skill around the natural environment in the way that an agency like mpi can bring to the table so i think but again if you're going to go down this road i think it's very important that you can see transparently what the appropriation is for environment and also for climate by the way so you know what's being spent and you can drill this this major domo who's going to be running at all the chief executive you can drill that person on okay how are you mobilizing the resources we're giving you for the environment if that's all lost in a single appropriation i think your job gets even harder as a select committee i might just jump in there Simon because you talked about departmental lego set i mean i suppose the point of this is to bring everything

[01:25:46] together in one so that there's less duplication it's more streamlined it's a bit more efficiency so i'm hearing from you that you're not necessarily opposed to that kind of merges into one to create more efficiency but just not with transport housing and environment that's not where you think it should so is that is that am i correct in saying that absolutely as i said right in the beginning there's no right to way to arrange government departments but i mean you could ask the question why isn't lunes part of this merger i mean this land information new zealand spatial planning information this is all logically connected i think it's the wrong way to think about it how could we arrange the lego i'd look at form and function you know what are you trying to achieve

[01:26:32] what the government's currently trying to achieve through spatial planning is integrating some very important functions and i think that this ministry could do that well but it's also trying to achieve something environmental which is a bit different and so keeping those two apart i think would be a better plan okay thank you so one last question from pre anchor just super quick since you've suggested alternative saman we had a submitter who suggested that mfv climate change and doc could go together what are your thoughts on that i don't agree with that doc is an operational department you know it's a big big big land manager and it needs a lot of of of real technical advice and land management advice now they're

meetings.transcriptText:
[00:00:00] Yes. But anyway, congratulations for that. Now, we've got a bit of time with you this morning. Looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. As a committee, as you know, we love asking questions. I have become accustomed to that reality, but you come to me just to make some, for this occasion, very short remarks, just to try and frame the conversation and shape some of the questions. So thanks, firstly, for inviting us to talk to you about the long-term insight briefing. And I think you also wanted to maybe spend a short amount of time on the Mendeima's order. Yes. We've got one member particularly interested.

[00:00:46] Everyone should be interested if you need to talk about the legal situation. So we asked you a bit. Awesome. So I'll cover that in my opening remarks just in terms of framing it, because I am able to talk about it to a level and then there are some limitations. But can I just make sure I've introduced my colleagues listening at the table? You are familiar, I think, with Dr Anita Ryan, Deputy Chief Executive of my community's partnerships and pathways, and Tom Baker, who's a Chief Digital Officer. So they're both here. I've seen the Chief Digital Officer's role here is not needing to be explained. And Anita obviously brings lots of insight around the regulatory and integrative services, where we think it's a good opportunity. And also at the moment, it's covering the community partnerships and pathways space, where also technology has a quite

[00:01:35] significant role to play.
